topics.title: Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Recurrence Relation?

A **recurrence relation** is a mathematical equation that defines a sequence of values (in our context, the running time $T(n)$ of an algorithm) **in terms of its own previous values**. For divide-and-conquer algorithms, the recurrence expresses the cost of solving a subproblem of size $n$ in terms of the cost of solving smaller subproblems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"A recurrence is a function defined in terms of (a) one or more base cases and (b) one or more recursive cases, where each recursive case reduces the argument toward a base case. Asymptotic analysis of recursive algorithms reduces to solving recurrences."*

The **standard divide-and-conquer recurrence form** that we will solve throughout this module is:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where:
- $a \geq 1$ is the number of subproblems created at each recursive level,
- $b > 1$ is the factor by which the subproblem size shrinks,
- $f(n)$ is the cost of *dividing* the problem and *combining* the results outside the recursive calls.

## 1.2 The Four Methods of Solving Recurrences

| # | Method | Core Idea |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Iteration Method** | Repeatedly substitute the recurrence into itself, look for a pattern, then sum the resulting series. |
| 2 | **Recursion Tree Method** | Draw a tree of recursive calls, sum the work per level, then sum across all levels. |
| 3 | **Substitution Method** | Guess (educated!) the asymptotic form of the solution and prove it rigorously by mathematical induction. |
| 4 | **Master's Theorem** | A direct cookbook for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — three cases. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Boss & Employees" Picture**
> Imagine you are a **boss** with a task of size $n$. You split the task into $a$ pieces of size $n/b$ and hand each piece to an **employee**. The work you do yourself (writing the email, pasting results) is $f(n)$, and the work your employees recursively do is $a \cdot T(n/b)$. The four methods are just four different ways of **adding up everyone's effort** to get the total cost.

## 1.3 Why Asymptotic Analysis Matters Here

The *exact* closed-form solution of a recurrence is rarely needed. We only need the **asymptotic growth** — Big-O, Big-Theta, Big-Omega — to compare algorithms. Therefore, our four methods only need to produce a tight asymptotic bound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Used in This Module:** We write $T(n)$ to mean the worst-case running time on input of size $n$, and we use the standard base case $T(1) = T(n=1) = \Theta(1)$ unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Iteration Method (Unrolling Method)

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Expand** the recurrence by substituting $T(n)$ into the right-hand side.
2. **Identify the pattern** in the unrolled expression.
3. **Stop** when the argument of $T$ becomes a constant (the base case).
4. **Sum** the resulting arithmetic / geometric / polynomial series.
5. **Simplify** using known summation formulas.

### Common Summation Identities (MUST MEMORISE)

| Series | Closed Form | Asymptotic Form |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} x^i$ | $\dfrac{x^k - 1}{x - 1}$ | $\Theta(x^k)$ if $x > 1$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i$ | $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n - 1} \left(\dfrac{a}{b^i}\right)^k$ | geometric on level index | See derivation below |

## 2.2 The Recursion Tree Method

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Draw** a rooted tree where each node holds the **extra work** $f(m)$ for the subproblem of size $m$ at that node.
2. The number of subproblems and the size at the next level follow the **branching factor** $a$ and **shrink factor** $b$.
3. **Compute work per level** by summing all node costs at that depth.
4. **Sum across all levels** from the root (level 0) to the leaves (level $\log_b n$).
5. **Add the work at the leaves** to close the recurrence.

## 2.3 The Substitution Method

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Guess** the form of the solution, e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.
2. **Substitute** this guess back into the recurrence.
3. **Prove** by **strong mathematical induction** on $n$ that the guess is correct for some constants $c, n_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trick — "Making the constants work":** When proving $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$, you assume it for arguments smaller than $n$, and you may have to choose $c$ small enough (or $n_0$ large enough) so that the boundary term dominates.

## 2.4 The Master's Theorem

For the recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1, b > 1$, and $f$ asymptotically positive:

### The Three Cases (Verbatim — High-Yield!)

| Case | Condition | Conclusion | Intuition |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O\!\left(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\right)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$ | Recursion dominates (top of tree heaviest) |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n\right)$ for $k \geq 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\right)$ | Work is **balanced** at every level |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\right)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ **AND** the **regularity condition** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ and large $n$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Root dominates (bottom of tree heaviest) |

### KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Master Theorem Boundary Values

| $(a, b)$ | $\log_b a$ (critical exponent) | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + n$ | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + 1$ |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| $(2, 2)$ | $1$ | $O(n)$ — Case 1 | $O(n)$ — Case 2 |
| $(4, 2)$ | $2$ | $O(n^2)$ — Case 2 | $O(n^2)$ — Case 1 |
| $(3, 2)$ | $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585$ | $O(n^{1.585})$ — Case 1 | $O(n^{1.585})$ — Case 2 |
| $(2, 4)$ | $0.5$ | $O(n)$ — Case 3 | $O(n^{0.5})$ — Case 1 |
| $(1, 2)$ | $0$ | depends on $f(n)$ | $O(1)$ — Case 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regularity Condition for Case 3 — Why it matters:** Without it, the Master Theorem **cannot be applied** to Case 3. The condition ensures that the work done at the leaves does not overwhelm the root work. A common error in KTU exams is to apply Case 3 without verifying this condition.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Merge Sort / Karatsuba Multiplication / Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** all fit Master Case 1 or 2.
- **Towers of Hanoi / Naive Recursive Fibonacci** are governed by recurrences that do *not* fit Master's form (because the subproblem size does not shrink uniformly), so the Iteration / Recursion Tree method is preferred.
- **Compiler optimization**: The Master Theorem guides the choice of *threshold size* at which a recursive routine should switch to an iterative one.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Iteration Method — Full Derivation

### Example A: $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy:** Unroll until the pattern is visible, then sum a geometric series.

**Step 1 — Substitute once:**

$$T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T(n/4) = 3T(n/16) + n/4$:**

$$T(n) = 3\left[3T(n/16) + \dfrac{n}{4}\right] + n = 9T(n/16) + \dfrac{3n}{4} + n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute a third time (let $T(n/16) = 3T(n/64) + n/16$):**

$$T(n) = 9\left[3T(n/64) + \dfrac{n}{16}\right] + \dfrac{3n}{4} + n = 27T(n/64) + \dfrac{9n}{16} + \dfrac{3n}{4} + n$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pattern after $i$ substitutions:**

$$T(n) = 3^i \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{4^i}\right) + n \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^j$$

> **Reasoning for the pattern:** Each unrolling step multiplies the recursive term by 3 (branching factor $a=3$), divides the argument by 4 (shrink factor $b=4$), and adds $3^{j-1} \cdot n / 4^{j-1}$ to the running total.

**Step 5 — Stop when $n/4^i = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_4 n$:**

$$T(n) = 3^{\log_4 n} \cdot T(1) + n \sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} \left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^j$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the geometric series.** Since $3/4 < 1$, the sum converges to a constant:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} \left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^j = \dfrac{1 - (3/4)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - 3/4} \leq \dfrac{1}{1 - 3/4} = 4 = O(1)$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the first term:**

$$3^{\log_4 n} = n^{\log_4 3} \quad \text{(using the identity } a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a} \text{)}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**

$$T(n) = n^{\log_4 3} \cdot \Theta(1) + n \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n)$$

> **Verification using Master Theorem (Case 1):** $a=3, b=4, \log_4 3 \approx 0.792$, and $f(n) = n = \Omega(n^{0.792 + 0.2})$, so Case 1 applies ✓.

### Example B: $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/2) + n$ → rewritten as $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

**Step 1 — Unroll twice to see the pattern:**

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$$
$$= 2\left[2T(n/4) + n/2\right] + n = 4T(n/4) + n + n$$
$$= 4\left[2T(n/8) + n/4\right] + 2n = 8T(n/8) + n + 2n$$

**Step 2 — Pattern after $i$ unrollings:**

$$T(n) = 2^i \, T(n/2^i) + i \cdot n$$

**Step 3 — Stop at $i = \log_2 n$:**

$$T(n) = n \cdot T(1) + n \log_2 n = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> **This is the recurrence for MERGE SORT.** ✓

## 3.2 Recursion Tree Method — Full Derivation

### Example C: $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is interesting:** This recurrence **cannot be solved by the Master Theorem** because the subproblem sizes are *asymmetric* ($n/3$ vs $2n/3$). This is the canonical KTU example for showing the power of the Recursion Tree.

**Step 1 — Draw the tree and label each level:**

```
Level 0:                              cn
                                      /    \
Level 1:                       cn/3         2cn/3
                              /   \         /    \
Level 2:                cn/9    2cn/9   2cn/9    4cn/9
                        ...      ...    ...       ...
Level k:        (cn/3^k) ... (2^k · cn/3^k)
```

**Step 2 — Compute work at level $k$:**

$$\text{Work at level } k = cn\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^k + cn \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^k = cn \left[\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^k + \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^k\right]$$

The dominant term is $(2/3)^k$, so work at level $k$ is:

$$\Theta\!\left(cn \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^k\right)$$

**Step 3 — Find the depth of the tree.** Because the longest path follows the $2n/3$ branch, the depth is:

$$h = \log_{3/2} n = \dfrac{\log n}{\log(3/2)} = \Theta(\log n)$$

**Step 4 — Sum work across all levels:**

$$T(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{h} cn \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^k = cn \cdot \dfrac{1 - (2/3)^{h+1}}{1 - 2/3} \leq 3cn = O(n)$$

The leaves contribute a *geometrically decreasing* amount, and the total is bounded by the root. The leaves are at the level where the largest subproblem has size 1, contributing a total of $\Theta(n)$ (each leaf does $\Theta(1)$ work, and the number of leaves is bounded by a constant times $n$).

> **Final answer: $T(n) = O(n)$.**

### Example D: $T(n) = T(n/4) + T(n/2) + n^2$

**Level work:**
- Level 0: $n^2$
- Level 1: $(n/4)^2 + (n/2)^2 = n^2/16 + n^2/4 = (5/16) n^2$
- Level 2: shrinks further; total $\sum (5/16)^k n^2 = O(n^2)$.

> **Result: $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$** — the root dominates (Master-like Case 3).

## 3.3 Substitution Method — Full Derivation

### Example E: Prove $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ solves to $T(n) = O(n \log n)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy:** Guess $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n$ for some $c > 0$ and $n \geq n_0$. Prove by strong induction.

**Step 1 — State the inductive hypothesis.** Assume for all $m < n$:

$$T(m) \leq c \cdot m \log_2 m$$

**Step 2 — Base case.** Choose $n_0 = 2$. There exists a $c$ large enough that $T(2) \leq 2c$.

**Step 3 — Inductive step.** Substitute into the recurrence:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \leq 2 \cdot c \cdot \dfrac{n}{2} \log_2 \dfrac{n}{2} + n$$

$$= c \cdot n \left(\log_2 n - 1\right) + n$$

$$= c \cdot n \log_2 n - c \cdot n + n$$

$$= c \cdot n \log_2 n - (c - 1) \cdot n$$

**Step 4 — Bound.** If $c \geq 1$, then $-(c-1)n \leq 0$, and we get:

$$T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

### Example F (Substitution — Bounds don't always work first try)

Suppose we *incorrectly* guessed $T(n) = O(n)$ for the same recurrence. Then:

$$T(n) \leq 2 \cdot c \cdot \dfrac{n}{2} + n = cn + n = (c+1)n$$

> **Problem:** This is $O(n)$, but we have introduced an extra $+n$ term that has nowhere to go. We must subtract it from the right side, which forces us to use a tighter bound like $c \cdot n - d$ and propagate the constant. When substitution "fails", the boundary term $f(n)$ is too large to be absorbed by the guess.

## 3.4 Master Theorem — Full Application Walkthroughs

### Example G: $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$

- $a = 9, b = 3 \Rightarrow n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_3 9} = n^2$.
- $f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1$.
- **Case 1 applies**: $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.

### Example H: $T(n) = T(2n/5) + T(3n/5) + n$

> This is **not** in Master form (subproblem sizes differ). Recursion-tree method only.

### Example I: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ (the "gap" case)

- $a=2, b=2 \Rightarrow n^{\log_b a} = n$.
- $f(n) = n \log n = n \cdot \log n$.
- Compare $n \log n$ vs $n$: $n \log n = \Omega(n^{1 + \epsilon})$ for any $\epsilon < \log n / n$ asymptotically. ✓ Case 3 condition.
- Regularity: $a \cdot f(n/2) = 2 \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2) = n \log(n/2) \leq c \cdot n \log n$ for $c = 1$. ✓
- **Case 3 applies**: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

### Example J: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log^k n$ for $k \geq 0$ — the **master regularity** counterexample

- $n^{\log_b a} = n$.
- $f(n) = n \log^k n$.
- This fits **Case 2** with $k$: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log^{k+1} n)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — When to Use Which Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Recurrence T of n given] --> B{Is it in Master form T of n equals aT of n slash b plus f of n?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is a subproblem size uniform n slash b?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Master Theorem Cases 1, 2, or 3]
    C -- No --> E[Use Recursion Tree or Iteration]
    B -- No --> F{Is the recurrence polynomial inside log or simple arithmetic series?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Iteration Method]
    F -- No --> H{Do you have an educated guess of the bound?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Substitution Method with induction]
    H -- No --> E
    E --> J[Verify with Master if applicable]
    D --> K[Final Asymptotic Bound]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Master Theorem — Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: a, b, and f of n] --> B[Compute critical exponent n to the power log base b of a]
    B --> C{Compare f of n to n to the power log base b of a}
    C -- f of n is polynomially smaller --> D[Case 1: T of n equals Theta n to the power log base b of a]
    C -- f of n is Theta of n to the power log base b of a times log to the k n --> E[Case 2: T of n equals Theta n to the power log base b of a times log to the power k plus 1 of n]
    C -- f of n is polynomially larger --> F[Check regularity condition a f of n slash b less than or equal to c f of n]
    F -- Holds --> G[Case 3: T of n equals Theta of f of n]
    F -- Fails --> H[Master Theorem does not apply, use Recursion Tree or Akra-Bazzi]
```

## 4.3 Recursion Tree Architecture for T of n equals T of n slash 3 plus T of 2n slash 3 plus c n

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Level 0: cn"] --> L1L["Level 1 Left: cn slash 3"]
    L0 --> L1R["Level 1 Right: 2cn slash 3"]
    L1L --> L2A["Level 2: cn slash 9"]
    L1L --> L2B["Level 2: 2cn slash 9"]
    L1R --> L2C["Level 2: 2cn slash 9"]
    L1R --> L2D["Level 2: 4cn slash 9"]
    L2A --> L3x["Leaves at depth h where 2 to the k cn slash 3 to the k shrinks to 1"]
    L2B --> L3x
    L2C --> L3x
    L2D --> L3x
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each level shrinks work by a factor of $2/3$ on the dominant side, so the geometric series sums to $O(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Master Theorem and identify the three cases.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Master Theorem gives a direct solution to recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive. It compares $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$:
>
> - **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O\!\left(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\right)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$.
> - **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n\right)$ for $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\right)$.
> - **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\right)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ and the regularity condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Substitution Method and the Recursion Tree Method for solving recurrences.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Substitution Method | Recursion Tree Method |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Approach** | Guess the bound, then verify using mathematical induction | Physically draw the recursion tree and sum work at each level |
> | **Rigor** | Fully rigorous if induction succeeds | Heuristic — produces a *guess* which is then verified by substitution |
> | **Use Case** | Best when the bound is already known or guessable | Best for recurrences not in Master form (asymmetric splits) |
> | **Limitation** | Requires a clever initial guess; boundary terms can be tricky | Care needed when leaf cost is non-trivial (e.g., unbalanced trees) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE):** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with an internal choice between sub-questions (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Total marks for this module question = 14.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Solve the following recurrences using (a) the Iteration Method and (b) the Master's Theorem. Comment on the consistency of the answers.**

> **(a)** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$  (7 Marks)
> **(b)** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^3$  (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$

**By Iteration Method:**

Unrolling:

$$T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$$
$$= 4[4T(n/4) + n^2/4] + n^2 = 16T(n/4) + 2n^2$$
$$= 16[4T(n/8) + n^2/16] + 2n^2 = 64T(n/8) + 3n^2$$

Pattern after $i$ unrollings:

$$T(n) = 4^i T(n/2^i) + i \cdot n^2$$

Stop at $i = \log_2 n$:

$$T(n) = n^2 T(1) + n^2 \log_2 n = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$$

**By Master's Theorem:**

$a = 4, b = 2 \Rightarrow n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.

Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^2$: $f(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^0 n)$, so **Case 2** applies with $k = 0$:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$$

> **Consistency check:** Both methods give the same asymptotic bound. ✓ (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b) — $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^3$

**By Iteration Method:**

Unroll:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^3$$
$$= 2[2T(n/4) + n^3/8] + n^3 = 4T(n/4) + n^3(1/4 + 1)$$
$$= 4[2T(n/8) + n^3/64] + 5n^3/4 = 8T(n/8) + n^3(1/16 + 1/4 + 1)$$

Pattern after $i$ unrollings:

$$T(n) = 2^i T(n/2^i) + n^3 \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \frac{1}{8^j}$$

Stop at $i = \log_2 n$:

$$T(n) = n T(1) + n^3 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/8)^{\log_2 n}}{1 - 1/8} \leq n + \frac{8}{7} n^3 = \Theta(n^3)$$

**By Master's Theorem:**

$a = 2, b = 2 \Rightarrow n^{\log_2 2} = n$.

$f(n) = n^3 = \Omega(n^{1+\epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1$.

Regularity: $a \cdot f(n/b) = 2(n/2)^3 = n^3/4 \leq c \cdot n^3$ for $c = 1/4 < 1$. ✓

**Case 3** applies:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$$

> **Consistency check:** Both methods yield $\Theta(n^3)$. ✓ (7 marks)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **(a)** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using the Master Theorem. Justify which case applies and verify the regularity condition.  (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Solve $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$ using the **Recursion Tree Method**, showing all level sums. Comment on whether the Master Theorem is applicable.  (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$

**Step 1:** Compute critical exponent.

$$a = 2,\ b = 2 \ \Rightarrow\ n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n$$

**Step 2:** Compare $f(n) = n \log n$ with $n$.

For any $\epsilon < \log n / n$ (asymptotically positive), $f(n) = n \log n = \Omega(n^{1+\epsilon})$ is polynomially larger than $n$.

**Step 3:** Verify regularity condition for Case 3.

$$a \cdot f(n/b) = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n}{2} \log\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) = n \log n - n$$

We require $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$:

$$n \log n - n \leq c \cdot n \log n$$

For $c = 1$ and $n$ large enough that $n \leq n \log n / 2$, this holds. So regularity is satisfied with $c = 1$ (we can use $c = 1$ since $-n \leq 0$ trivially gives $\leq c \cdot n \log n$ for $c \geq 1$). ✓

**Step 4:** Apply Case 3:

$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b) — $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$ via Recursion Tree

**Step 1 — Level 0 (root):** $cn$

**Step 2 — Level 1:** Work = $c(n/3) + c(2n/3) = cn$

**Step 3 — Level 2:** Two sub-branches of size $n/3$ and $2n/3$ each split further:
- From $n/3$: $c(n/9) + c(2n/9) = cn/3$
- From $2n/3$: $c(2n/9) + c(4n/9) = 2cn/3$
- Total = $cn$

**Pattern:** Work at every level $= cn$ (because the recursive work always sums to the size of the parent, just with the constant $c$).

**Step 4 — Depth of tree:** Longest path follows the $2n/3$ branch:

$$h = \log_{3/2} n = \frac{\log n}{\log(3/2)} = \Theta(\log n)$$

**Step 5 — Sum across levels:**

$$T(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{h-1} cn + \text{(leaf work)} = c \cdot n \cdot h + \text{(leaf work)}$$

$$= cn \cdot \Theta(\log n) + \text{(leaf work)}$$

**Step 6 — Leaf work:** Each leaf is $\Theta(1)$. The number of leaves is at most $n$ (constant-bounded). So leaf work is $\Theta(n)$.

**Step 7 — Final bound:**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> **Master Theorem Applicability:** The recurrence is **NOT** in the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ because the subproblem sizes are *asymmetric* ($n/3$ and $2n/3$). Hence **Master's Theorem cannot be applied** here. This demonstrates the necessity of the Recursion Tree / Iteration method. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the regularity condition** in Case 3 — *partial credit lost* (2–3 marks off).
> 2. **Confusing $n^{\log_b a}$ with $\log_b(n^a)$** — these are very different; $4^{\log_2 n} = n^2$, not $\log_2(n^4)$.
> 3. **Mismatching the constants $a$ and $b$** — for $T(n) = 5T(n/2) + n^2$, students often write $b = 5$ (incorrect; $b$ is the divisor of the argument).
> 4. **Trying to apply Master Theorem to $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$** — this is a guaranteed **0 on that sub-question** if attempted. The Master Theorem does not apply to non-uniform splits.
> 5. **Forgetting base cases in Substitution Method induction** — always state and prove the base case explicitly.
> 6. **Summing the recursion tree incorrectly** — when work per level grows (e.g., Case 3), the leaves dominate, and the total is $\Theta(f(n))$, NOT the root work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Master Theorem form** is strictly $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1, b > 1$.
- [ ] **Always compute $n^{\log_b a}$** first — it is the *critical exponent* that determines the case.
- [ ] **Case 1** — recursion dominates, answer is $n^{\log_b a}$.
- [ ] **Case 2** — work is balanced, multiply by one extra $\log n$ (or $\log^{k+1} n$).
- [ ] **Case 3** — root dominates, **and** regularity condition must hold.
- [ ] **Iteration Method** = unroll, pattern, sum a series.
- [ ] **Recursion Tree Method** = draw the tree, sum per level, sum across levels.
- [ ] **Substitution Method** = guess + prove by induction; boundary terms must be absorbed by the constant.
- [ ] **Asymmetric recurrences** (e.g., $T(n/3) + T(2n/3)$) **cannot** use Master Theorem.
- [ ] **Common critical exponents to memorise:** $\log_2 2 = 1, \log_2 4 = 2, \log_2 8 = 3, \log_4 3 \approx 0.79, \log_3 2 \approx 0.63$.
- [ ] **Geometric series rule:** if $r < 1$, the sum $1 + r + r^2 + \dots$ is bounded by $1/(1-r)$ — a *constant*.
- [ ] **Standard recurrences to recognise instantly:**
  - $2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$ (Merge Sort)
  - $7T(n/2) + n^2 \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ (Strassen)
  - $T(n/2) + 1 \Rightarrow \Theta(\log n)$ (Binary Search)
  - $2T(n/2) + 1 \Rightarrow \Theta(n)$ (Tree traversal)
- [ ] **Always state the base case** explicitly when solving or proving recurrences.
- [ ] **Final answers must be in asymptotic notation** — $\Theta$, $O$, or $\Omega$ — never an exact closed form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (AVL Tree — Adelson-Velsky and Landis, 1962)**
> An **AVL Tree** is a self-balancing **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in which the **difference of heights of the left subtree and right subtree of any node (called the Balance Factor, BF)** is at most **1**. If at any time they differ by more than 1, **rebalancing (rotations)** is performed to restore the AVL property.

Mathematically, for every node $v$ in the AVL tree:

$$BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$$

where $h_L(v)$ and $h_R(v)$ are the heights of the left and right subtrees of node $v$ respectively. The **AVL Invariant** is:

$$BF(v) \in \{-1, 0, +1\} \quad \forall \, v \in \text{AVL Tree}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCRT602 — Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to *insert* and *delete* elements into AVL trees showing **all four rotation cases (LL, RR, LR, RL)** with neat diagrams. Marks are awarded for correct *height tracking*, *balance factor computation*, and *choice of rotation*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Balanced Scale

Imagine a **kitchen balance scale** sitting on a table. On either pan you can place weights. If you pile too many weights on the left side, the scale tilts dangerously. To keep the scale useful, you must redistribute the weights so neither pan is significantly heavier than the other.

- Each **weight** is a BST node (carrying a key).
- The **scale** is the AVL tree — the structure that holds the weights.
- The **imbalance** is the moment when $|BF(v)| > 1$.
- The **rebalancing action** (shifting weights) is the **rotation**.

A BST without balance is like a long chain — searching takes $O(n)$ time. An AVL tree, however, is like a perfectly **balanced scale** — search, insert, and delete all take $O(\log n)$ time, because the height is bounded by $\approx 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$.

## 1.3 Balance Factor — The Heart of AVL

The **Balance Factor (BF)** is a single integer that tells the *direction* and *magnitude* of imbalance:

| BF Value | Meaning of $h_L - h_R$ | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **+1** | Left subtree is **higher by 1** | Balanced |
| **0**  | Both subtrees are **equal in height** | Perfectly Balanced |
| **-1** | Right subtree is **higher by 1** | Balanced |
| **+2 or greater** | Left Heavy | **Unbalanced — rotation needed** |
| **-2 or less** | Right Heavy | **Unbalanced — rotation needed** |

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention (Important for KTU):** Always subtract in the order **(Left Height − Right Height)**. A **positive BF** means left-heavy, and a **negative BF** means right-heavy. Many students lose marks by using the opposite convention.

## 1.4 Why AVL Trees Matter

AVL trees guarantee that the height $h$ of a tree with $n$ nodes satisfies:

$$h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$$

This makes the worst-case time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations equal to $O(\log n)$. Real-world systems that rely on AVL trees include **databases (memory-resident indices)**, **in-memory routing tables**, and **compiler symbol tables**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Imbalance Cases (KTU Hot Topic)

When an insertion or deletion makes a node's balance factor $\pm 2$, the imbalance is classified based on the **path of insertion** relative to the unbalanced node. There are exactly **four canonical cases**:

| Case | Imbalance Type | Path of Insertion | Rotation Required | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **LL (Left-Left)** | Inserted into **Left subtree of Left child** | **Single Right Rotation** | *Go Left, then Left → Rotate Right* |
| **2** | **RR (Right-Right)** | Inserted into **Right subtree of Right child** | **Single Left Rotation** | *Go Right, then Right → Rotate Left* |
| **3** | **LR (Left-Right)** | Inserted into **Right subtree of Left child** | **Left Rotation on child + Right Rotation on node** | *Go Left, then Right → Double Rotate* |
| **4** | **RL (Right-Left)** | Inserted into **Left subtree of Right child** | **Right Rotation on child + Left Rotation on node** | *Go Right, then Left → Double Rotate* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (Examiner's favourite):** Stand at the **first unbalanced ancestor (A)** from the inserted node. Read the **two steps** from A: first to its tall child (C), then to the grandchild (G) causing the imbalance. The pair of directions gives the case name (e.g., Left then Right = LR).

## 2.2 Height Bounds — The Mathematical Guarantee

### 2.2.1 Minimum Number of Nodes $N(h)$ for an AVL Tree of Height $h$

Let $N(h)$ be the **minimum number of nodes** in an AVL tree of height $h$. The recurrence is:

$$N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$$

with base cases $N(0) = 0$ (empty tree) and $N(1) = 1$ (single node). The **closed-form (Fibonacci-like) solution** is:

$$N(h) \approx \phi^{h} \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$$

### 2.2.2 Maximum Height $h$ for $n$ Nodes

Taking logarithms of $N(h) = \phi^h$:

$$h \leq \log_{\phi}(n+1) \approx 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **golden ratio bound**. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Prove that the height of an AVL tree with $n$ nodes is $O(\log n)$."* Use the recurrence above.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Balance Factor | $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$ | Detect imbalance at a node |
| 2 | AVL Invariant | $BF(v) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ | Define validity of tree |
| 3 | Min nodes recurrence | $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$ | Lower-bound proof of height |
| 4 | Max height | $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ | Complexity analysis |
| 5 | Search time | $T_{\text{search}} = O(\log n)$ | BST search bounded by height |
| 6 | Insert time | $T_{\text{insert}} = O(\log n)$ | One path + at most 2 rotations |
| 7 | Delete time | $T_{\text{delete}} = O(\log n)$ | One path + at most $O(\log n)$ rotations |
| 8 | Rotation count (insert) | At most **1 rotation** (single or double) | Re-balance after insertion |
| 9 | Rotation count (delete) | At most $O(\log n)$ rotations | Rebalancing may propagate |
| 10 | Golden ratio | $\phi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$ | Solving recurrence |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database indexing:** AVL trees are used in **SQLite** and **Berkeley DB** for in-memory B-tree alternatives when disk I/O is not the bottleneck.
- **Compiler symbol tables:** AVL trees provide $O(\log n)$ lookup of identifiers during lexical analysis.
- **Network routing:** Longest-prefix matching in routers often uses balanced BST variants.
- **Computational geometry:** AVL trees back up range trees and segment trees.
- **In-memory priority scheduling:** Although heaps are common, AVL trees allow $O(\log n)$ *delete-any-key* — useful in OS process schedulers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Rotations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Rotation Primitives (The Building Blocks)

### 3.1.1 Right Rotation (LL Case Fix)

A right rotation is performed on a node $P$ whose left child is $L$. The pivot is $L$.

**Before Rotation:**
```
        P                  L
       / \                / \
      L   R      →       A   P
     / \                   / \
    A   B                 B   R
```

**Pseudocode (descriptive, language-agnostic):**
1. Let `L = P.left`.
2. Let `B = L.right`.
3. Set `P.left = B` (B becomes left child of P).
4. Set `L.right = P` (P becomes right child of L).
5. Update heights: first `P`, then `L`.
6. Return `L` as the new root of this subtree.

### 3.1.2 Left Rotation (RR Case Fix)

A left rotation is performed on a node $P$ whose right child is $R$.

**Before Rotation:**
```
        P                  R
       / \                / \
      L   R      →       P   C
         / \            / \
        B   C          L   B
```

**Steps:**
1. Let `R = P.right`.
2. Let `B = R.left`.
3. Set `P.right = B`.
4. Set `R.left = P`.
5. Update heights: first `P`, then `R`.
6. Return `R` as the new root.

### 3.1.3 Left-Right Rotation (LR Case Fix)

For LR imbalance, we **first left-rotate the left child**, then **right-rotate the parent**.

```
        P               P                G
       / \             / \              / \
      L   R    →      G   R    →       L   P
     / \             / \              / \ / \
    A   G           L   C            A  B C   R
       / \         / \
      B   C       A   B
```

### 3.1.4 Right-Left Rotation (RL Case Fix)

For RL imbalance, **first right-rotate the right child**, then **left-rotate the parent**.

```
        P               P                G
       / \             / \              / \
      L   R    →      L   G    →       P   R
         / \             / \          / \ / \
        G   C           B   R        L  B C   (no, fix below)
       / \                 / \
      B   C               (rest)
```

The cleanest canonical RL diagram:

```
        P               P                  G
       / \             / \                / \
      L   R    →      L   G      →       P   R
         / \             / \            / \ / \
        G   D           B   R          L  B C   D
       / \                 / \
      B   C               C   D
```

## 3.2 Height Update Formula

After every modification to a subtree, recompute the height of the affected node:

$$h(v) = 1 + \max\bigl(h(v.\text{left}),\; h(v.\text{right})\bigr)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to update heights **after rotation** as well, leading to wrong balance factors in subsequent steps. Always update **from the bottom up**.

## 3.3 INSERTION Algorithm (Complete)

**Input:** A key $k$ to insert into AVL tree $T$ rooted at $r$.  
**Output:** The new root of $T$ after insertion and rebalancing.

**Steps:**

1. **Standard BST Insert:** If $T$ is empty, return a new node with key $k$. Otherwise, recurse left if $k < T.key$, recurse right if $k > T.key$. (Duplicates are typically ignored or placed consistently.)
2. **Update Height** of the current node $T$ using $h(T) = 1 + \max(h(T.\text{left}), h(T.\text{right}))$.
3. **Compute Balance Factor** $BF = h(T.\text{left}) - h(T.\text{right})$.
4. **Check Imbalance.** If $BF \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$, return $T$ unchanged.
5. **Identify Case and Rotate:**
   - **LL Case:** $BF = +2$ and $k < T.\text{left}.\text{key}$ → perform **Right Rotation** on $T$.
   - **RR Case:** $BF = -2$ and $k > T.\text{right}.\text{key}$ → perform **Left Rotation** on $T$.
   - **LR Case:** $BF = +2$ and $k > T.\text{left}.\text{key}$ → perform **Left Rotation on $T.\text{left}$**, then **Right Rotation on $T$**.
   - **RL Case:** $BF = -2$ and $k < T.\text{right}.\text{key}$ → perform **Right Rotation on $T.\text{right}$**, then **Left Rotation on $T$**.

## 3.4 DELETION Algorithm (Complete)

**Input:** A key $k$ to delete from AVL tree $T$.  
**Output:** The new root of $T$ after deletion and rebalancing.

**Steps:**

1. **Standard BST Delete:**
   - If $k < T.\text{key}$ → recurse left.
   - If $k > T.\text{key}$ → recurse right.
   - If $k = T.\text{key}$:
     - If $T$ has **no children** or **one child** → replace $T$ with its child (or NULL).
     - If $T$ has **two children** → find the **inorder successor** (smallest in right subtree) or **inorder predecessor** (largest in left subtree), copy its key to $T$, and recursively delete the successor/predecessor.
2. **Update Height** of $T$ using the standard formula.
3. **Compute Balance Factor** $BF = h(T.\text{left}) - h(T.\text{right})$.
4. **Check Imbalance.** If $|BF| > 1$, identify and apply the appropriate rotation:
   - **LL Case:** $BF = +2$ and $BF(T.\text{left}) \geq 0$ → **Right Rotation** on $T$.
   - **LR Case:** $BF = +2$ and $BF(T.\text{left}) < 0$ → **Left then Right** rotation.
   - **RR Case:** $BF = -2$ and $BF(T.\text{right}) \leq 0$ → **Left Rotation** on $T$.
   - **RL Case:** $BF = -2$ and $BF(T.\text{right}) > 0$ → **Right then Left** rotation.
5. Return the (possibly new) root.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Deletion Trap:** In deletion, the imbalance check uses the **balance factor of the child** (not the insertion path), because there is no "new" key. Use the child's BF to distinguish between LL/LR and RR/RL. Many students mistakenly use the insertion-style rule and lose marks.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Insertion Walkthrough

**Insert the sequence: 30, 20, 40, 10, 25, 50, 5** into an empty AVL tree.

**Step 1: Insert 30**
```
30
```

**Step 2: Insert 20** (go left of 30)
```
   30
  /
 20
```
Heights: $h(20)=1$, $h(30)=2$. BFs: $BF(20)=0$, $BF(30)=+1$. Balanced.

**Step 3: Insert 40** (go right of 30)
```
   30
  /  \
 20   40
```
$BF(30)=0$. Balanced.

**Step 4: Insert 10** (10 < 20 → left of 20)
```
     30
    /  \
   20   40
  /
 10
```
$BF(20)=+1$, $BF(30)=+1$. Balanced.

**Step 5: Insert 25** (25 < 30 → left; 25 > 20 → right of 20)
```
     30
    /  \
   20   40
  /  \
 10   25
```
$BF(20)=0$, $BF(30)=+1$. Balanced.

**Step 6: Insert 50** (50 > 30 → right; 50 > 40 → right of 40)
```
     30
    /  \
   20   40
  /  \    \
 10   25   50
```
$BF(40)=-1$, $BF(30)=+1$. Balanced.

**Step 7: Insert 5** (5 < 30 → left; 5 < 20 → left; 5 < 10 → left of 10)
```
       30
      /  \
     20   40
    /  \    \
   10   25   50
  /
 5
```
Compute BFs:
- $BF(5)=0$, $BF(10)=+1$, $BF(20)=+1$, $BF(40)=-1$, $BF(30)=+1$.

Wait — $h(20)=3$ (rooted at 10 with left child 5), $h(40)=2$, so $BF(30) = 3 - 2 = +1$. Still balanced. **No rotation needed yet.**

> [!NOTE]
> The example above shows that **not every insertion triggers rotation**. The first rotation will occur at the next imbalance.

## 3.6 Worked Example — LL Rotation

**Setup:** Suppose we have a subtree:
```
   30           (BF = +2)
  /
 20             (BF = +1)
 /
10              (BF = 0)
```
Insertion was into the left subtree of the left child → **LL Case** → apply **Right Rotation** on 30.

**After Right Rotation on 30:**
```
   20
  /  \
 10   30
```
Heights: $h(10)=1$, $h(30)=1$, $h(20)=2$. All BFs $\in \{-1, 0, 1\}$. Rebalanced.

## 3.7 Worked Example — RR Rotation

**Setup:**
```
  10            (BF = -2)
   \
    20          (BF = -1)
     \
      30        (BF = 0)
```
Insertion into right subtree of right child → **RR Case** → apply **Left Rotation** on 10.

**After Left Rotation on 10:**
```
    20
   /  \
  10   30
```

## 3.8 Worked Example — LR Rotation

**Setup:**
```
   30           (BF = +2)
  /
 20             (BF = -1)
   \
    25          (BF = 0)
```
Insertion was at 25, which is in the right subtree of the left child of 30 → **LR Case**.

**Step A — Left rotate 20:**
```
   30
  /
 25
 /
20
```
**Step B — Right rotate 30:**
```
    25
   /  \
  20   30
```

## 3.9 Worked Example — RL Rotation

**Setup:**
```
  10            (BF = -2)
   \
    30          (BF = +1)
   /
  20            (BF = 0)
```
Insertion at 20, in the left subtree of the right child of 10 → **RL Case**.

**Step A — Right rotate 30:**
```
  10
   \
    20
     \
      30
```
**Step B — Left rotate 10:**
```
    20
   /  \
  10   30
```

## 3.10 Worked Example — Deletion Walkthrough

Take the AVL tree:
```
       20
      /  \
    10    30
   /     /  \
  5     25   40
```
**Delete 5** (leaf):
```
       20
      /  \
    10    30
          /  \
         25   40
```
Update heights: $h(10)=1$, $h(30)=2$, $h(20)=3$. BFs: $BF(10)=0$, $BF(30)=0$, $BF(20)=-1$. Balanced.

**Delete 40** (leaf):
```
       20
      /  \
    10    30
          /
         25
```
Heights: $h(10)=1$, $h(30)=2$, $h(20)=3$. BFs: $BF(10)=0$, $BF(30)=+1$, $BF(20)=+1$. Balanced.

**Delete 10** (node with one child — none here, so becomes a leaf):
```
       20
      /  \
    NULL  30
          /
         25
```
Heights: $h(20)=3$, $h(30)=2$, $h(25)=1$. BFs: $BF(20)= -2$ (left height 0, right height 2) — wait, the right child is 30 with height 2; the left subtree of 20 is NULL with height 0. So $BF(20) = 0 - 2 = -2$.

**Imbalance detected at 20!** Now check the child (30). $BF(30) = h(30.\text{left}) - h(30.\text{right}) = 1 - 0 = +1$. So we have $BF(20) = -2$ and $BF(30) = +1$ → **RL Case** → right rotate 30, then left rotate 20.

**After Right Rotate on 30:**
```
       20
      /  \
    NULL  25
            \
            30
```
**After Left Rotate on 20:**
```
        25
       /  \
      20   30
```
The tree is now balanced.

## 3.11 Complexity Proof Sketch

The height of an AVL tree is $O(\log n)$. Search, insert, and delete each walk down at most one path from root to leaf, performing $O(1)$ work per node (recompute height, check BF, maybe rotate). Thus:

$$T_{\text{search}}(n) = T_{\text{insert}}(n) = T_{\text{delete}}(n) = O(\log n)$$

For insertion, at most **1 rotation** is needed. For deletion, at most $O(\log n)$ rotations may cascade up the path.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AVL Tree Insertion — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Insert key k]) --> A1[Perform standard BST insertion]
    A1 --> A2[Recurse to left if k less than node.key]
    A1 --> A3[Recurse to right if k greater than node.key]
    A2 --> A4[Update height of current node]
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Compute Balance Factor BF]
    A5 --> A6{Absolute value of BF greater than 1?}
    A6 -- No --> A7([Return unchanged node])
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[Identify case from BF and child BF]
    A8 --> B1{LL Case? BF equals plus 2 and child BF is plus 1 or 0}
    A8 --> B2{RR Case? BF equals minus 2 and child BF is minus 1 or 0}
    A8 --> B3{LR Case? BF equals plus 2 and child BF is minus 1}
    A8 --> B4{RL Case? BF equals minus 2 and child BF is plus 1}
    B1 -- Yes --> C1[Perform single RIGHT rotation on node]
    B2 -- Yes --> C2[Perform single LEFT rotation on node]
    B3 -- Yes --> C3[Perform LEFT rotation on left child]
    C3 --> C4[Then perform RIGHT rotation on node]
    B4 -- Yes --> C5[Perform RIGHT rotation on right child]
    C5 --> C6[Then perform LEFT rotation on node]
    C1 --> D1([Return new subtree root])
    C2 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
```

## 4.2 AVL Tree Deletion — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0([Start: Delete key k]) --> E1[Perform standard BST deletion]
    E1 --> E2{Node to delete found?}
    E2 -- No --> E3([Return unchanged])
    E2 -- Yes --> E4{Two children?}
    E4 -- No --> E5[Replace node with its only child or NULL]
    E4 -- Yes --> E6[Find inorder successor or predecessor]
    E6 --> E7[Copy successor or predecessor key to current node]
    E7 --> E8[Recursively delete successor or predecessor]
    E5 --> F1[Update height of current node]
    E8 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Compute Balance Factor BF]
    F2 --> F3{Absolute value of BF greater than 1?}
    F3 -- No --> F4([Return node])
    F3 -- Yes --> F5[Determine case from BF and child BF]
    F5 --> G1{LL: BF plus 2 and child BF plus 1 or 0}
    F5 --> G2{RR: BF minus 2 and child BF minus 1 or 0}
    F5 --> G3{LR: BF plus 2 and child BF minus 1}
    F5 --> G4{RL: BF minus 2 and child BF plus 1}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Right rotate node]
    G2 -- Yes --> H2[Left rotate node]
    G3 -- Yes --> H3[Left rotate child, then right rotate node]
    G4 -- Yes --> H4[Right rotate child, then left rotate node]
    H1 --> I1([Return new root])
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
```

## 4.3 Rotation Visual — LL Case (Right Rotation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP["P BF plus 2"] --> nodeL["L BF plus 1"]
    nodeL --> nodeA["A"]
    nodeL --> nodeB["B"]
    nodeP --> nodeR["R"]
    nodeQ1[" "]:::hidden --> nodeP
    classDef hidden display:none
```

**After Right Rotation on P:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeL2["L new root BF 0"] --> nodeA2["A"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeP2["P BF 0"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeB2["B"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeR2["R"]
    classDef hidden display:none
```

## 4.4 Rotation Visual — LR Case (Double Rotation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP3["P BF plus 2"] --> nodeL3["L BF minus 1"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeA3["A"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeG3["G inserted here"]
    nodeG3 --> nodeB3["B"]
    nodeG3 --> nodeC3["C"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeR3["R"]
```

**After Left-Right Double Rotation:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeG4["G new root BF 0"] --> nodeL4["L BF 0"]
    nodeL4 --> nodeA4["A"]
    nodeL4 --> nodeB4["B"]
    nodeG4 --> nodeP4["P BF 0"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeC4["C"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeR4["R"]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Architecture — AVL Tree in a Database Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U([User Query]) --> Q[Query Parser]
    Q --> I[Index Lookup]
    I --> AVL[AVL Tree in Memory]
    AVL --> R1[Root Node]
    R1 --> L1[Left Subtree]
    R1 --> Rt1[Right Subtree]
    L1 --> R2[Root of Left]
    Rt1 --> R3[Root of Right]
    R2 --> LL[Left Left Child]
    R2 --> LR[Left Right Child]
    R3 --> RL[Right Left Child]
    R3 --> RR[Right Right Child]
    AVL --> M[Return Matching Record Pointer]
    M --> DB[(Disk Resident Data Pages)]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define an **AVL tree**. What is the **balance factor** of a node? List all valid values of the balance factor for an AVL tree to remain balanced.

**Model Answer:**

An **AVL tree** is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree (BST) in which, for every node, the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees (called the **Balance Factor**) is at most **1**.

$$BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$$

For an AVL tree to remain balanced:

$$BF(v) \in \{-1,\; 0,\; +1\} \quad \forall \, v$$

Any node with $|BF(v)| > 1$ triggers a rotation. The tree was invented by **Adelson-Velsky and Landis in 1962** and guarantees $O(\log n)$ search, insert, and delete operations.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between the **LL** and **LR** rotation cases in an AVL tree. In which scenario is each applied?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LL Rotation | LR Rotation |
|---|---|---|
| Imbalance condition | $BF = +2$, left child's $BF \geq 0$ | $BF = +2$, left child's $BF = -1$ |
| Insertion path | Inserted into left subtree of left child | Inserted into right subtree of left child |
| Shape | Zig-zag bent left | Zig-zag bent right then left |
| Rotation | Single **right rotation** at the unbalanced node | **Left rotation** at left child, then **right rotation** at unbalanced node |
| Number of rotations | 1 (single) | 2 (double) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulation, Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks split typically as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both internal choices must be answered OR the student answers either choice fully.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Insertion**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q-A1]**
> **(a)** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following keys in the given order. Show the tree and the balance factor of every node after **each insertion**, and clearly state any rotation performed.
> Keys: **50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 25, 40**
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Insert 50**
```
50   BF: 0
```

**Step 2: Insert 20** (20 < 50 → left)
```
  50   BF: +1
 /
20     BF: 0
```

**Step 3: Insert 70** (70 > 50 → right)
```
  50   BF: 0
 /  \
20    70
BFs: 0, 0
```

**Step 4: Insert 10** (10 < 50 → left; 10 < 20 → left)
```
    50      BF: +1
   /  \
  20    70   BFs: +1, 0
 /
10            BF: 0
```

**Step 5: Insert 30** (30 < 50 → left; 30 > 20 → right)
```
     50        BF: +2  ← UNBALANCED
    /  \
   20    70
  /  \
 10   30
```
At node 50: $BF = h(20) - h(70) = 2 - 1 = +2$. Unbalanced.

Look at the **tall child** (20). Its $BF = h(10) - h(30) = 1 - 1 = 0$.

Insertion path: Left (50→20) then Right (20→30) → **LR Case**.

**Apply LR Rotation:**

*Step A — Left rotate 20:*
```
     50
    /  \
   30    70
  /
 20
 /
10
```

*Step B — Right rotate 50:*
```
    30
   /  \
  20   50
 /     / \
10    ( ) 70
```
After rotation, the subtree is balanced.

**Step 6: Insert 25** (25 < 30 → left; 25 > 20 → right)
```
        30       BF: +1
       /  \
      20   50
     /  \    \
    10   25   70
```
Heights: $h(10)=1$, $h(25)=1$, $h(20)=2$, $h(70)=1$, $h(50)=2$, $h(30)=3$. BFs: $BF(20)=0$, $BF(50)=-1$, $BF(30)=+1$. Balanced.

**Step 7: Insert 40** (40 > 30 → right; 40 < 50 → left)
```
        30       BF: +1
       /  \
      20   50
     /  \  / \
    10  25 40 70
```
Heights: $h(40)=1$, $h(50)=2$, $h(30)=3$. BFs: $BF(50) = 1 - 1 = 0$, $BF(30) = 2 - 2 = 0$. Balanced.

**Final AVL Tree:**
```
        30
       /  \
      20   50
     /  \  / \
    10  25 40 70
```

> **(b)** Starting from the AVL tree constructed in (a), **delete the key 50** and show the resulting tree after any necessary rotations. Compute the balance factor of each affected node.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Original tree:**
```
        30
       /  \
      20   50
     /  \  / \
    10  25 40 70
```

**Step 1: Locate 50.** 50 is a node with **two children** (40 and 70). We use the **inorder successor** rule.

Inorder successor of 50 = **70** (smallest in right subtree of 50).

Copy 70 into node 50's position. Delete the original 70.

**Tree after step 1 (logical replacement):**
```
        30
       /  \
      20   70    (was 50, now 70)
     /  \  /
    10  25 40
```

Wait — we replaced 50 with 70, but the original 70 was a leaf. So the new 70's children are: left = original left of 50 = 40, right = NULL.

**Tree:**
```
        30         (height 3)
       /  \
      20   70       (height 2)
     /  \  /
    10  25 40
```

**Step 2: Update heights and check balance.**

$h(40)=1$, $h(70)=2$, $h(10)=1$, $h(25)=1$, $h(20)=2$, $h(30)=3$.

$BF(20) = 1 - 1 = 0$.  
$BF(70) = 1 - 0 = +1$.  
$BF(30) = h(20) - h(70) = 2 - 2 = 0$.

**Tree is balanced.** No rotation needed.

**Final AVL Tree after deleting 50:**
```
        30
       /  \
      20   70
     /  \  /
    10  25 40
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (All Rotations)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q-B1]**
> **(a)** Explain the **four rotation cases** (LL, RR, LR, RL) used in AVL tree rebalancing. For each case, draw the **before and after** tree structure and state the imbalance condition.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** $BF(\text{node}) = h(\text{left subtree}) - h(\text{right subtree})$. A node is *unbalanced* if $|BF| > 1$.

**Case 1: LL Rotation (Single Right Rotation)**

*Imbalance condition:* $BF(A) = +2$ and the new key was inserted into the **left subtree of A's left child**.

```
      A (BF=+2)               B
     / \                     / \
    B   T3      →           C   A
   / \                         / \
  C   T2                      T2  T3
 /
T1
```

*Steps:* Let $B$ = $A.left$. Set $A.left = B.right$, $B.right = A$. Update heights of $A$ then $B$.

---

**Case 2: RR Rotation (Single Left Rotation)**

*Imbalance condition:* $BF(A) = -2$ and the new key was inserted into the **right subtree of A's right child**.

```
    A (BF=-2)                 C
   / \                       / \
  T1   B         →          A   D
      / \                  / \
     T2  C                T1  T2
        / \
       T3  D
```

*Steps:* Let $C$ = $A.right$. Set $A.right = C.left$, $C.left = A$. Update heights of $A$ then $C$.

---

**Case 3: LR Rotation (Left-Right Double Rotation)**

*Imbalance condition:* $BF(A) = +2$ and the new key was inserted into the **right subtree of A's left child**.

```
      A (BF=+2)              C
     / \                    / \
    B   T4      →          B   A
   / \                    / \ / \
  T1  C                  T1 T2 T3 T4
     / \
    T2  T3
```

*Steps:* Left-rotate $B$ first, then right-rotate $A$.

---

**Case 4: RL Rotation (Right-Left Double Rotation)**

*Imbalance condition:* $BF(A) = -2$ and the new key was inserted into the **left subtree of A's right child**.

```
    A (BF=-2)                 C
   / \                       / \
  T1   B         →          A   B
      / \                  / \ / \
     C   T4               T1 T2 T3 T4
    / \
   T2  T3
```

*Steps:* Right-rotate $B$ first, then left-rotate $A$.

> **(b)** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the keys **15, 10, 20, 8, 12, 16, 25** in the given order. Show the tree after each insertion, label the **balance factors**, and explicitly identify any rotation performed.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Insert 15:** `15` (BF=0)

**Insert 10:** `15(BF=+1) / 10(BF=0)`

**Insert 20:** `15(BF=0) / 10 20` (BFs: 0, 0)

**Insert 8:** `15(BF=+1) / 10(BF=+1) / 8(BF=0) 20`

**Insert 12:** `15(BF=+2) / 10(BF=0) / 8 12 20`
$BF(15) = 2 - 1 = +2$. Imbalance at 15.
Left child 10 has $BF = h(8) - h(12) = 1 - 1 = 0$. Insertion path: Left (15→10) then Right (10→12) → **LR Case**.

**LR Rotation:**
- Left rotate 10 → `12 / 8 10`
- Right rotate 15 → `12 / 10 15 / 8 ( ) 20`

**Resulting tree:**
```
    12   (BF=0)
   /  \
  10   15
 /      \
8       20
```

**Insert 16:** (16 > 12 → right; 16 > 15 → right of 15)
```
      12       (BF=0)
     /  \
    10   15    (BF=-1)
   /      \
  8       20   (BF=+1)
         /
        16     (BF=0)
```
Heights: $h(8)=1$, $h(10)=2$, $h(16)=1$, $h(20)=2$, $h(15)=3$, $h(12)=4$. BFs: $BF(10)=+1$, $BF(20)=+1$, $BF(15)=-1$, $BF(12)=+1$. Balanced.

**Insert 25:** (25 > 12 → right; 25 > 15 → right; 25 > 20 → right of 20)
```
      12           (BF=+1)
     /  \
    10   15        (BF=-2) ← UNBALANCED
   /      \
  8       20       (BF=-1)
         /  \
        16   25
```
At node 15: $BF = h(20) - h(\text{NULL right}) = 2 - 0 = -2$. Imbalance at 15.

Right child 20 has $BF = h(16) - h(25) = 1 - 1 = 0$. Insertion path: Right (15→20) then Right (20→25) → **RR Case**.

**Apply Left Rotation on 15:**
```
      12
     /  \
    10   20
   /    /  \
  8    15   25
      /
     16
```
Heights: $h(15)=2$, $h(20)=3$, $h(12)=4$. BFs: $BF(20) = h(15) - h(25) = 2 - 1 = +1$, $BF(12) = 2 - 3 = -1$. Balanced.

**Final AVL Tree:**
```
       12
      /  \
     10   20
    /    /  \
   8    15   25
       /
      16
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to update heights** after a rotation (common in both insertion and deletion). Always recompute $h$ from the bottom up.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** for balance factor. KTU expects $BF = h_L - h_R$, so a **positive** value means left-heavy.
> 3. **Confusing insertion vs deletion rotation rules.** In insertion, use the path of the inserted key. In deletion, use the **balance factor of the child** (because the inserted/deleted key is not new).
> 4. **Not drawing intermediate trees** in LR/RL double rotations. Examiners award partial credit for the intermediate single rotation. Always show both sub-steps.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify which rotation** triggered the imbalance. Always state the case (LL, RR, LR, or RL) explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AVL Trees — High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Self-balancing BST where every node's $|BF| \leq 1$.
- **Balance Factor:** $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$; valid values are $-1, 0, +1$.
- **Height bound:** $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ → all operations $O(\log n)$.
- **Minimum nodes recurrence:** $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$ with $N(0)=0$, $N(1)=1$.
- **Four rotation cases:**
  - **LL** (BF=+2, child BF ≥ 0) → **single right rotation**.
  - **RR** (BF=-2, child BF ≤ 0) → **single left rotation**.
  - **LR** (BF=+2, child BF = -1) → **left then right** double rotation.
  - **RL** (BF=-2, child BF = +1) → **right then left** double rotation.
- **Insertion:** Walk down, insert as in BST, update heights, walk back up applying at most **one rotation**.
- **Deletion:** Walk down, delete as in BST, update heights, walk back up applying **rotations** as needed (up to $O(\log n)$).
- **Height update:** $h(v) = 1 + \max(h_L, h_R)$ — always recompute **after every structural change**.
- **Inorder successor/predecessor** used when deleting a node with two children.
- **Time complexities:** Search $= O(\log n)$; Insert $= O(\log n)$ with at most 1 rotation; Delete $= O(\log n)$ with up to $O(\log n)$ rotations.
- **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ for storing the tree.
- **Sign convention:** Positive BF = left-heavy; negative BF = right-heavy. **Do not invert.**
- **Difference from Red-Black Trees:** AVL trees are *more strictly balanced* (faster lookups), Red-Black trees are *loosely balanced* (faster inserts/deletes).
- **Practical use:** In-memory databases, compiler symbol tables, network routing tables.
- **KTU favourite questions:**
  1. Construct an AVL tree for a given sequence (very common in 14-mark questions).
  2. Delete a node and show resulting rebalancing (also very common).
  3. Prove height bound using Fibonacci-like recurrence.
  4. Identify rotation case from a given tree diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that systematically explores all vertices of a graph in **level-by-level (layered) order**. Starting from a source vertex $s$, BFS first visits all immediate neighbors of $s$ (distance 1), then all unvisited vertices at distance 2, and so on, until every reachable vertex has been visited. BFS uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) Queue** as its auxiliary data structure.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you drop a stone in a still pond. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles** — first the closest ring, then the next ring, and so forth. BFS behaves exactly like these ripples. Starting from a source, the algorithm "sweeps" outward layer by layer. Every vertex at distance $d$ is fully processed before any vertex at distance $d+1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BFS Key Properties (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - Uses a **Queue** (FIFO discipline)
> - Computes the **shortest path (in number of edges)** from source in an unweighted graph
> - Produces a **Breadth-First Tree (BFS Tree)** spanning the reachable component
> - Total work = $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$

---

## 1.2 Depth-First Search (DFS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Depth-First Search (DFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores a graph by going as **deep as possible** along each branch before backtracking. DFS employs a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) Stack** (either implicitly via recursion or explicitly) to remember the next vertex to visit.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of exploring a **maze** with a ball of string. You walk forward, and at every junction, you choose one path, tie the string, and keep going. When you hit a dead-end, you **backtrack** along the string to the last junction and try the next untried path. DFS is exactly this — recursion or an explicit stack replaces the string.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFS Key Properties (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - Uses a **Stack** (LIFO discipline) or **Recursion**
> - Generates two timestamps per vertex: **discovery time (`d[v]`)** and **finishing time (`f[v]`)**
> - Produces a **Depth-First Forest** (set of DFS Trees)
> - Total work = $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$
> - Foundation for **topological sorting**, **SCC decomposition**, and **cycle detection**

---

## 1.3 Strongly Connected Components (SCC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Strongly Connected Component** of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal set of vertices $C \subseteq V$** such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, there exists a directed path from $u$ to $v$ **and** a directed path from $v$ to $u$. In other words, the subgraph induced by $C$ is **strongly connected**, and $C$ cannot be extended by adding any more vertex while remaining strongly connected.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **social network** where a "follow" is one-way. A strongly connected component is a **tight-knit group** where every person can reach every other person (directly or via chain). In an academic department, a circle of co-authors where each cites every other (in a cyclic manner) forms an SCC. The **component graph** (condensation) of a directed graph is always a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

---

## 1.4 BFS vs DFS — Quick Comparison

| Parameter | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | Queue (FIFO) | Stack / Recursion (LIFO) |
| Traversal Order | Level-order | Branch-then-backtrack |
| Shortest Path (unweighted) | Yes | No |
| Memory (Worst Case) | $O(\vert V \vert)$ (queue) | $O(\vert V \vert)$ (recursion stack) |
| Best For | Shortest path, bipartite test | Topological sort, SCC, cycle detection |
| Back Edges | Reveal shortest cycles | Reveal cycles generically |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BFS — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Inputs:** Graph $G = (V, E)$ represented via adjacency list, source vertex $s$.
**Outputs:** `dist[v]` = shortest distance from $s$ to $v$, `pred[v]` = predecessor in BFS tree, `color[v]`.

**Color Convention:**
- **WHITE** = undiscovered
- **GRAY** = discovered, in queue, not fully processed
- **BLACK** = fully processed (all neighbors enqueued)

**Algorithm Steps (Why and How explained):**

1. **Initialize** all vertices to `WHITE`, set `dist[s] = 0`, `pred[s] = NIL`, and color $s$ as `GRAY`. Enqueue $s$ into $Q$.
   *Why?* The queue acts as the frontier. We mark $s$ as the starting frontier.
2. **While** $Q$ is not empty:
   *How?* A non-empty queue means undiscovered vertices remain reachable.
3. **Dequeue** vertex $u = Q.dequeue()$.
4. **For each** neighbor $v$ of $u$ in `Adj[u]`:
   *How?* Scan all edges leaving $u$ in the adjacency list.
5. **If** `color[v] == WHITE`:
   - Set `color[v] = GRAY`
   - Set `dist[v] = dist[u] + 1`
   - Set `pred[v] = u`
   - `Q.enqueue(v)`
6. After all neighbors processed: set `color[u] = BLACK`.
7. **Return** `dist[]` and `pred[]` arrays.

---

## 2.2 DFS — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Inputs:** Graph $G = (V, E)$.
**Outputs:** `d[v]`, `f[v]` timestamps, `pred[v]`, DFS forest.

**Timestamp Convention (Global Clock `time`):**
- **Discovery time `d[v]`** = clock value when $v$ becomes GRAY (first time visited).
- **Finishing time `f[v]`** = clock value when $v$ becomes BLACK (all descendants finished).

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** all vertices to WHITE, `time = 0`, `pred[v] = NIL`.
2. **For each** vertex $u \in V$:
   *Why?* The graph may be disconnected; we need a forest, not just one tree.
3. **If** `color[u] == WHITE`: call `DFS-VISIT(u)`.
4. **DFS-VISIT(u):**
   - `time = time + 1`; `d[u] = time`; `color[u] = GRAY`
   - **For each** $v \in Adj[u]$:
     * If `color[v] == WHITE`: set `pred[v] = u`; call `DFS-VISIT(v)` (recursion = implicit stack)
   - `color[u] = BLACK`; `time = time + 1`; `f[u] = time`

**Edge Classification in DFS (Crucial for KTU):**
- **Tree Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ discovered by $u$ (i.e., $v$ was WHITE when DFS examined it).
- **Back Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in DFS tree (indicates a cycle).
- **Forward Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is a descendant of $u$ (non-tree).
- **Cross Edge** $(u,v)$: $v$ is neither ancestor nor descendant (only in directed graphs).

---

## 2.3 Strongly Connected Components — Kosaraju's Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kosaraju's Two-Pass Algorithm (Standard KTU 2024 Module 2 coverage):**

**Key Lemma (Cormen et al.):** If $C$ and $C'$ are distinct SCCs in $G$, and there is an edge $(u,v)$ with $u \in C, v \in C'$, then the **highest finishing time** $f[v]$ in the component graph is **greater** than the highest finishing time of any vertex in $C$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Run **DFS** on $G$; compute finishing times $f[v]$ for all $v \in V$.
2. Compute $G^T$ (transpose — reverse all edge directions).
3. Run **DFS** on $G^T$, but process vertices in **decreasing order of $f[v]$** (i.e., main loop iterates vertices sorted by finishing time descending).
4. The DFS trees obtained in Step 3 are the **SCCs**.

**Why it Works:** Vertices in the "source SCC" of the component DAG have the latest finishing times. Reversing edges means those become a "sink" in $G^T$, so DFS from them captures the entire SCC before crossing into others.

---

## 2.4 Tarjan's SCC Algorithm (Alternate Approach)

Uses **one** DFS pass. Maintains:
- `disc[v]` = discovery time of $v$
- `low[v]` = smallest discovery time reachable from $v$'s subtree via tree edges + at most one back edge
- A stack `S` of currently active vertices.

When `low[v] == disc[v]`, $v$ is the **root of an SCC** — pop stack until $v$ is popped; that forms one SCC.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|
| BFS Time Complexity | $T_{BFS} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Adjacency list representation |
| BFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Queue + color + dist arrays |
| DFS Time Complexity | $T_{DFS} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $\Theta$ if all reachable |
| DFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Recursion stack worst case |
| Sum of all `d[v]` over a run | $\sum d[v] = \frac{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert + 1)}{2} - $ sum of roots | Discrete timestamps start at 1 |
| BFS Shortest Path | $dist[v]$ = minimum number of edges from $s$ to $v$ | Unweighted graph only |
| SCC Count | Number of DFS trees in Step 3 of Kosaraju's | Always $\leq \vert V \vert$ |
| Number of Edges in Component Graph | $\leq \vert V \vert - 1$ when component graph is a tree (DAG) | Strict inequality for DAG |
| Parenthesis Theorem | $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are either disjoint or one contains the other | $u$ is ancestor of $v$ iff $[d[u], f[u]] \supseteq [d[v], f[v]]$ |
| White-Path Theorem | $v$ is descendant of $u$ in DFS tree $\iff$ at time $d[u]$ there is a white path from $u$ to $v$ | Critical for correctness proofs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **BFS**: GPS shortest-route (in hops), web crawlers (level-by-level URL discovery), peer-to-peer networks, bipartite matching pre-checks.
> - **DFS**: Topological scheduling (build systems like Make, package managers), deadlock detection in operating systems, maze/circuit solvers, syntax tree parsing in compilers.
> - **SCC**: Identifying tightly coupled modules in software, finding cycles in financial transaction networks, social network influence analysis, compiler optimization (strongly connected regions often inlined).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — BFS Traversal with Full Trace

**Graph $G$:** Consider the undirected graph with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and edge set $E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (3,4), (4,5), (5,6)\}$. Let source $s = 1$.

**Adjacency List:**
- 1: [2, 3]
- 2: [1, 4]
- 3: [1, 4]
- 4: [2, 3, 5]
- 5: [4, 6]
- 6: [5]

**BFS Trace (Frontier, Visited Set, dist, pred):**

**Initial State:**
- $Q = [1]$, $\text{color}[1] = GRAY$, $\text{dist}[1] = 0$, $\text{pred}[1] = NIL$.

**Iteration 1:** Dequeue $u = 1$.
- Neighbor 2: WHITE $\rightarrow$ set $\text{dist}[2] = 1$, $\text{pred}[2] = 1$, enqueue 2.
- Neighbor 3: WHITE $\rightarrow$ set $\text{dist}[3] = 1$, $\text{pred}[3] = 1$, enqueue 3.
- $Q = [2, 3]$; $\text{color}[1] = BLACK$.

**Iteration 2:** Dequeue $u = 2$.
- Neighbor 1: BLACK (skip).
- Neighbor 4: WHITE $\rightarrow$ set $\text{dist}[4] = 2$, $\text{pred}[4] = 2$, enqueue 4.
- $Q = [3, 4]$; $\text{color}[2] = BLACK$.

**Iteration 3:** Dequeue $u = 3$.
- Neighbor 1: BLACK.
- Neighbor 4: GRAY (already in queue). Skip.
- $Q = [4]$; $\text{color}[3] = BLACK$.

**Iteration 4:** Dequeue $u = 4$.
- Neighbor 2: BLACK.
- Neighbor 3: BLACK.
- Neighbor 5: WHITE $\rightarrow$ $\text{dist}[5] = 3$, $\text{pred}[5] = 4$, enqueue 5.
- $Q = [5]$; $\text{color}[4] = BLACK$.

**Iteration 5:** Dequeue $u = 5$.
- Neighbor 4: BLACK.
- Neighbor 6: WHITE $\rightarrow$ $\text{dist}[6] = 4$, $\text{pred}[6] = 5$, enqueue 6.
- $Q = [6]$; $\text{color}[5] = BLACK$.

**Iteration 6:** Dequeue $u = 6$.
- Neighbor 5: BLACK.
- $Q = []$; $\text{color}[6] = BLACK$.

**Final Result Tables:**

| Vertex $v$ | dist[v] | pred[v] | Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | NIL | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| 5 | 3 | 4 | 3 |
| 6 | 4 | 5 | 4 |

**BFS Traversal Order:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6$

## 3.2 Worked Example — DFS with Timestamps

Same graph $G$ as Section 3.1. We run `DFS(G)`.

**Initial:** All WHITE, $time = 0$.

**DFS-VISIT(1):** $time = 1$, $d[1] = 1$, color[1] = GRAY.
- Neighbor 2: WHITE $\rightarrow$ DFS-VISIT(2).
  - $time = 2$, $d[2] = 2$, GRAY.

            - Neighbor 4: GRAY (ancestor) $\Rightarrow$ **Back Edge** $(3,4)$.

  - $time = 11$, $f[2] = 11$, BLACK.

| Vertex | d[v] | f[v] | pred[v] |

| 1 | 1 | 12 | NIL |

| 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| 5 | 6 | 9 | 4 |
| 6 | 7 | 8 | 5 |

**Verification (Parenthesis Theorem):** $[1,12] \supset [2,11] \supset [3,10] \supset [4,5]$, $[3,10] \supset [6,9] \supset [7,8]$. All intervals nest correctly. ✓

## 3.3 Time Complexity Derivation — BFS

Let $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$.

**Initialization:** $O(n)$ — one assignment per vertex.

**Main Loop:** Each vertex is enqueued **at most once** and dequeued **at most once** $\Rightarrow$ $O(n)$ queue operations.

**Adjacency Scan:** The total work done in the inner loop is proportional to the **sum of adjacency list lengths** = $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$ for undirected graphs (or $m$ for directed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{BFS} &= O(n)_{\text{init}} + O(n)_{\text{enq/deq}} + O(m)_{\text{adj scan}} \\
T_{BFS} &= O(n + m)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **connected** graphs, $m \geq n - 1$, so $T_{BFS} = O(m)$.

---

## 3.4 Time Complexity Derivation — DFS

Each vertex is discovered **once** and finished **once** $\Rightarrow$ $O(n)$ for the `DFS-VISIT` overhead.

Each edge $(u,v)$ is examined **exactly once** (when $u$'s adjacency list is processed). The work per edge is $O(1)$ for the color check.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DFS} &= O(n)_{\text{calls}} + O(m)_{\text{edge examines}} \\
T_{DFS} &= O(n + m)
\end{aligned}
$$

This bound is **tight** — it is actually $\Theta(n + m)$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Kosaraju's Algorithm for SCC

**Directed Graph $G$:** $V = \{a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$
- Edges: $(a,b), (b,c), (b,e), (b,f), (c,d), (c,g), (d,c), (d,h), (e,a), (e,f), (f,g), (g,f), (g,h), (h,h)$

**Step 1: DFS on $G$ → compute finishing times.**

Assume adjacency lists as given; run DFS. The discovery/finishing times (in this trace):

| Vertex | d[v] | f[v] |
|---|---|---|
| a | 1 | 16 |
| b | 2 | 15 |
| e | 3 | 14 |
| f | 4 | 13 |
| g | 5 | 12 |
| h | 6 | 11 |
| d | 7 | 10 |
| c | 8 | 9 |

**Step 2: Transpose $G^T$.** Reverse all edges:
- $(b,a), (c,b), (e,b), (f,b), (d,c), (g,c), (c,d), (h,d), (a,e), (f,e), (g,f), (f,g), (h,g), (h,h)$

**Step 3: DFS on $G^T$ in decreasing order of $f[v]$.**

Order of decreasing $f$: $a(16), b(15), e(14), f(13), g(12), h(11), d(10), c(9)$.

- **Start with `a`:** In $G^T$, $a$ has edge from $b$ only. $b$ is WHITE. Go to $b$. From $b$: edges from $c$ and $e$ (in $G^T$). Go to $c$. From $c$: edge from $d$ only. Go to $d$. From $d$: edge from $h$ only. Go to $h$. From $h$: edges from $g$ and $h$. $g$ is WHITE. Go to $g$. From $g$: edges from $f$ and $h$ (in $G^T$). $f$ is WHITE. Go to $f$. From $f$: edges from $e$ and $g$ (in $G^T$). Both already visited. Back up.
  - **Tree 1 DFS Roots Sequence:** $a \to b \to c \to d \to h \to g \to f$. **SCC₁ = {a, b, c, d, e, f, g}** (this is one giant SCC).

Wait — let us re-verify via cycle reachability in original $G$:
- $a \to b \to e \to a$: cycle involving $a, b, e$.
- $b \to f \to g \to f$: also $b \to f \to g \to h$.
- $c \to d \to c$: cycle involving $c, d$.
- Are these linked? $b \to c$ exists; $d \to h \to h$ (self-loop at $h$); $c \to g$.
- Actually, checking: $a \to b \to c$ and $c \to d \to c$ is a 2-cycle. From $d \to h$, but $h$ has only a self-loop, so $h$ alone forms its own SCC. $c \to g \to f \to g$ forms a 2-cycle. $b \to f$ connects $b$'s component with $f$'s component. So all of $a, b, c, d, e, f, g$ are reachable both ways $\Rightarrow$ one large SCC. And $h$ is a self-loop SCC.

**Correct SCC Partition:**
- **SCC₁ = {a, b, c, d, e, f, g}** (the big cycle)
- **SCC₂ = {h}** (self-loop)

**Component Graph (Condensation DAG):** One node for SCC₁, one for SCC₂, and an edge from SCC₁ to SCC₂ (since $d \to h$ existed in $G$).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Algorithm | Time | Space | Optimal? | Determinism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Yes (for BFS tree) | Yes (tie-breaking aside) |
| DFS (recursive) | $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ stack | Yes | Yes (tie-breaking aside) |
| Kosaraju's SCC | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Yes | Yes |
| Tarjan's SCC | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Yes | Yes |


**Interpretation:** Each horizontal "layer" represents vertices at the same BFS distance. BFS finishes all of Layer 1 before touching any Layer 2 vertex.

## 4.2 DFS Recursion Stack — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["Call DFS u Root"] --> nodeChild1["Call DFS v Child of u"]
    nodeChild1 --> nodeChild2["Call DFS w Grandchild"]
    nodeChild2 --> nodeChild3["Call DFS x Leaf"]
    nodeChild3 --> nodeRet3["Return x"]
    nodeChild2 --> nodeRet2["Return w"]
    nodeChild1 --> nodeRet1["Return v"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeRet0["Return u"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each downward edge is a recursive call (push to stack); each "Return" node is a stack pop and backtrack step.

---

## 4.3 SCC Decomposition Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Directed Graph G"]
        nodeIn1["V1"]
        nodeIn2["V2"]
        nodeIn3["V3"]
        nodeIn4["V4"]
        nodeIn5["V5"]
    end

    subgraph SCC1["SCC Set 1"]
        nodeSCC1A["V1"]
        nodeSCC1B["V2"]
        nodeSCC1C["V3"]
    end

    subgraph SCC2["SCC Set 2"]
        nodeSCC2A["V4"]
        nodeSCC2B["V5"]
    end

    subgraph COMPGRAPH["Component Graph Condensation DAG"]
        nodeCG1["Node C1"]
        nodeCG2["Node C2"]
    end

    nodeIn1 --> nodeSCC1A
    nodeIn2 --> nodeSCC1B
    nodeIn3 --> nodeSCC1C
    nodeIn4 --> nodeSCC2A
    nodeIn5 --> nodeSCC2B

    nodeSCC1A --> nodeCG1
    nodeSCC1B --> nodeCG1
    nodeSCC1C --> nodeCG1
    nodeSCC2A --> nodeCG2
    nodeSCC2B --> nodeCG2

    nodeCG1 --> nodeCG2
```

**Interpretation:** The directed graph is partitioned into strongly connected components. Each SCC becomes a **single supernode** in the component graph, which is always a **DAG** (acyclic).

## 4.4 BFS vs DFS — High-Level Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["Start Traversal at Source s"] --> nodeChoice{"Choose Strategy"}

    nodeChoice --> nodeBFS["BFS Use FIFO Queue"]
    nodeBFS --> nodeBFS1["Enqueue s Mark GRAY"]
    nodeBFS1 --> nodeBFS2["Dequeue u Process all WHITE neighbors"]
    nodeBFS2 --> nodeBFS3{"Queue Empty?"}
    nodeBFS3 --> nodeBFS4["Mark u BLACK Loop back to Dequeue"]
    nodeBFS4 --> nodeBFS2
    nodeBFS3 --> nodeEnd["End Traversal"]

    nodeChoice --> nodeDFS["DFS Use LIFO Stack or Recursion"]
    nodeDFS --> nodeDFS1["Visit s Mark GRAY"]
    nodeDFS1 --> nodeDFS2["Recurse on first WHITE neighbor"]
    nodeDFS2 --> nodeDFS3{"Any WHITE neighbor?"}
    nodeDFS3 --> nodeDFS4["Recurse further down the branch"]
    nodeDFS4 --> nodeDFS2
    nodeDFS3 --> nodeDFS5["Backtrack Mark BLACK Return to parent"]
    nodeDFS5 --> nodeEnd
```

**Interpretation:** The flowchart contrasts the **iterative queue-based** BFS loop with the **recursive depth-priority** DFS flow.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Breadth-First Search (BFS). Explain its working with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the **present depth** (distance from source) before moving to vertices at the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO Queue** to keep track of the frontier of unprocessed vertices.

**Working Principle (3 key steps):**
1. **Initialize:** Mark all vertices WHITE. Mark the source $s$ as GRAY, set $\text{dist}[s] = 0$, and enqueue $s$.
2. **Loop:** While the queue is non-empty, dequeue vertex $u$, scan all neighbors $v$ of $u$. For each WHITE $v$: mark it GRAY, set $\text{dist}[v] = \text{dist}[u] + 1$, set $\text{pred}[v] = u$, and enqueue $v$.
3. **Complete:** After processing all neighbors, mark $u$ as BLACK and continue until the queue is empty.

**Example (sketch):** For a graph with source $s = A$ and edges $A \to B, A \to C, B \to D, C \to D$, BFS visits $A \to B \to C \to D$. Distances: $d[A]=0, d[B]=1, d[C]=1, d[D]=2$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Strongly Connected Component (SCC)? Write the steps of Kosaraju's algorithm to find SCCs.**

**Model Answer:**
