| 1 | 1 | 10 | 0 | ✅ Yes ($0+10=10 \le 50$) | 1.00 | $1 \cdot 60 = 60$ | 60 | 10 |
| 2 | 2 | 20 | 10 | ✅ Yes ($10+20=30 \le 50$) | 1.00 | $1 \cdot 100 = 100$ | 160 | 30 |
| 3 | 3 | 30 | 30 | ❌ No ($30+30=60 > 50$) | — | — | — | — |

**Step 3b — Fractional take for Item 3:**

Remaining capacity:

$$
W_{\text{rem}} = W - \text{currentWeight} = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ kg}
$$

Fraction of Item 3 taken:

$$
x_3 = \frac{W_{\text{rem}}}{w_3} = \frac{20}{30} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

Value added by the fraction:

$$
\Delta V_3 = x_3 \cdot v_3 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 120 = 80
$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$
x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 1, \quad x_3 = \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
\text{Total Weight} = 1 \cdot 10 + 1 \cdot 20 + \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot 30 = 10 + 20 + 20 = 50 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Maximum Value } Z^* = 60 + 100 + 80 = \boxed{240}
$$

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification — Why is Greedy Optimal Here?

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Optimality of Greedy for Fractional Knapsack):** The greedy strategy of sorting by decreasing value density and filling the knapsack in that order produces a **globally optimal** solution.

**Proof Sketch (Exchange Argument):**

Let the items be sorted so that $\rho_1 \ge \rho_2 \ge \dots \ge \rho_n$.
Let $G$ be the greedy solution and $O$ be *any* optimal solution. We show $G$ is optimal.

* If $O$ already agrees with $G$ on every $x_i$, then $G = O$ and we are done.
* Otherwise, let $j$ be the **first index** where $G$ and $O$ differ.
  * Since $G$ picks the *full* amount of the highest-density items first, $x_j^G \ge x_j^O$.
  * This means $G$ has used **at least as much weight** of items $\le j$ as $O$ has.
  * The remaining capacity of $O$ is therefore filled with items of density $\le \rho_j$.
  * Replace in $O$ any portion of an item with density $< \rho_j$ by an equivalent weight of item $j$ (or fractions thereof). This swap **does not decrease** the value of $O$.
* Repeat the exchange step until $O$ matches $G$. Hence $Z(G) = Z(O) = Z^*$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Time Complexity Derivation (Exhaustive)

The Fractional Knapsack algorithm has three identifiable phases:

| Phase | Operation | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Compute densities | One pass over $n$ items | $O(n)$ |
| 2. Sort by density | Comparison-based sort (e.g., merge sort) | $O(n \log n)$ |
| 3. Greedy selection loop | Single linear pass | $O(n)$ |

Total runtime:

$$
T(n) = O(n) + O(n \log n) + O(n) = O(n \log n)
$$

The dominant term is the sort, so the asymptotic complexity is $O(n \log n)$. Space usage is $O(n)$ for the auxiliary density array and sort buffer.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fractional vs. 0/1 Knapsack

| Aspect | **Fractional Knapsack** | **0/1 Knapsack** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Allowed $x_i$ | $0 \le x_i \le 1$ (continuous) | $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (binary) |
| Strategy | **Greedy** by density | **Dynamic Programming** / Branch & Bound |
| Time Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(nW)$ pseudo-polynomial |
| Greedy-Choice Property | ✅ Satisfied | ❌ Violated (counterexample exists) |
| Optimal Substructure | ✅ Satisfied | ✅ Satisfied |
| Resulting Optimality | **Provably optimal** | **Provably optimal** (via DP) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow of the Generic Greedy Abstraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize solution := empty set]
    B --> C{i = 1 to n ?}
    C -- Yes --> D["x := Select(A) — pick best candidate"]
    D --> E["Feasible(x, solution) ?"]
    E -- "TRUE" --> F["solution := Union(solution, x)"]
    E -- "FALSE" --> G["Discard x — do not add to solution"]
    F --> H["A := A − x — remove from candidate pool"]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Increment i]
    I --> C
    C -- "No — all candidates processed" --> J[/Return final solution/]
    J --> K([End])

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow node `Select(A)` is where the **greedy choice** is made. The red node `Feasible?` is the **constraint check**. The green node `Union` is the **commitment step**. These three boxes are the *only* problem-specific components in the entire abstract template.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Fractional Knapsack Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        P1["Items: w1..wn"]
        P2["Values: v1..vn"]
        P3["Knapsack: Capacity W"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING PIPELINE"]
        Q1["DENSITY ENGINE: rho_i = v_i / w_i"]
        Q2["SORTER: Decreasing order of rho_i"]
        Q3["GREEDY PACKER: Sequential Feasibility Check"]
        Q4["SLICER: Fractional take for the last item"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        R1["Decision Vector x1..xn"]
        R2["Maximum Total Value Z*"]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q3
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 -- "fits fully" --> Q3
    Q3 -- "does not fit" --> Q4
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q3 --> R2
    Q4 --> R2

    style Q1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    style Q4 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#831843,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Greedy Decision Tree for the Worked Example ($W=50$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Start: W = 50, Items 1,2,3]) --> S1[Sort by density: rho1=6, rho2=5, rho3=4]
    S1 --> S2{Capacity left: 50}
    S2 --> S3[Take Item 1 fully: x1=1, weight=10, value=60]
    S3 --> S4{Capacity left: 40}
    S4 --> S5[Take Item 2 fully: x2=1, weight=20, value=100]
    S5 --> S6{Capacity left: 20}
    S6 -- "Item 3 weight 30 > 20" --> S7["Slice Item 3: x3 = 20/30 = 0.667"]
    S7 --> S8["Add value: 0.667 x 120 = 80"]
    S8 --> S9[Capacity left: 0 — STOP]
    S9 --> S10[/Total Z* = 60 + 100 + 80 = 240/]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern (PCCRT602):** Part A = short 3-mark questions; Part B = 14-mark long-answer with **internal choice** between two alternatives. The questions below mirror the exact board pattern.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q2a]`

**State and briefly explain the two key properties that an optimization problem must satisfy for the greedy method to yield an optimal solution.**

**Model Answer:**

The two essential properties are:

1. **Greedy-Choice Property:** It must be possible to construct a globally optimal solution by making a sequence of locally optimal (greedy) choices. In other words, the locally best move at each step can lead to a globally optimal result without needing to reconsider previous decisions.

2. **Optimal Substructure:** An optimal solution to the overall problem must contain within it an optimal solution to every sub-problem generated by removing one or more greedy choices. The sub-problems must be solvable independently and their optimal solutions must compose into the global optimum.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each property with brief explanation.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Write the control abstraction of the greedy method. What is the role of the function `Feasible()`?**

**Model Answer:**

```
Algorithm Greedy(a, n)
{
    solution := ∅;
    for i := 1 to n do
    {
        x := Select(a);
        if Feasible(x, solution) then
            solution := Union(solution, x);
        a := a − {x};
    }
    return solution;
}
```

**Role of `Feasible(x, solution)`:** It checks whether adding the candidate `x` to the partially built `solution` still satisfies **all the constraints** of the problem. It returns `true` if the augmented solution is permissible (e.g., total knapsack weight $\le W$), and `false` otherwise. This prevents the algorithm from committing to an infeasible solution. *(3 marks: 2 for the abstraction, 1 for the role of Feasible.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinee must attempt EITHER Question A OR Question B.** Each carries 14 marks split into 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q11a Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Solve the following instance of the Fractional Knapsack problem using the greedy method.**

* Knapsack Capacity $W = 60$
* Items:

| Item $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Value $v_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10 | 100 |
| 2 | 20 | 220 |
| 3 | 30 | 180 |
| 4 | 40 | 320 |

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Compute value densities** $\rho_i = v_i / w_i$.

$$
\rho_1 = 100/10 = 10.00, \quad \rho_2 = 220/20 = 11.00, \quad \rho_3 = 180/30 = 6.00, \quad \rho_4 = 320/40 = 8.00
$$

**Step 2: Sort in decreasing order of $\rho$.**

$$
\rho_2 = 11.0 \;\ge\; \rho_1 = 10.0 \;\ge\; \rho_4 = 8.0 \;\ge\; \rho_3 = 6.0
$$

**New sorted order: Item 2 → Item 1 → Item 4 → Item 3**

| Step | Item | $w_i$ | Used | Fits? | $x_i$ | Value Added | Cum. Value |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 | 20 | 0 | ✅ ($0+20=20 \le 60$) | 1.00 | 220 | 220 |
| 2 | 1 | 10 | 20 | ✅ ($20+10=30 \le 60$) | 1.00 | 100 | 320 |
| 3 | 4 | 40 | 30 | ❌ ($30+40=70 > 60$) | — | — | — |

**Step 3b — Slice Item 4:**

Remaining capacity: $W_{\text{rem}} = 60 - 30 = 30$ kg.

$$
x_4 = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75
$$

Value added: $0.75 \times 320 = 240$.

**Step 4: Final Result**

$$
x_2 = 1,\ x_1 = 1,\ x_4 = 0.75,\ x_3 = 0
$$

Total weight = $20 + 10 + 30 = 60$ kg ✅. Total value $Z^* = 220 + 100 + 240 = \mathbf{560}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derive the time complexity of the Fractional Knapsack greedy algorithm and explain why greedy fails for 0/1 Knapsack with a counterexample.**

**Model Solution:**

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

The algorithm has three stages:
* Density computation: $O(n)$.
* Sorting by density: $O(n \log n)$.
* Greedy scan + slicing: $O(n)$.

$$
T(n) = O(n) + O(n \log n) + O(n) = O(n \log n)
$$

**Why Greedy Fails for 0/1 Knapsack — Counterexample:**

Consider $W = 50$, two items:
* Item A: $w_A = 30$, $v_A = 40$ (density $1.33$)
* Item B: $w_B = 30$, $v_B = 40$ (density $1.33$, but B fits only if A is dropped)

A clearer counterexample with $W=10$:
* Item 1: $w=7$, $v=9$, density $=1.29$
* Item 2: $w=4$, $v=5$, density $=1.25$
* Item 3: $w=4$, $v=5$, density $=1.25$

Greedy (by density) takes Item 1 fully → weight 7, value 9. Remaining = 3 (Item 2 doesn't fit). Total = 9.

**Optimal 0/1 solution** = take Items 2 and 3 fully → weight 8, value 10. Better than greedy.

Thus the greedy-choice property is **violated** for 0/1 Knapsack because once you commit to whole items, you may end up with wasted slack. The continuous (fractional) version, by contrast, can always fill the slack with a fraction of a higher-density item, restoring optimality.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q8]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the control abstraction of the greedy method. How is it specialized to solve the Fractional Knapsack problem?**

**Model Solution:**

The **control abstraction** of the greedy method is a generic, problem-independent algorithmic template that uses three abstract functions — `Select`, `Feasible`, and `Union` — to iterate over candidates and incrementally build a feasible solution. The abstraction hides problem-specific details and exposes the common logical flow:

```
Greedy(a, n):
    solution := ∅
    for i := 1 to n:
        x := Select(a)
        if Feasible(x, solution):
            solution := Union(solution, x)
        a := a − {x}
    return solution
```

**Specialization to Fractional Knapsack:**

| Abstract Function | Knapsack Instantiation |
| :--- | :--- |
| `Select(a)` | Selects the item with the **highest value/weight ratio** $\rho_i = v_i / w_i$ from the unprocessed items. |
| `Feasible(x, solution)` | Returns `true` if the cumulative weight after adding item $x$ is $\le W$. |
| `Union(solution, x)` | Adds either the **whole** item (if it fits) or a **fraction** $x = (W-\text{used})/w_i$ that exactly fills the remaining capacity. |

A one-time preprocessing step — sorting items by decreasing $\rho$ — is added before the loop to make `Select` a simple $O(1)$ operation. This specialization produces the Fractional Knapsack algorithm with $O(n \log n)$ complexity.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given $W=15$, solve the following 0/1 and Fractional Knapsack problems and comment on the difference in results.**

| Item $i$ | $w_i$ | $v_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | 12 |
| 2 | 5 | 25 |
| 3 | 4 | 16 |
| 4 | 6 | 30 |

**Model Solution:**

**Densities:** $\rho_1 = 4.00$, $\rho_2 = 5.00$, $\rho_3 = 4.00$, $\rho_4 = 5.00$.

**Sorted order:** Item 2 ($\rho=5$) → Item 4 ($\rho=5$) → Item 1 ($\rho=4$) → Item 3 ($\rho=4$).

**Fractional Knapsack (Greedy — works here):**
* Take Item 2 (w=5, v=25). Used = 5.
* Take Item 4 (w=6, v=30). Used = 11.
* Take Item 1 fully (w=3, v=12). Used = 14.
* Remaining capacity = 1. Take fraction of Item 3: $x_3 = 1/4$, value = $0.25 \times 16 = 4$.
* **Total value = 25 + 30 + 12 + 4 = 71**, Total weight = 15 ✅.

**0/1 Knapsack (DP — optimal):**
We must enumerate subsets of size $\le W=15$:
* {2,4,1}: $w = 5+6+3 = 14$, $v = 25+30+12 = 67$ ✅ feasible.
* {2,4,3}: $w = 5+6+4 = 15$, $v = 25+30+16 = 71$ ✅ feasible.
* {2,1,3}: $w = 5+3+4 = 12$, $v = 25+12+16 = 53$.
* {4,1,3}: $w = 6+3+4 = 13$, $v = 30+12+16 = 58$.

**Optimal 0/1 solution:** Items {2, 4, 3}, value = **71**.

**Comment:** In this particular instance, both methods coincidentally yield 71. However, this is a special case — generally 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack give different answers, and the 0/1 version **cannot** be solved by a simple density-based greedy (as proven by counterexamples in Part A above).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> * ✘ Forgetting to **sort by density first** (most common: 2-mark penalty).
> * ✘ Using the **same greedy for 0/1 Knapsack** without justifying why it fails.
> * ✘ Not showing the **fractional calculation** explicitly ($x_i \cdot v_i$).
> * ✘ Omitting the **weight verification** that the knapsack is exactly full.
> * ✘ Conflating **Select() with Sort()** — sorting is a one-time preprocessing; `Select()` is a per-iteration call.
> * ✘ Writing the **control abstraction** with problem-specific variable names (it must be generic!).
> * ✘ Failing to state the **two key properties** (greedy-choice + optimal substructure) in definition questions.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Greedy** = locally optimal, irrevocable choices that *hopefully* lead to a global optimum.
- **Control abstraction** = generic template using `Select`, `Feasible`, `Union`; problem-specific code is plugged in.
- **Two mandatory correctness properties:** Greedy-Choice Property + Optimal Substructure.
- **Fractional Knapsack:** $x_i \in [0,1]$; sort by density $\rho_i = v_i/w_i$ in **decreasing** order; take full items, then **slice** the last item to fill remaining capacity.
- **Canonical Algorithm Steps:** Compute density → Sort (decreasing) → Scan items → Slice last if needed.
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ (dominated by sorting); becomes $O(n)$ if pre-sorted.
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathbf{O(n)}$.
- **Greedy fails for 0/1 Knapsack** because the discrete constraint ($x_i \in \{0,1\}$) violates the greedy-choice property — fractions of items cannot be split to fill the residual capacity efficiently.
- **Counterexample to remember:** $W=10$, items $(w=7, v=9)$, $(w=4, v=5)$, $(w=4, v=5)$ — Greedy gives 9; Optimal 0/1 gives 10.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $\rho_i = v_i/w_i$, $x_k = (W - \text{used})/w_k$, $Z^* = \sum v_i x_i$, $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.
- **Mnemonic:** **"Sort, Take, Slice"** — Sort by density → Take full items → Slice the last.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Cost Spanning Tree - Kruskal's and Prim's, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum Cost Spanning Tree - Kruskal's and Prim's, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spanning Tree — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Spanning Tree (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A *spanning tree* of a connected, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a subgraph that is a tree and includes **every vertex** of $G$. It is **acyclic**, **connected**, and contains exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.

A graph may have many different spanning trees. Each one is a *minimal* connected subgraph in the sense that removing any edge disconnects it.

## 1.2 Minimum Cost Spanning Tree (MST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum (Cost) Spanning Tree**
> Given a **connected, weighted, undirected** graph $G = (V, E, w)$, the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** is a spanning tree whose sum of edge weights is **minimum** among all possible spanning trees of $G$.
> $$\text{Cost}(T^\*) = \min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_{(u,v) \in T} w(u,v)$$
> where $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of all spanning trees of $G$.

**Key facts (board-exam favourites):**
- The MST is **not unique** when two or more edges share the same minimum weight in a cut.
- If all edge weights are **distinct**, the MST is **unique**.
- The MST always has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cable-Laying Problem

Imagine you are a **telecom engineer in Kerala** asked to connect **6 district offices** (A, B, C, D, E, F) with **optical-fibre cables**. Constraints:

1. Every office must be reachable from every other office (connectivity).
2. No loops — looping cables is wasteful (acyclic).
3. You have a limited budget — total cable length / cost must be **minimum**.

> You must pour concrete into a "tree-shaped" trench network that touches every building exactly once via the cheapest possible path. That cheapest trench network is your **MST**.

This is precisely the MST problem. Kruskal's and Prim's are the two classical, greedy algorithms that solve it.

## 1.4 Why a Greedy Strategy Works for MST

MST is one of the canonical problems where a **greedy choice** (locally optimal edge) is provably globally optimal. The correctness rests on two fundamental properties discussed in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Properties of MST

### 2.1.1 Cut Property (used by Prim's)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cut Property**
> For any **cut** $(S, V \setminus S)$ of $G$, the edge of **minimum weight** crossing the cut belongs to **every** MST of $G$.

**Why it works:** Suppose an MST $T$ does not contain the lightest crossing edge $e$. Adding $e$ to $T$ creates a cycle; some other crossing edge $f$ lies on this cycle. Replacing $f$ with $e$ keeps connectivity, removes the cycle, and does not increase cost (since $w(e) \le w(f)$).

### 2.1.2 Cycle Property (used by Kruskal's)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Property**
> For any **cycle** $C$ in $G$, the edge of **maximum weight** on $C$ does **not** belong to any MST of $G$ (provided it is the unique maximum).

**Why it works:** Removing the heaviest edge from a cycle keeps the graph connected but strictly reduces (or preserves) total weight, contradicting minimality if the edge were required.

### 2.1.3 Other Important Properties

- An MST has $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.
- For a complete graph $K_n$ with distinct weights, by **Cayley's formula**, the number of distinct spanning trees is $n^{n-2}$.
- The MST of $G$ is the same as the MST of any subgraph containing it, provided the relative order of edge weights is preserved.

## 2.2 The Generic-MST Framework

Both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms are instances of the following generic procedure:

```
GENERIC-MST(G, w)
  A ← ∅
  while A does not form a spanning tree
       do find an edge (u,v) that is safe for A
          A ← A ∪ {(u,v)}
  return A
```

An edge is **safe** if adding it to $A$ keeps $A$ a subset of *some* MST. The two algorithms differ **only** in how they choose a safe edge.

| Algorithm | Safe-Edge Strategy | Underlying Property | Data Structure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kruskal's** | Smallest-weight edge that does not create a cycle | Cycle Property | Disjoint-Set (Union-Find) |
| **Prim's** | Smallest-weight edge crossing the cut (Tree, $V \setminus$ Tree) | Cut Property | Min-Priority Queue (Binary Heap / Fibonacci Heap) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE — memorise the complexities, especially the dominant terms.

| Algorithm | Implementation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prim's** | Adjacency Matrix + Linear Search | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | **Dense** graphs ($\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2$) |
| **Prim's** | Binary Heap (Min-Heap) + Adj. List | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | General purpose |
| **Prim's** | Fibonacci Heap + Adj. List | $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Theoretical optimum |
| **Kruskal's** | Sort + Union-Find | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert E \vert) = O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | **Sparse** graphs ($\vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert$) |
| **Borůvka's** | Parallel-friendly | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Parallel / external memory |

**Key edge-count relation for a connected graph:**
$$\vert E \vert \ge \vert V \vert - 1$$

**For a complete graph:** $\vert E \vert = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$, hence $\log \vert E \vert \le 2 \log \vert V \vert$.

**Engineering Utility of MST:**
- **Network design:** LAN cabling, telecommunication backbones, electrical grids.
- **Approximation algorithms:** Travelling Salesman (tour at most $2 \cdot$ MST cost), Steiner Tree, clustering.
- **Image segmentation, handwriting recognition, road/river network analysis** in civil engineering and GIS.
- **Cluster analysis** in data mining and bioinformatics (single-linkage clustering).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

We will work out **both algorithms on a common graph** so you can cross-verify.

## 3.1 Reference Graph

Consider a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = 6$:

| Edge | $(A,B)$ | $(A,F)$ | $(B,C)$ | $(B,F)$ | $(C,D)$ | $(C,F)$ | $(D,E)$ | $(D,F)$ | $(E,F)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight** | 4 | 2 | 6 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 4 |

A pictoral representation:

```
        A —4— B
        |     |  \
        2     3   6
        |     |    \
        F —1— C —3— D
        |             |
        4             2
        |             |
        E ———————5———— D?    (D-E and D-F as given)
```

(Mermaid rendition is in Section 4.)

---

## 3.2 Kruskal's Algorithm — Exhaustive Walkthrough

### 3.2.1 Algorithm Steps (Logical Description)

Kruskal's algorithm is a **global, edge-centric** greedy approach.

1. **Sort** all edges of $G$ in **non-decreasing** order of weight.
2. Initialise a **Disjoint-Set (Union-Find)** for each vertex.
3. Initialise $A = \emptyset$ (the running MST).
4. For each edge $(u, v)$ in sorted order:
    - If **FIND$(u) \neq$ FIND$(v)$** (i.e., they are in different components):
        - Add $(u, v)$ to $A$.
        - **UNION** the sets containing $u$ and $v$.
    - Else: discard the edge (it would form a cycle).
5. Stop when $A$ contains $\vert V \vert - 1 = 5$ edges, or all edges processed.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 — Sort edges (ascending):**

| Order | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(C, F)$ | 1 |
| 2 | $(A, F)$ | 2 |
| 3 | $(D, E)$ | 2 |
| 4 | $(B, F)$ | 3 |
| 5 | $(C, D)$ | 3 |
| 6 | $(A, B)$ | 4 |
| 7 | $(E, F)$ | 4 |
| 8 | $(D, F)$ | 5 |
| 9 | $(B, C)$ | 6 |

**Step 2 — Initialise disjoint sets:** $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}, \{F\}$. $\;A_{\text{MST}} = \emptyset$.

| Step | Edge | Weight | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Decision | Sets After | MST Edges |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(C,F)$ | 1 | $\{C\}$ | $\{F\}$ | **Accept** | $\{C,F\}$ | $\{(C,F)\}$ |
| 2 | $(A,F)$ | 2 | $\{A\}$ | $\{C,F\}$ | **Accept** | $\{A,C,F\}$ | $\{(C,F),(A,F)\}$ |
| 3 | $(D,E)$ | 2 | $\{D\}$ | $\{E\}$ | **Accept** | $\{A,C,F\},\{D,E\}$ | + $(D,E)$ |
| 4 | $(B,F)$ | 3 | $\{B\}$ | $\{A,C,F\}$ | **Accept** | $\{A,B,C,F\},\{D,E\}$ | + $(B,F)$ |
| 5 | $(C,D)$ | 3 | $\{A,B,C,F\}$ | $\{D,E\}$ | **Accept** (merges) | $\{A,B,C,D,E,F\}$ | + $(C,D)$ |
| 6 | $(A,B)$ | 4 | same | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | — |
| 7 | $(E,F)$ | 4 | same | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | — |
| 8 | $(D,F)$ | 5 | same | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | — |
| 9 | $(B,C)$ | 6 | same | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | — |

**Step 3 — Stop:** $\vert A \vert = 5 = \vert V \vert - 1$. ✓

**MST found by Kruskal's:**
$$T_{\text{Kruskal}} = \{(C,F), (A,F), (D,E), (B,F), (C,D)\}$$
$$\text{Cost}(T_{\text{Kruskal}}) = 1 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 3 = 11$$

### 3.2.3 Time-Complexity Derivation of Kruskal's

Let $\vert V \vert = n$, $\vert E \vert = m$.

**Sorting the edges** (Step 1):
$$T_{\text{sort}} = O(m \log m)$$

**Union-Find operations** (Step 4): we perform at most $2m$ FINDs and at most $n - 1$ UNIONs. With **path compression** + **union by rank**:
$$T_{\text{union-find}} = O(m \cdot \alpha(n)) \approx O(m)$$
where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function, $\alpha(n) \le 4$ for any practical $n$.

**Total time:**
$$T(n,m) = O(m \log m) + O(m \cdot \alpha(n)) = O(m \log m)$$

Since $m \le n(n-1)/2$ for simple graphs, we have $\log m \le 2 \log n$, hence:
$$T(n,m) = O(m \log n)$$

---

## 3.3 Prim's Algorithm — Exhaustive Walkthrough

### 3.3.1 Algorithm Steps (Logical Description)

Prim's algorithm is a **local, vertex-centric** greedy approach (it grows a single tree).

1. Pick an **arbitrary root** $r$.
2. Maintain three arrays of size $n$:
    - `key[v]` — minimum weight of any edge connecting $v$ to the current tree.
    - `parent[v]` — the vertex that gives `key[v]`.
    - `inMST[v]` — boolean flag.
3. Initialise: `key[v] = ∞` for all $v$, `key[r] = 0`, `parent[v] = NIL`.
4. While there exists a vertex not yet in the MST:
    - Pick $u = \arg\min_{v \notin \text{MST}} \text{key}[v]$ (Extract-Min from a min-priority queue).
    - Add $u$ to the MST.
    - For each neighbour $v$ of $u$:
        - If $v \notin$ MST and $w(u,v) < \text{key}[v]$:
            - `key[v] = w(u,v)`
            - `parent[v] = u`
5. The MST edges are $\{( \text{parent}[v], v ) : v \ne r, v \in \text{MST}\}$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Trace (starting at $r = A$)

Initial: `key = [A:0, B:∞, C:∞, D:∞, E:∞, F:∞]`, `parent = [NIL, NIL, NIL, NIL, NIL, NIL]`, `inMST = [F,F,F,F,F,F]`.

| Iteration | Extract-Min $u$ | `key` Update & Reason | MST so far | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **A** (key=0) | Neighbours of A: B(4), F(2) → key[B]=4, parent[B]=A; key[F]=2, parent[F]=A | $\{(A,F)\}$ | 0+2 |
| 2 | **F** (key=2) | Neighbours: A ✓, B(3) < key[B]=4 → key[B]=3, parent[B]=F; C(1) → key[C]=1, parent[C]=F; D(5), E(4) | $\{(A,F),(F,C)\}$ | 0+2+1 |
| 3 | **C** (key=1) | Neighbours: B(6) > key[B]=3 ✗; D(3) → key[D]=3, parent[D]=C; F ✓ | + $(C,D)$ | +3 |
| 4 | **D** (key=3, tied with B) → pick D | Neighbours: C ✓; E(2) → key[E]=2, parent[E]=D; F(5) | + $(D,E)$ | +2 |
| 5 | **E** (key=2) | Neighbours: D ✓; F(4) > key[F]? No, key[F] is already "used" since F is in MST. So no change. | + $(E,\cdot)$ – E has no useful outgoing edge | 0 |
| 6 | **B** (key=3) | All neighbours of B in MST, no change. | + $(F,B)$ | +3 |

**MST found by Prim's:**
$$T_{\text{Prim}} = \{(A,F), (F,C), (C,D), (D,E), (F,B)\}$$
$$\text{Cost}(T_{\text{Prim}}) = 2 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 3 = 11 \;\;\checkmark$$

> Both algorithms produce **equivalent MSTs** with the same minimum cost of **11**, confirming the result.

### 3.3.3 Time-Complexity Derivation of Prim's

There are three principal implementations:

**(a) Adjacency Matrix + Linear Search:**
- For each of $n$ Extract-Mins, scan all $n$ vertices: $O(n^2)$.
- Update step: scan $n$ entries per vertex: $O(n^2)$.
- Total: $O(n^2)$. **Best for dense graphs.**

**(b) Binary Min-Heap + Adjacency List:**
- Initial heap build: $O(n)$.
- $n$ Extract-Mins: $O(n \log n)$.
- For each edge, at most one **Decrease-Key**: $m \cdot O(\log n) = O(m \log n)$.
- Total:
$$T(n,m) = O(n \log n) + O(m \log n) = O(m \log n) \;\;\text{(since } m \ge n-1\text{)}$$

**(c) Fibonacci Heap + Adjacency List:**
- $n$ Extract-Mins: $O(n \log n)$ amortised.
- $m$ Decrease-Keys: $O(m)$ amortised.
- Total:
$$T(n,m) = O(n \log n + m)$$
This is the **theoretically best** known MST complexity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to choose which algorithm — KTU board-exam favourite**

| Criterion | Kruskal's | Prim's |
|---|---|---|
| Greedy view | Edge-based (global) | Vertex-based (local) |
| Data structure | Union-Find | Min-Priority Queue |
| Initial action | Sort all edges | Pick any root vertex |
| Edge selection | Smallest edge not forming a cycle | Smallest edge connecting tree to outside |
| Better for | **Sparse** graphs ($m = O(n)$) | **Dense** graphs ($m = O(n^2)$) |
| Time | $O(m \log m) = O(m \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ (matrix) or $O(m \log n)$ (heap) |
| Space | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n^2)$ (matrix) or $O(n+m)$ (heap) |
| Easy to implement with | DSU | Heap |
| Produces same MST cost? | Yes | Yes |

## 3.5 Correctness Proofs (Sketch for KTU 14-mark answers)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect either a clear **proof sketch** or a clean **worked example** in 14-mark questions. Both earn full marks. The proofs below are concise enough to write in 4–5 lines.

### 3.5.1 Correctness of Kruskal's (by induction on $|A|$)

**Invariant:** At every step, $A$ is a subset of *some* MST.

**Base case:** $A = \emptyset$ trivially holds.

**Inductive step:** Assume $A$ is a subset of an MST $T^\*$. Consider the next edge $e = (u, v)$ chosen by Kruskal's. Since FIND$(u) \ne$ FIND$(v)$, $e$ does not create a cycle in $A$. If $e \in T^\*$, $A \cup \{e\}$ is a subset of $T^\*$. If $e \notin T^\*$, then $T^\* \cup \{e\}$ contains a cycle $C$. By the cycle property, $C$ contains an edge $f$ with $w(f) \ge w(e)$. Replacing $f$ by $e$ in $T^\*$ yields another MST $T^{\*\*}$ containing $A \cup \{e\}$. Hence the invariant is maintained. When $|A| = n-1$, $A$ is a spanning tree of minimum weight. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5.2 Correctness of Prim's (by induction on $|A|$)

**Invariant:** $A$ is always a subtree of *some* MST, and the cut $(A, V \setminus A)$ is respected.

**Base case:** $A$ contains only the root $r$.

**Inductive step:** Let $e = (u, v)$ be the lightest edge crossing the cut $(A, V \setminus A)$, where $u \in A$ and $v \notin A$. By the **cut property**, $e$ belongs to *every* MST. Adding $e$ to $A$ preserves the invariant. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Graph (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[A] ---|4| nodeB[B]
    nodeA[A] ---|2| nodeF[F]
    nodeB[B] ---|6| nodeC[C]
    nodeB[B] ---|3| nodeF[F]
    nodeC[C] ---|3| nodeD[D]
    nodeC[C] ---|1| nodeF[F]
    nodeD[D] ---|2| nodeE[E]
    nodeD[D] ---|5| nodeF[F]
    nodeE[E] ---|4| nodeF[F]
```

## 4.2 Kruskal's Algorithm — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK[Start] --> initK[Initialise Disjoint Sets and MST as empty]
    initK --> sortK[Sort all edges in non-decreasing order of weight]
    sortK --> pickK[Pick the smallest-weight unprocessed edge]
    pickK --> checkK{Endpoints in different components?}
    checkK -- Yes --> unionK[UNION the two components]
    unionK --> addK[Add edge to MST]
    addK --> sizeK{MST has V minus 1 edges?}
    checkK -- No --> skipK[Discard edge and skip]
    skipK --> moreK{More edges to process?}
    sizeK -- Yes --> doneK[Return MST]
    sizeK -- No --> moreK
    moreK -- Yes --> pickK
    moreK -- No --> doneK
```

## 4.3 Prim's Algorithm — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startP[Start] --> rootP[Pick arbitrary root vertex r]
    rootP --> initP[Initialise key values to infinity, key r equals 0]
    initP --> extractP[Extract vertex u with minimum key value]
    extractP --> addP[Add u to MST]
    addP --> scanP[For every neighbour v of u]
    scanP --> condP{v not in MST and w u v less than key v?}
    condP -- Yes --> updateP[Update key v to w u v and parent v to u]
    condP -- No --> nextP[Skip v]
    updateP --> moreP{All vertices added to MST?}
    nextP --> moreP
    moreP -- No --> extractP
    moreP -- Yes --> outputP[Output MST edges from parent array]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[A] ---|2| nodeF[F]
    nodeF[F] ---|1| nodeC[C]
    nodeF[F] ---|3| nodeB[B]
    nodeC[C] ---|3| nodeD[D]
    nodeD[D] ---|2| nodeE[E]
```

> MST Cost = 2 + 1 + 3 + 3 + 2 = **11**. Both algorithms reach this optimum.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q.** Define a *Minimum Spanning Tree*. State two properties of an MST.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a connected, weighted, undirected graph $G = (V, E, w)$ is a spanning tree whose total edge weight is minimum among all spanning trees of $G$.
$$\text{Cost}(T^\*) = \min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_{(u,v) \in T} w(u,v)$$

**Properties:**
1. It contains exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges and is acyclic.
2. **Cut Property:** The minimum-weight edge across any cut belongs to every MST.
3. **Cycle Property:** The maximum-weight edge on any cycle is excluded from every MST (any two of these suffice for 3 marks).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q.** Compare Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms with respect to time complexity and graph density.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kruskal's | Prim's |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| Best for | Sparse graphs | Dense graphs |
| Data structure | Union-Find | Min-Priority Queue |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> In 14-mark MST questions, students frequently lose marks for: **(i)** forgetting to draw the **initial graph** with edge weights, **(ii)** not showing the **sorted order** of edges (Kruskal's) or the **key/parent table** (Prim's), **(iii)** failing to **verify the cut/cycle property** in the explanation, and **(iv)** skipping the final **cost computation** as a check. Always present the data structure state (Union-Find sets or key/parent arrays) at every step — this is what examiners scan for.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Kruskal's + Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> Consider the graph shown below. **Find the MST using Kruskal's algorithm.** Show all intermediate steps. **Derive the time complexity** of the algorithm in terms of $\vert V \vert$ and $\vert E \vert$.

| Edge | $(1,2)$ | $(1,3)$ | $(1,4)$ | $(2,4)$ | $(2,5)$ | $(3,4)$ | $(3,6)$ | $(4,5)$ | $(4,6)$ | $(5,6)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight** | 4 | 1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 2 | 9 | 10 |

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** Find the MST using Kruskal's algorithm.

**Model Solution (Kruskal's Trace):**

**Step 1:** Sort edges ascending.

| Order | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(1,3)$ | 1 |
| 2 | $(4,5)$ | 2 |
| 3 | $(1,4)$ | 3 |
| 4 | $(1,2)$ | 4 |
| 5 | $(2,4)$ | 5 |
| 6 | $(3,4)$ | 6 |
| 7 | $(2,5)$ | 7 |
| 8 | $(3,6)$ | 8 |
| 9 | $(4,6)$ | 9 |
| 10 | $(5,6)$ | 10 |

**Step 2:** Initialise Disjoint Sets: $\{1\}, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{4\}, \{5\}, \{6\}$.

| Step | Edge | Weight | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Action | Sets After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(1,3)$ | 1 | $\{1\}$, $\{3\}$ | diff | Accept | $\{1,3\}$ |
| 2 | $(4,5)$ | 2 | $\{4\}$, $\{5\}$ | diff | Accept | $\{4,5\}$ |
| 3 | $(1,4)$ | 3 | $\{1,3\}$, $\{4,5\}$ | diff | Accept | $\{1,3,4,5\}$ |
| 4 | $(1,2)$ | 4 | $\{1,3,4,5\}$, $\{2\}$ | diff | Accept | $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ |
| 5 | $(2,4)$ | 5 | same set | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 6 | $(3,4)$ | 6 | same set | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 7 | $(2,5)$ | 7 | same set | same | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged |
| 8 | $(3,6)$ | 8 | $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$, $\{6\}$ | diff | Accept | $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ ✓ |

**MST found:** $\{(1,3), (4,5), (1,4), (1,2), (3,6)\}$
$$\text{Cost} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 8 = 18$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** Time-complexity derivation.

Kruskal's algorithm consists of:
- **Sorting** the $m$ edges: $O(m \log m)$ comparisons.
- **Union-Find operations:** at most $2m$ FINDs and $n-1$ UNIONs. With path compression + union by rank, each is $O(\alpha(n))$, giving $O(m \cdot \alpha(n)) \approx O(m)$.

Therefore:
$$T(n,m) = O(m \log m) + O(m) = O(m \log m)$$
For any graph, $m \le \binom{n}{2} = O(n^2)$, hence $\log m \le 2\log n$, so:
$$T(n,m) = O(m \log n)$$
