### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Prim's + Analysis*  *(Internal choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> Using the **same graph** as in Question A, find the MST with **Prim's algorithm**, starting at vertex **1**. Show the `key` and `parent` array evolution at every step. State and briefly prove the **cut property** that justifies Prim's greedy choice.

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** Apply Prim's algorithm starting at vertex 1.

**Model Solution (Prim's Trace):**

**Initialisation:**
- `key = [1:0, 2:∞, 3:∞, 4:∞, 5:∞, 6:∞]`
- `parent = [1:NIL, 2:NIL, 3:NIL, 4:NIL, 5:NIL, 6:NIL]`
- `inMST = [F, F, F, F, F, F]`

| Iter | Extract-Min $u$ | Updates Performed | `key` after iter | `parent` after iter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **1** (key=0) | key[2]=4, key[3]=1, key[4]=3 | $[0,4,1,3,∞,∞]$ | $[-,1,1,1,-,-]$ |
| 2 | **3** (key=1) | key[4]: min(3, 6) = 3 (no change); key[6]=8 | $[0,4,1,3,∞,8]$ | $[-,1,1,1,-,3]$ |
| 3 | **4** (key=3) | key[2]: min(4,5) = 4 (no change); key[5]=2; key[6]: min(8,9) = 8 (no change) | $[0,4,1,3,2,8]$ | $[-,1,1,1,4,3]$ |
| 4 | **5** (key=2) | key[6]: min(8,10) = 8 (no change) | $[0,4,1,3,2,8]$ | $[-,1,1,1,4,3]$ |
| 5 | **2** (key=4) | All neighbours in MST, no change | unchanged | unchanged |
| 6 | **6** (key=8) | All neighbours in MST, no change | unchanged | unchanged |

**MST edges** (from `parent` array): $(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (4,5), (3,6)$.
$$\text{Cost} = 4 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 8 = 18 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (matches Kruskal)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** State and prove the cut property.

**Statement (Cut Property):** Let $G = (V, E, w)$ be a connected, weighted, undirected graph. For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$, if $e^\* = (u, v)$ is the **minimum-weight edge** crossing the cut, then $e^\*$ belongs to **every** MST of $G$.

**Proof:**
Let $T$ be any MST. Consider two cases:

**Case 1:** $e^\* \in T$. Done — $e^\*$ is in an MST.

**Case 2:** $e^\* \notin T$. Add $e^\*$ to $T$. Since $T$ is a tree on $\vert V \vert$ vertices, adding $e^\*$ creates a unique cycle $C$. The cycle $C$ must contain some edge $f$ that crosses the cut (since both endpoints of $e^\*$ lie in different parts). Since $w(e^\*) \le w(f)$ (by minimality of $e^\*$), construct $T' = T \cup \{e^\*\} \setminus \{f\}$. Then $T'$ is still a spanning tree, and
$$w(T') = w(T) - w(f) + w(e^\*) \le w(T).$$
Since $T$ is a minimum spanning tree, $w(T') = w(T)$, so $T'$ is also an MST and contains $e^\*$. Thus $e^\*$ belongs to (at least one, hence the property generalises) MST. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MST (KTU Module 3)**

- **MST Definition:** A spanning tree of minimum total edge weight; has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges; always exists if $G$ is connected.
- **Two pivotal properties:** **Cut Property** (lightest crossing edge ⇒ in every MST) and **Cycle Property** (heaviest edge in a cycle ⇒ not in any MST).
- **Generic-MST skeleton:** all MST algorithms iterate *find a safe edge → add to A → repeat*.
- **Kruskal's algorithm:**
    - Edge-centric, **global** greedy choice.
    - **Sort** edges ascending → pick smallest edge that does **not** form a cycle (verified via **Union-Find**).
    - Time: $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert E \vert) = O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$.
    - Best for **sparse** graphs.
- **Prim's algorithm:**
    - Vertex-centric, **local** greedy choice.
    - Grows a **single tree** by repeatedly adding the **cheapest edge from the tree to a non-tree vertex**.
    - Uses **Min-Priority Queue** (or Fibonacci Heap for theoretical optimum).
    - Time: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ (matrix) or $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ (binary heap).
    - Best for **dense** graphs.
- **Union-Find** is the crucial auxiliary DS for Kruskal's: it must use **path compression + union by rank** to achieve near-constant amortised cost.
- **Comparison at a glance:** Both produce the **same minimum cost**; choice depends on **graph density** and **DS availability**.
- **Real-world applications:** Telecom / power-grid cabling, TSP approximation, single-linkage clustering, image segmentation, road network design, bioinformatics phylogeny.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers,** always present: **graph + sorted edge list (Kruskal's) / key-parent table (Prim's) + final MST + cost + complexity derivation**. A 2-line cut/cycle property sketch earns 2 bonus marks.
- **Memory aid:** "**K**ruskal = **K**utsort first" (sort edges by weight); "**P**rim = **P**ick nearest neighbour" (grow one cluster).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Problem - Dijkstra's Algorithm, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shortest Path Problem - Dijkstra's Algorithm, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shortest Path Problem (Single-Source):** Given a weighted, directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ that maps every edge to a non-negative real number, and a distinguished **source vertex** $s \in V$, the objective is to determine, for every vertex $v \in V$, the minimum total weight path from $s$ to $v$. The quantity $d[v]$ denotes the **shortest-path estimate** (the current best known distance from $s$ to $v$), while $\pi[v]$ denotes the **predecessor** of $v$ on the finalized shortest path.

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a **greedy algorithm** proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra (1956) that solves the single-source shortest path problem on a graph with non-negative edge weights. It maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose final shortest-path weights from the source have already been determined, and repeatedly selects the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance (using a min-priority queue).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Dijkstra's algorithm belongs to the **Greedy Paradigm** family of algorithms (along with Prim's MST, Kruskal's MST, and Huffman Coding). It is one of the highest-weightage topics in this module and frequently appears in ESE Part B questions (14 marks) and as a Part A conceptual question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart City Cab Navigator"

Imagine you are standing at **Point A** in a city, and you want to find the *cheapest* (shortest-time) route to **Points B, C, D, E, and F**. You do not know the road map in advance. Your strategy:

1. You mark Point A as your starting point with a known cost of **0**.
2. You inspect all immediate roads from your current position and calculate the cost to reach each neighbouring point. You keep only the **cheapest** known cost to each point.
3. From all the points you have *visited but not yet fully finalized*, you pick the one with the **cheapest current cost** (this is your greedy choice).
4. From that newly finalized point, you "relax" the costs of its unvisited neighbours — i.e., if going through this new point gives a cheaper total cost to some other point, you update your records.
5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 until every point is finalized.

This is **exactly how Dijkstra's algorithm works** — the city is the **graph**, the roads are the **edges**, the travel times are the **weights**, and your "cheapest known cost so far" is the **shortest-path estimate** $d[v]$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Dijkstra's greedy strategy works *only because* all edge weights are **non-negative**. If a road could have *negative* length (time-travel!), the greedy choice would fail because a previously finalized vertex could later get a shorter path through an unvisited one.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $V$ | Set of vertices (nodes) in the graph |
| $E$ | Set of edges (arcs) |
| $w(u,v)$ | Weight (cost) of edge from $u$ to $v$ |
| $d[v]$ | Shortest-path estimate from source $s$ to vertex $v$ |
| $\pi[v]$ | Predecessor of $v$ on the shortest path from $s$ |
| $S$ | Set of vertices whose shortest paths are finalized |
| $Q$ | Min-priority queue of vertices keyed by $d[\cdot]$ |
| $s$ | Source vertex |
| **Relaxation** | The core operation: $d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u,v))$ |

---

> *Section 1 establishes the formal definition, the greedy intuition via the cab-routing analogy, and the foundational vocabulary needed for the deep analysis that follows.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Dijkstra's Algorithm

Dijkstra's algorithm is built on three pillars: **Initialization**, **Selection**, and **Relaxation**.

### Step 1 — Initialization
- Assign $d[s] = 0$ (the distance from source to itself is zero).
- Assign $d[v] = \infty$ for every other vertex $v \neq s$.
- Assign $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$ for every vertex $v$ (no known predecessor).
- Build the min-priority queue $Q$ containing all vertices of $V$, keyed by $d[v]$.

### Step 2 — Greedy Selection (The Heart of the Algorithm)
- Repeatedly extract the vertex $u \in Q$ such that $d[u]$ is **minimum** (this is the Extract-Min operation).
- Add $u$ to the finalized set $S$.
- The justification: because all edge weights are non-negative, any alternative path to $u$ through another unvisited vertex would be at least as long.

### Step 3 — Edge Relaxation (The Update Step)
- For every edge $(u, v)$ leaving the extracted vertex $u$:
  - Compute an alternative distance: $\text{alt} = d[u] + w(u, v)$.
  - If $\text{alt} < d[v]$, then update:
    - $d[v] \leftarrow \text{alt}$
    - $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$ (record the better predecessor)
    - Perform a **Decrease-Key** operation on $v$ in $Q$.

### Step 4 — Termination
- The loop terminates when $Q$ becomes empty (every vertex has been finalized).
- The final $d[v]$ values represent the true shortest-path distances from $s$ to all $v \in V$.

## 2.2 The Relaxation Operation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Relaxation of an edge $(u,v)$** is the act of testing whether the current best known path from $s$ to $v$ can be improved by going through $u$ first, then traversing edge $(u,v)$.

$$
\text{RELAX}(u, v, w) : \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) \text{ then} \\
\quad d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v) \\
\quad \pi[v] \leftarrow u
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Notation / Constraints |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shortest-path weight (recursive) | $\delta(s, v) = \min\{w(p) : s \xrightarrow{p} v\}$ | $w(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ |
| Triangle inequality | $\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ | For any edge $(u,v)$ |
| Upper-bound property | $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ always | Invariant during execution |
| Convergence property | If $u$ is finalized, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ | After Extract-Min on $u$ |
| Path-relaxation property | Once $\delta(s,v)$ is found, it never changes | Holds for non-negative weights |
| Relaxation condition | $d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) \implies$ update | Strict inequality triggers update |
| **Time Complexity (Array)** | $O(V^2)$ | Suitable for dense graphs |
| **Time Complexity (Min-Heap)** | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | Suitable for sparse graphs |
| **Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap)** | $O(E + V \log V)$ | Asymptotically optimal |
| Space Complexity | $O(V)$ | For $d[\cdot]$, $\pi[\cdot]$, and $Q$ |
| Correctness precondition | $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for all edges | **Non-negativity is mandatory** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS Navigation Systems (Google Maps, Ola, Uber):** Find fastest route from current location to destination across a weighted road network.
- **Network Routing Protocols (OSPF — Open Shortest Path First):** Routers compute the least-cost path to every other router in an Autonomous System using Dijkstra's algorithm (specifically the **Link-State** routing protocol).
- **Telecommunications & IP Telephony:** Used in **SS7 (Signalling System No. 7)** networks to find minimum-delay call paths.
- **Social Networks & Recommendation Engines:** Finding the "minimum hops" or minimum-influence path between two users.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time path planning where vertices represent waypoints and weights represent traversal cost (energy, time, distance).
- **Game Development (AI Pathfinding):** Variant known as **A\* Search** uses Dijkstra with a heuristic function.

## 2.5 Why Greedy Works — The Proof Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Greedy Choice Property:** When vertex $u$ is extracted from $Q$ (i.e., has the minimum $d[u]$ among all unvisited vertices), $d[u]$ is already equal to the true shortest distance $\delta(s, u)$. This is because:
>
> 1. Suppose there exists a shorter path $P$ from $s$ to $u$ through some unvisited vertex $x$.
> 2. Let $y$ be the **first** unvisited vertex on $P$ that lies just after a visited vertex $z$.
> 3. The edge relaxation from $z$ to $y$ would have set $d[y] \leq d[z] + w(z,y) \leq d[u]$ (since weights are non-negative, the cumulative distance along $P$ is monotonically non-decreasing).
> 4. This contradicts $u$ being the minimum in $Q$.

---

> *Section 2 elaborates the algorithmic mechanics, the formal relaxation rule, the high-yield formula sheet, and the engineering relevance that KTU examiners love to test in "discuss with real-world examples" questions.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Pseudocode (KTU Standard Notation)

The pseudocode below is what KTU examiners expect in their answer sheets. Memorize this structure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: } \text{DIJKSTRA}(G, w, s) \\
&\textbf{Input: } \text{Weighted graph } G=(V,E), \text{ weight fn } w, \text{ source } s \\
&\textbf{Output: } d[v] \text{ and } \pi[v] \text{ for all } v \in V \\
\\
&1.\quad \text{INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE}(G, s) \\
&2.\quad S \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&3.\quad Q \leftarrow V[G] \quad \text{// build min-priority queue} \\
&4.\quad \textbf{while } Q \neq \emptyset \textbf{ do} \\
&5.\quad \quad u \leftarrow \text{EXTRACT-MIN}(Q) \\
&6.\quad \quad S \leftarrow S \cup \{u\} \\
&7.\quad \quad \textbf{for each } v \in \text{Adj}[u] \textbf{ do} \\
&8.\quad \quad \quad \text{RELAX}(u, v, w) \\
&9.\quad \quad \quad \textbf{if } d[v] \text{ was updated} \\
&10.\quad \quad \quad \textbf{then } \text{DECREASE-KEY}(Q, v, d[v])
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Full Step-by-Step Trace

Consider the following directed weighted graph with 5 vertices: $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$. Source vertex: $A$.

**Edges and Weights:**
- $(A, B) = 4$
- $(A, C) = 2$
- $(C, B) = 1$
- $(C, D) = 4$
- $(B, D) = 5$
- $(B, E) = 6$
- $(D, E) = 2$

### Initialization Step
$$
\begin{aligned}
d[A] &= 0, \quad d[B] = \infty, \quad d[C] = \infty, \quad d[D] = \infty, \quad d[E] = \infty \\
\pi[A] &= \pi[B] = \pi[C] = \pi[D] = \pi[E] = \text{NIL} \\
S &= \emptyset, \quad Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Iteration 1 — Extract Min from Q
- $u \leftarrow A$ (since $d[A] = 0$ is minimum).
- $S = \{A\}$.
- Relax edges from $A$:
  - $(A, B)$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty$ → $d[B] = 4, \pi[B] = A$.
  - $(A, C)$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 2 = 2 < \infty$ → $d[C] = 2, \pi[C] = A$.

**State after Iteration 1:**

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | In $S$? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | **0** | NIL | ✓ |
| B | 4 | A | ✗ |
| C | 2 | A | ✗ |
| D | $\infty$ | NIL | ✗ |
| E | $\infty$ | NIL | ✗ |

### Iteration 2 — Extract Min from Q
- $u \leftarrow C$ (since $d[C] = 2$ is minimum among $\{B,C,D,E\}$).
- $S = \{A, C\}$.
- Relax edges from $C$:
  - $(C, B)$: $\text{alt} = 2 + 1 = 3 < 4$ → $d[B] = 3, \pi[B] = C$.  ✓ **UPDATE**
  - $(C, D)$: $\text{alt} = 2 + 4 = 6 < \infty$ → $d[D] = 6, \pi[D] = C$.

**State after Iteration 2:**

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | In $S$? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 0 | NIL | ✓ |
| B | **3** | C | ✗ |
| C | 2 | A | ✓ |
| D | 6 | C | ✗ |
| E | $\infty$ | NIL | ✗ |

### Iteration 3 — Extract Min from Q
- $u \leftarrow B$ (since $d[B] = 3$ is minimum among $\{B,D,E\}$).
- $S = \{A, C, B\}$.
- Relax edges from $B$:
  - $(B, D)$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 5 = 8 > 6$ → **No update** (current $d[D] = 6$ is better).
  - $(B, E)$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 6 = 9 < \infty$ → $d[E] = 9, \pi[E] = B$.

**State after Iteration 3:**

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | In $S$? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 0 | NIL | ✓ |
| B | 3 | C | ✓ |
| C | 2 | A | ✓ |
| D | 6 | C | ✗ |
| E | 9 | B | ✗ |

### Iteration 4 — Extract Min from Q
- $u \leftarrow D$ (since $d[D] = 6$ is minimum among $\{D, E\}$).
- $S = \{A, C, B, D\}$.
- Relax edges from $D$:
  - $(D, E)$: $\text{alt} = 6 + 2 = 8 < 9$ → $d[E] = 8, \pi[E] = D$. ✓ **UPDATE**

**State after Iteration 4:**

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | In $S$? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 0 | NIL | ✓ |
| B | 3 | C | ✓ |
| C | 2 | A | ✓ |
| D | 6 | C | ✓ |
| E | **8** | D | ✗ |

### Iteration 5 — Extract Min from Q
- $u \leftarrow E$ (only vertex remaining).
- $S = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$.
- No outgoing edges from $E$ in this graph, so no relaxation occurs.
- $Q = \emptyset$, loop terminates.

### Final Result — Shortest Path Distances from A

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\delta(A, A) &= 0 \\
\delta(A, B) &= 3 \quad \text{(Path: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B\text{)} \\
\delta(A, C) &= 2 \quad \text{(Path: } A \rightarrow C\text{)} \\
\delta(A, D) &= 6 \quad \text{(Path: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D\text{)} \\
\delta(A, E) &= 8 \quad \text{(Path: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E\text{)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

## 3.3 Time Complexity Derivation (Array-Based Implementation)

When the priority queue $Q$ is implemented as a simple unordered array:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE} &: O(V) \\
\text{Each Extract-Min operation} &: O(V) \text{ (linear scan)} \\
\text{Total Extract-Min calls} &: V \text{ times} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Total Extract-Min cost} &: V \cdot O(V) = O(V^2) \\
\text{Each Relax operation} &: O(1) \text{ (constant time)} \\
\text{Total Relax calls} &: \leq E \text{ times (once per edge)} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Total Relax cost} &: O(E) \\
\hline
\therefore \text{Total Time Complexity} &: O(V^2 + E) = \boxed{O(V^2)} \text{ for connected graphs}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Time Complexity Derivation (Binary Min-Heap Implementation)

When $Q$ is implemented as a binary min-heap:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE} &: O(V) \text{ (heapify)} \\
\text{Each Extract-Min operation} &: O(\log V) \\
\text{Total Extract-Min cost} &: V \cdot O(\log V) = O(V \log V) \\
\text{Each Decrease-Key + Relax} &: O(\log V) \\
\text{Total Decrease-Key cost} &: \leq E \cdot O(\log V) = O(E \log V) \\
\hline
\therefore \text{Total Time Complexity} &: \boxed{O((V + E) \log V)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** For dense graphs where $E \approx V^2$, the array implementation $O(V^2)$ is asymptotically faster than the heap implementation $O(V^2 \log V)$. For sparse graphs where $E \approx V$, the heap implementation $O(V \log V)$ dominates. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Which implementation is preferred for sparse graphs and why?"* — answer: **Binary Min-Heap with $O((V+E)\log V)$**.

## 3.5 Space Complexity Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Array } d[\cdot] &: O(V) \\
\text{Array } \pi[\cdot] &: O(V) \\
\text{Priority Queue } Q &: O(V) \\
\text{Set } S &: O(V) \\
\hline
\therefore \text{Total Space Complexity} &: \boxed{O(V)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Correctness Conditions (KTU Favourite Question)

Dijkstra's algorithm is correct **if and only if** all edge weights are **non-negative**. Proof sketch:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Convergence Property):** Once a vertex $u$ is added to $S$ (i.e., extracted from $Q$), $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ and remains unchanged thereafter.
>
> **Proof by contradiction:** Assume $u$ is the first vertex added to $S$ for which $d[u] \neq \delta(s, u)$. Then there exists a shortest path $P: s \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow x \rightarrow y \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow u$ where $y$ is the first vertex on $P$ not in $S$. Since $x \in S$ and edge $(x,y)$ was relaxed, we have $d[y] = \delta(s,y)$. Since edge weights are non-negative, $\delta(s, y) \leq \delta(s, u) \leq d[u]$. But $u$ was extracted as the minimum from $Q$, so $d[u] \leq d[y]$. Combining: $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. **Contradiction resolved.**

---

> *Section 3 delivers the exhaustive step-by-step trace on a 5-vertex graph, the full time and space complexity derivations for both array and heap implementations, and the formal correctness theorem — all the high-mark-yielding components KTU examiners reward.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Dijkstra's Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: DIJKSTRA G w s]) --> init[Initialize d s to 0, d v to infinity for all v not s, pi v to NIL]
    init --> buildQ[Build min priority queue Q from V]
    buildQ --> loopCheck{Q is empty?}
    loopCheck -- No --> extract[Extract Min u from Q]
    extract --> addS[Add u to finalized set S]
    addS --> getAdj[For each neighbor v in Adj u]
    getAdj --> relaxCheck{d v greater than d u plus w u v?}
    relaxCheck -- Yes --> updateD[Update d v to d u plus w u v]
    updateD --> updatePi[Update predecessor pi v to u]
    updatePi --> decKey[Perform Decrease Key on v in Q]
    decKey --> nextNeighbor{More neighbors of u?}
    relaxCheck -- No --> nextNeighbor
    nextNeighbor -- Yes --> getAdj
    nextNeighbor -- No --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- Yes --> output[Output d array and pi array]
    output --> stop([End: Shortest paths finalized])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architectural Flow of Inputs and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        graphG[Weighted Graph G with V vertices and E edges]
        weightFn[Weight Function w mapping edges to non negative reals]
        sourceNode[Source Vertex s]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[Dijkstra Engine Core]
        initUnit[Initialization Unit: d and pi arrays, queue Q]
        extractUnit[Extract Min Unit: selects vertex u with minimum d u]
        relaxUnit[Relaxation Unit: tests and updates d v and pi v]
        finalizeUnit[Finalization Unit: adds u to set S]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE[Data Structures]
        dArray[d array of size V: shortest path estimates]
        piArray[pi array of size V: predecessor pointers]
        queueQ[Min Priority Queue Q keyed by d]
        setS[Finalized Vertex Set S]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        distances[Shortest Distances delta s v for all v in V]
        spt[Shortest Path Tree SPT: trace via pi pointers]
    end

    graphG --> initUnit
    weightFn --> initUnit
    sourceNode --> initUnit
    initUnit --> dArray
    initUnit --> piArray
    initUnit --> queueQ
    extractUnit --> queueQ
    extractUnit --> finalizeUnit
    finalizeUnit --> setS
    relaxUnit --> dArray
    relaxUnit --> piArray
    relaxUnit --> queueQ
    dArray --> distances
    piArray --> spt
    setS --> distances
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph Visualization — Worked Example (From Section 3.2)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A)) -- 4 --- nodeB((B))
    nodeA -- 2 --- nodeC((C))
    nodeC -- 1 --- nodeB
    nodeC -- 4 --- nodeD((D))
    nodeB -- 5 --- nodeD
    nodeB -- 6 --- nodeE((E))
    nodeD -- 2 --- nodeE
```

**Shortest Path Tree (SPT) extracted from the example:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    sptA((A: 0)) -- 2 --> sptC((C: 2))
    sptC -- 1 --> sptB((B: 3))
    sptC -- 4 --> sptD((D: 6))
    sptD -- 2 --> sptE((E: 8))
```

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The SPT contains exactly $V - 1 = 4$ edges (for a 5-vertex graph) and forms a directed tree rooted at the source $A$. Every vertex has a unique predecessor, allowing full path reconstruction by backtracking through the $\pi$ array.

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Block — Dijkstra vs. Bellman-Ford vs. Floyd-Warshall

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPARISON[Algorithm Comparison Landscape]
        dijk[Dijkstra: Single Source, Non Negative Weights, Greedy, O V plus E log V with heap]
        bellman[Bellman Ford: Single Source, Handles Negative Weights, Dynamic Programming, O V times E]
        floyd[Floyd Warshall: All Pairs Shortest Path, Handles Negative Weights, DP, O V cubed]
    end

    dijk -.->|Faster but restricted| bellman
    bellman -.->|More versatile but slower| floyd
    dijk -.->|Specialized single source| floyd

    style dijk fill:#90EE90
    style bellman fill:#FFD700
    style floyd fill:#87CEEB
```

---

> *Section 4 provides the visual schematics — algorithmic flow, architectural block diagram, the worked-example graph, the resulting Shortest Path Tree, and a comparative landscape of related shortest-path algorithms that examiners frequently request.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Single-Source Shortest Path Problem. State the necessary condition for Dijkstra's algorithm to produce correct results.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) Problem** is defined as follows: given a weighted directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a non-negative weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and a distinguished source vertex $s \in V$, the objective is to find, for every vertex $v \in V$, the minimum-weight path $P$ from $s$ to $v$, where the weight of path $P$ is $w(P) = \sum_{(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in P} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$.

The **necessary condition for correctness of Dijkstra's algorithm** is that **all edge weights must be non-negative**, i.e., $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for every edge $(u, v) \in E$. If negative-weight edges exist, the greedy Extract-Min step may finalize a vertex prematurely, and later relaxations through negative edges may yield a shorter path — violating the convergence property.

*(Examiner's note: A clear statement of both parts with the formula for path weight earns full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: What is edge relaxation in the context of Dijkstra's algorithm? Write the relaxation step.**

**Model Answer:**

**Edge relaxation** is the core update operation in Dijkstra's algorithm. When processing an edge $(u, v)$ from a finalized vertex $u$ to an unvisited vertex $v$, the algorithm tests whether routing the path to $v$ through $u$ yields a shorter total distance than the current estimate $d[v]$.

**Relaxation Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RELAX}(u, v, w) : \quad & \textbf{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \\
& \textbf{then } d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u, v) \\
& \textbf{and } \pi[v] \leftarrow u
\end{aligned}
$$

If the new path through $u$ is strictly shorter, both the distance estimate and the predecessor pointer are updated. This is the only mechanism by which $d[v]$ values decrease during execution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**Q(a)** Trace Dijkstra's algorithm on the following weighted directed graph. Take vertex **1** as the source. Show the state of $d[\cdot]$, $\pi[\cdot]$, and set $S$ after each iteration. **(7 Marks)**

**Edges:**
- $(1,2)=10$, $(1,4)=5$
- $(4,2)=3$, $(4,3)=9$, $(4,6)=2$
- $(2,3)=1$, $(2,6)=6$
- $(3,6)=4$, $(6,5)=7$, $(6,8)=8$
- $(5,8)=2$, $(3,5)=5$

**Q(b)** Explain the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm when the priority queue is implemented using (i) an unordered array, and (ii) a binary min-heap. State which is preferred for sparse graphs and why. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Q(a) Step-by-Step Trace (7 Marks):**

**Initialization:**
- $d[1]=0$, all others $=\infty$
- $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$ for all $v$
- $S=\emptyset$, $Q=\{1,2,3,4,5,6,8\}$

**Iteration 1:** Extract $u=1$ (min $d=0$). $S=\{1\}$.
- Relax $(1,2)$: $\text{alt}=10<\infty \Rightarrow d[2]=10, \pi[2]=1$
- Relax $(1,4)$: $\text{alt}=5<\infty \Rightarrow d[4]=5, \pi[4]=1$

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d$ | **0** | 10 | $\infty$ | 5 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | NIL | 1 | NIL | 1 | NIL | NIL | NIL |

**Iteration 2:** Extract $u=4$ (min $d=5$). $S=\{1,4\}$.
- Relax $(4,2)$: $\text{alt}=5+3=8<10 \Rightarrow d[2]=8, \pi[2]=4$ ✓
- Relax $(4,3)$: $\text{alt}=5+9=14<\infty \Rightarrow d[3]=14, \pi[3]=4$
- Relax $(4,6)$: $\text{alt}=5+2=7<\infty \Rightarrow d[6]=7, \pi[6]=4$

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d$ | 0 | 8 | 14 | **5** | $\infty$ | 7 | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | NIL | 4 | 4 | NIL | NIL | 4 | NIL |

**Iteration 3:** Extract $u=6$ (min $d=7$). $S=\{1,4,6\}$.
- Relax $(6,5)$: $\text{alt}=7+7=14<\infty \Rightarrow d[5]=14, \pi[5]=6$
- Relax $(6,8)$: $\text{alt}=7+8=15<\infty \Rightarrow d[8]=15, \pi[8]=6$

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d$ | 0 | 8 | 14 | 5 | 14 | **7** | 15 |
| $\pi$ | NIL | 4 | 4 | NIL | 6 | NIL | 6 |

**Iteration 4:** Extract $u=2$ (min $d=8$). $S=\{1,4,6,2\}$.
- Relax $(2,3)$: $\text{alt}=8+1=9<14 \Rightarrow d[3]=9, \pi[3]=2$ ✓
- Relax $(2,6)$: $\text{alt}=8+6=14>7 \Rightarrow$ no update

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $d$ | 0 | **8** | 9 | 5 | 14 | 7 | 15 |
| $\pi$ | NIL | NIL | 2 | NIL | 6 | NIL | 6 |

**Iteration 5:** Extract $u=3$ (min $d=9$). $S=\{1,4,6,2,3\}$.
- Relax $(3,5)$: $\text{alt}=9+5=14 \not<14 \Rightarrow$ no update
- Relax $(3,6)$: $\text{alt}=9+4=13>7 \Rightarrow$ no update

**Iteration 6:** Extract $u=5$ (min $d=14$). $S=\{1,4,6,2,3,5\}$.
- Relax $(5,8)$: $\text{alt}=14+2=16>15 \Rightarrow$ no update

**Iteration 7:** Extract $u=8$ (min $d=15$). $S=\{1,4,6,2,3,5,8\}$. $Q=\emptyset$, stop.

**Final Shortest Distances from vertex 1:**

| Vertex | $\delta(1,v)$ | Path |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0 | — |
| 2 | 8 | $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2$ |
| 3 | 9 | $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ |
| 4 | 5 | $1 \rightarrow 4$ |
| 5 | 14 | $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 5$ |
| 6 | 7 | $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6$ |
| 8 | 15 | $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 8$ |

**Q(b) Time Complexity Analysis (7 Marks):**

**(i) Unordered Array Implementation:**
- Each **Extract-Min** operation performs a linear scan through all remaining vertices in $Q$ to find the minimum $d$-value. Cost: $O(V)$.
- There are exactly $V$ Extract-Min operations in total.
- Total Extract-Min cost: $V \times O(V) = O(V^2)$.
- Each **Relax/Decrease-Key** operation is $O(1)$ with an array (just an assignment to $d[v]$ and $\pi[v]$).
- Total Relax cost: $O(E)$.
- **Overall: $T(n) = O(V^2 + E) = O(V^2)$** for a connected graph.

**(ii) Binary Min-Heap Implementation:**
- Each **Extract-Min** operation costs $O(\log V)$ (heap-pop with re-heapify).
- Total Extract-Min cost: $V \times O(\log V) = O(V \log V)$.
- Each **Decrease-Key** operation (triggered after a successful relaxation) also costs $O(\log V)$ (heap-bubble-up).
- There are at most $E$ successful relaxations, so total Decrease-Key cost: $O(E \log V)$.
- **Overall: $T(n) = O(V \log V + E \log V) = O((V+E) \log V)$**.

**Preference for Sparse Graphs:**
For sparse graphs, $E \approx O(V)$ (e.g., $E = cV$ for small constant $c$). The heap complexity becomes $O(V \log V)$, which is asymptotically **faster** than the array's $O(V^2)$. Hence the **binary min-heap implementation is preferred for sparse graphs**, while the unordered array is preferred for dense graphs where $E \approx V^2$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q(a)** State and explain Dijkstra's algorithm. Prove the correctness of the algorithm (Convergence Property). **(7 Marks)**

**Q(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following graph with vertex **S** as source and find the shortest distances. Show all iterations clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Edges:**
- $(S,A)=1$, $(S,B)=4$
- $(A,B)=2$, $(A,C)=5$
- $(B,C)=1$, $(C,T)=3$, $(A,T)=10$
- $(B,T)=6$

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Q(a) Algorithm Statement and Correctness Proof (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm Statement:**
Dijkstra's algorithm solves the single-source shortest path problem on a weighted directed graph $G=(V,E)$ with non-negative edge weights. It maintains a set $S$ of finalized vertices, an array $d[v]$ of shortest-path estimates, and a min-priority queue $Q$. It repeatedly extracts the vertex with minimum $d$-value, adds it to $S$, and relaxes all outgoing edges.

**Convergence Property Proof:**

> **Claim:** Once a vertex $u$ is extracted from $Q$ and added to $S$, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ permanently.

**Proof (by contradiction):**
Assume $u$ is the **first** vertex for which $d[u] \neq \delta(s, u)$ when added to $S$. Since $d[u] \geq \delta(s,u)$ (upper-bound property), we have $d[u] > \delta(s,u)$.

Consider the true shortest path $P: s \rightarrow x \rightarrow y \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow u$. Let $y$ be the **first** vertex on $P$ that is not in $S$ at the moment $u$ is extracted, and let $x$ be its immediate predecessor on $P$ (so $x \in S$).

When $x$ was added to $S$, the edge $(x,y)$ was relaxed, giving:
$$
d[y] = \delta(s, y)
$$
(by the path-relaxation property).

Since all edge weights are non-negative and $y$ lies on the shortest path from $s$ to $u$:
$$
\delta(s, y) \leq \delta(s, u) < d[u]
$$
But $u$ was chosen as the minimum in $Q$, so $d[u] \leq d[y]$. This yields $\delta(s, y) \leq \delta(s, u) < d[u] \leq d[y]$, which forces $\delta(s, y) = d[u]$. Contradiction. Hence $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ when $u$ enters $S$. ∎

**Q(b) Worked Trace (7 Marks):**

**Initialization:** $d[S]=0$, all others $=\infty$; $Q=\{S,A,B,C,T\}$

**Iteration 1:** Extract $u=S$. $S=\{S\}$.
- $(S,A)$: $0+1=1 < \infty \Rightarrow d[A]=1, \pi[A]=S$
- $(S,B)$: $0+4=4 < \infty \Rightarrow d[B]=4, \pi[B]=S$

**Iteration 2:** Extract $u=A$ (min $d=1$). $S=\{S,A\}$.
- $(A,B)$: $1+2=3 < 4 \Rightarrow d[B]=3, \pi[B]=A$ ✓
- $(A,C)$: $1+5=6 < \infty \Rightarrow d[C]=6, \pi[C]=A$
- $(A,T)$: $1+10=11 < \infty \Rightarrow d[T]=11, \pi[T]=A$

**Iteration 3:** Extract $u=B$ (min $d=3$). $S=\{S,A,B\}$.
- $(B,C)$: $3+1=4 < 6 \Rightarrow d[C]=4, \pi[C]=B$ ✓
- $(B,T)$: $3+6=9 < 11 \Rightarrow d[T]=9, \pi[T]=B$ ✓

**Iteration 4:** Extract $u=C$ (min $d=4$). $S=\{S,A,B,C\}$.
- $(C,T)$: $4+3=7 < 9 \Rightarrow d[T]=7, \pi[T]=C$ ✓

**Iteration 5:** Extract $u=T$ (min $d=7$). $S=\{S,A,B,C,T\}$. $Q=\emptyset$, stop.

**Final Results:**

| Vertex | $\delta(S,v)$ | Shortest Path |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S | 0 | — |
| A | 1 | $S \rightarrow A$ |
| B | 3 | $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B$ |
| C | 4 | $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ |
| T | 7 | $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow T$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show the Iteration table** — KTU examiners award marks for the *state after each iteration* (the table with $d[\cdot]$, $\pi[\cdot]$, and $S$). A single final answer with no intermediate steps earns at most 2-3 out of 7 marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong predecessor ($\pi$) updates** — When two paths have equal length, either predecessor is acceptable, but you must show the $\pi$ value at every step. Forgetting $\pi$ entirely costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 3. **Applying Dijkstra on graphs with negative edges** — If the question has a negative edge, Dijkstra **will give wrong answers**. KTU frequently includes this as a trick. State the non-negativity precondition explicitly.
>
> 4. **Confusing Dijkstra with Prim's algorithm** — Both are greedy and use priority queues, but Dijkstra minimizes **path weight from source** (accumulates), while Prim's minimizes **edge weight to tree** (does not accumulate). This is the #1 conceptual confusion.
>
> 5. **Time complexity mis-statement** — Writing only $O(V^2)$ without specifying array vs. heap implementation loses 1 mark. Always state: *"With adjacency matrix and array: $O(V^2)$; with binary heap: $O((V+E)\log V)$."*
>
> 6. **Not drawing the Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** — When the question says "trace and show the shortest path tree," forgetting the final SPT diagram costs 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Dijkstra's Algorithm** solves the **Single-Source Shortest Path Problem** for graphs with **non-negative edge weights**.
- [x] It is a **Greedy Algorithm** that uses a **Min-Priority Queue** to repeatedly extract the vertex with the smallest tentative distance.
- [x] Three core operations: **INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE**, **EXTRACT-MIN**, and **RELAX**.
- [x] **Relaxation formula:** $d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) \Rightarrow d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$, $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$.
- [x] **Time Complexity:**
  - Array implementation → $O(V^2)$ (best for dense graphs).
  - Binary min-heap → $O((V+E)\log V)$ (best for sparse graphs).
  - Fibonacci heap → $O(E + V\log V)$ (asymptotically optimal).
- [x] **Space Complexity:** $O(V)$.
- [x] **Correctness requires** $w(u,v) \geq 0$ for all edges. Negative weights → use **Bellman-Ford** instead.
- [x] **Convergence Property:** Once a vertex is extracted from $Q$, its $d$-value is final and equals $\delta(s, u)$.
- [x] **Path Reconstruction:** Backtrack from target $v$ through $\pi[v]$ until reaching source $s$.
- [x] **Shortest Path Tree (SPT):** A directed tree rooted at $s$ with $V-1$ edges, formed by the $\pi$ pointers.
- [x] **Real-world uses:** GPS navigation, OSPF routing, game AI (A\* is a generalization), telecom signalling.
- [x] **Comparison:** Dijkstra vs. Bellman-Ford vs. Floyd-Warshall — single-source vs. single-source (negative ok) vs. all-pairs.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** "Apply Dijkstra on a graph with a negative edge" → answer should state that Dijkstra is **not applicable** and suggest Bellman-Ford.

---

> *Section 5 completes the KTU-aligned assessment toolkit — Part A definitions, two complete Part B 14-mark model papers with internal choice, the proven step-by-step worked traces, the examiner's pitfalls warning, and the rapid-revision recap.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Programming - Control Abstraction, Optimality Principle, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Programming - Control Abstraction, Optimality Principle, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dynamic Programming — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Programming (DP)** is an algorithmic design paradigm that solves complex optimization problems by breaking them down into simpler overlapping subproblems, storing the solution of each subproblem in a table, and reusing these stored solutions to avoid recomputation.

The technique was formalized by **Richard Bellman** in the 1950s while working on mathematical optimization at RAND Corporation. Despite the name, "Dynamic Programming" has nothing to do with computer programming in the modern sense; Bellman coined the term to disguise the mathematical nature of his research from government sponsors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Saved Recipes" Kitchen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are a chef who frequently cooks a menu of **100 dishes**. Many dishes share sub-recipes (e.g., a base sauce, a dough). A naive chef re-prepares the base sauce every single time — wasteful. A *dynamic chef* prepares the base sauce **once**, stores it in the fridge (a **table**), and reuses it whenever needed. This is exactly what DP does — it **saves computed answers in a lookup table** and reuses them instead of recomputing.

The two essential preconditions for applying DP to a problem are:
1. **Optimal Substructure** — the optimal solution to a problem contains within it the optimal solutions of its subproblems.
2. **Overlapping Subproblems** — the recursive solution repeatedly solves the same subproblems (unlike Divide & Conquer, where subproblems are disjoint).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** DP is **not** a free-for-all technique. Applying it to a problem that lacks *overlapping subproblems* (e.g., Merge Sort, Quick Sort) is incorrect and wasteful. Apply DP **only** when both preconditions hold.

### 1.3 Two Implementation Strategies

| Strategy | Order of Computation | Data Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-Down (Memoization)** | Natural recursive order; lookup first | Hash-map or table `memo[]` |
| **Bottom-Up (Tabulation)** | Iterative from smallest subproblem to largest | 1-D, 2-D, or n-D array |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners typically prefer the **bottom-up tabulation** approach because it has predictable Θ(·) space, no recursion-stack overhead, and is easier to analyze in the exam hall.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bellman's Principle of Optimality (The Optimality Principle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Optimality (Bellman, 1957):** *Whatever the first decision in a multistage optimization problem, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with respect to the state resulting from the first decision.*

In simpler terms — **no matter what choice you make first, you must still solve the rest of the problem optimally from the resulting state.** The first choice cannot be compensated for by a sub-optimal second choice.

**Logical Consequence:** If the principle holds for a problem, then the problem is a candidate for DP. The recursive structure can be written as:

$$OPT(P) = \min_{choice \in Choices(P)} \Big[ \ Cost(choice) \ + \ OPT(\ State \ after \ choice)\Big]$$

### 2.2 Why Some Problems *Violate* the Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A problem violates the optimality principle when the **first decision affects the set of feasible decisions later**, making the subproblems *not independent* of the first choice. **Example:** Finding the *longest simple path* in a graph violates it — using an edge early may block a longer future route.

### 2.3 Control Abstraction for Dynamic Programming

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Abstraction** is a generic algorithmic template that captures the structural steps common to *every* DP problem. It hides the problem-specific cost function behind a uniform interface so the same skeleton can be reused.

The standard control abstraction proceeds in **four canonical stages**:

1. **Characterize the structure of an optimal solution.**
2. **Recursively define the value of an optimal solution** (write the recurrence).
3. **Compute the value bottom-up** (tabulation) and **store the split/choice information** in a parallel table `s[][]`.
4. **Construct the actual optimal solution** by backtracking through `s[][]` (optional — only if the answer is required, not just its cost).

**Generic Control Abstraction Pseudocode (the "DP Skeleton"):**

```
ALGORITHM DP_Control_Abstraction(n)
    // n = number of items or size of problem instance
    for i ← 1 to n
        OPT[i] ← -∞               // initialize memo table
    OPT[0] ← 0                     // base case

    for length ← 1 to n           // chain length
        for i ← 1 to (n - length + 1)
            j ← i + length - 1
            OPT[i, j] ← ∞
            for k ← i to (j - 1)  // candidate split
                q ← OPT[i, k] + OPT[k+1, j] + cost(i, k, j)
                if q < OPT[i, j]
                    OPT[i, j] ← q
                    SPT[i, j] ← k // store split point
    return OPT[1, n], SPT

ALGORITHM Print_Optimal(S, i, j)
    if i = j
        print "A" + i
    else
        print "("
        Print_Optimal(S, i, S[i, j])
        Print_Optimal(S, S[i, j]+1, j)
        print ")"
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Matrix Chain Multiplication

| Symbol / Quantity | Meaning | Formula / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $p[i]$ | Row count of $A_i$ and column count of $A_{i-1}$ | Given: $A_i$ is $p[i-1] \times p[i]$ |
| $m[i, j]$ | Minimum cost to multiply $A_i \cdot A_{i+1} \cdots A_j$ | — |
| $s[i, j]$ | Split index that achieves the minimum in $m[i,j]$ | — |
| **Base case** | Single matrix needs no multiplication | $m[i, i] = 0$ for all $i = 1, \dots, n$ |
| **General recurrence** | Try every split $k$ between $A_i \cdots A_j$ | $m[i, j] = \min\limits_{i \le k < j} \big( m[i, k] + m[k+1, j] + p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j] \big)$ |
| **Cost of a split at $k$** | Cost of left sub-chain + cost of right sub-chain + final merge | $C_k = p[i-1]\,p[k] + p[k]\,p[j]$ matrices ⇒ $p[i-1]\cdot p[k] \cdot p[j]$ scalar multiplications |
| **Final answer** | Cost of multiplying the entire chain | $m[1, n]$ |
| **Time complexity** | $\Theta(n^3)$ — three nested loops of length $n$ | — |
| **Space complexity** | $\Theta(n^2)$ for the $m$ and $s$ tables | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cost term $p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j]$ comes from multiplying a $(p[i-1] \times p[k])$ matrix with a $(p[k] \times p[j])$ matrix, which needs **one scalar multiply per entry** ⇒ total $p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j]$ scalar multiplications.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Matrix chain multiplication is **not just a textbook exercise**. Real applications:
- **Compiler code generation:** reordering nested matrix expressions to minimize FLOPS.
- **Graphics pipelines** (OpenGL, DirectX): chained 4×4 transformation matrices.
- **Deep learning frameworks** (TensorFlow, PyTorch): fusing and reordering matrix multiplications on GPUs.
- **Scientific computing** (BLAS, LAPACK): blocked matrix operations.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

### 3.1 Worked Example — Matrix Chain Multiplication

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** Compute the minimum number of scalar multiplications to multiply the chain $A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4$ where
> $A_1$ is $5 \times 2$, $A_2$ is $2 \times 3$, $A_3$ is $3 \times 4$, $A_4$ is $4 \times 6$.
> Hence, the dimension vector is $p = \langle 5, 2, 3, 4, 6 \rangle$ and $n = 4$.

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Initialize the $m$ and $s$ Tables

| Sub-chain length | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| 1 | 0 | — | — | — |
| 2 | — | 0 | — | — |
| 3 | — | — | 0 | — |
| 4 | — | — | — | 0 |

All diagonal entries $m[i, i] = 0$ because a single matrix needs zero multiplications.

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Fill Sub-chains of Length 2 (compute $m[i, i+1]$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$)

**Compute $m[1, 2]$** — multiply $A_1 \cdot A_2$, only split $k = 1$:

$$m[1, 2] \;=\; m[1, 1] + m[2, 2] + p[0]\cdot p[1]\cdot p[2] \;=\; 0 + 0 + 5 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \;=\; \mathbf{30}, \quad s[1, 2] = 1$$

**Compute $m[2, 3]$** — split $k = 2$:

$$m[2, 3] \;=\; m[2, 2] + m[3, 3] + p[1]\cdot p[2]\cdot p[3] \;=\; 0 + 0 + 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \;=\; \mathbf{24}, \quad s[2, 3] = 2$$

**Compute $m[3, 4]$** — split $k = 3$:

$$m[3, 4] \;=\; m[3, 3] + m[4, 4] + p[2]\cdot p[3]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 0 + 0 + 3 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \;=\; \mathbf{72}, \quad s[3, 4] = 3$$

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | **30** | — | — |
| 2 | — | 0 | **24** | — |
| 3 | — | — | 0 | **72** |
| 4 | — | — | — | 0 |

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Fill Sub-chains of Length 3 (compute $m[i, i+2]$ for $i = 1, 2$)

**Compute $m[1, 3]$** — try splits $k = 1$ and $k = 2$:

$$\text{For } k=1: \quad m[1,1] + m[2,3] + p[0]\cdot p[1]\cdot p[3] \;=\; 0 + 24 + 5 \cdot 2 \cdot 4 \;=\; 24 + 40 \;=\; 64$$

$$\text{For } k=2: \quad m[1,2] + m[3,3] + p[0]\cdot p[2]\cdot p[3] \;=\; 30 + 0 + 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \;=\; 30 + 60 \;=\; 90$$

Take the minimum:

$$m[1, 3] = \min(64, 90) = \mathbf{64}, \quad s[1, 3] = 1$$

**Compute $m[2, 4]$** — try splits $k = 2$ and $k = 3$:

$$\text{For } k=2: \quad m[2,2] + m[3,4] + p[1]\cdot p[2]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 0 + 72 + 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 6 \;=\; 72 + 36 \;=\; 108$$

$$\text{For } k=3: \quad m[2,3] + m[4,4] + p[1]\cdot p[3]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 24 + 0 + 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \;=\; 24 + 48 \;=\; 72$$

Take the minimum:

$$m[2, 4] = \min(108, 72) = \mathbf{72}, \quad s[2, 4] = 3$$

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Fill Sub-chains of Length 4 (compute $m[1, 4]$)

**Compute $m[1, 4]$** — try splits $k = 1$, $k = 2$, and $k = 3$:

$$\text{For } k=1: \quad m[1,1] + m[2,4] + p[0]\cdot p[1]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 0 + 72 + 5 \cdot 2 \cdot 6 \;=\; 72 + 60 \;=\; 132$$

$$\text{For } k=2: \quad m[1,2] + m[3,4] + p[0]\cdot p[2]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 30 + 72 + 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 6 \;=\; 30 + 72 + 90 \;=\; 192$$

$$\text{For } k=3: \quad m[1,3] + m[4,4] + p[0]\cdot p[3]\cdot p[4] \;=\; 64 + 0 + 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 \;=\; 64 + 120 \;=\; 184$$

Take the minimum:

$$m[1, 4] = \min(132, 192, 184) = \mathbf{132}, \quad s[1, 4] = 1$$

---

### 3.6 Step 5 — The Completed Tables

**Cost table $m[i, j]$:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 30 | 64 | **132** |
| **2** | — | 0 | 24 | 72 |
| **3** | — | — | 0 | 72 |
| **4** | — | — | — | 0 |

**Split table $s[i, j]$:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | — | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| **2** | — | — | 2 | 3 |
| **3** | — | — | — | 3 |
| **4** | — | — | — | — |

---

### 3.7 Step 6 — Backtrack to Find the Optimal Parenthesization

We use the recursive procedure `Print_Optimal(s, 1, 4)`:

- $i = 1, j = 4$, $s[1, 4] = 1$ ⇒ print `(`, solve left = `(1, 1)`, solve right = `(2, 4)`, print `)`.
  - Left: $i = 1, j = 1$ ⇒ print `A1`.
  - Right: $i = 2, j = 4$, $s[2, 4] = 3$ ⇒ print `(`, solve left = `(2, 3)`, solve right = `(4, 4)`, print `)`.
    - Left of right: $i = 2, j = 3$, $s[2, 3] = 2$ ⇒ print `(`, solve left = `(2, 2)`, solve right = `(3, 3)`, print `)`.
      - Print `A2`, print `A3`.
    - Right of right: Print `A4`.

**Final optimal parenthesization:**

$$\boxed{\big(A_1\big)\,\big(\,(A_2\,A_3)\,A_4\,\big)} \quad\text{with minimum cost } \mathbf{132 \text{ scalar multiplications}}$$

---

### 3.8 Cross-Verification (Enumeration of all 5 parenthesizations)

| # | Parenthesization | Scalar Muls | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $((A_1A_2)A_3)A_4$ | $30+90+360 = 480$ | |
| 2 | $(A_1(A_2A_3))A_4$ | $24+40+120 = 184$ | |
| 3 | $(A_1A_2)(A_3A_4)$ | $30+72+90 = 192$ | |
| 4 | $A_1((A_2A_3)A_4)$ | $24+48+60 = 132$ | ✓ OPTIMAL |
| 5 | $A_1(A_2(A_3A_4))$ | $72+48+60 = 180$ | |

The DP result **132** is confirmed by exhaustive enumeration.

---

### 3.9 Detailed Time and Space Complexity Analysis

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

$$T(n) \;=\; \sum_{\text{length} = 2}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n-\text{length}+1} (j - i)$$

Each cell $m[i, j]$ where $j - i = \text{length} - 1$ requires $(j - i) = (\text{length} - 1)$ iterations of the innermost loop (one per split). Therefore:

$$T(n) \;=\; \sum_{\text{length} = 2}^{n} (n - \text{length} + 1) \cdot (\text{length} - 1)$$

Let $L = \text{length} - 1$. Then $L$ runs from $1$ to $n - 1$:

$$T(n) \;=\; \sum_{L = 1}^{n-1} (n - L) \cdot L \;=\; n \sum_{L=1}^{n-1} L \;-\; \sum_{L=1}^{n-1} L^2$$

Using the standard identities $\sum_{L=1}^{n-1} L = \frac{(n-1)n}{2}$ and $\sum_{L=1}^{n-1} L^2 = \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6}$:

$$T(n) \;=\; n \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \;-\; \frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6} \;=\; \frac{n^2(n-1)}{2} \;-\; \frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6}$$

Factor out $\frac{n(n-1)}{6}$:

$$T(n) \;=\; \frac{n(n-1)}{6}\,\Big[\,3n - (2n-1)\,\Big] \;=\; \frac{n(n-1)(n+1)}{6} \;=\; \frac{n^3 - n}{6}$$

Asymptotically:

$$\boxed{\,T(n) \;=\; \Theta(n^3)\,}$$

**Space Complexity:** Two tables $m[1 \dots n, 1 \dots n]$ and $s[1 \dots n, 1 \dots n]$, hence $\Theta(n^2)$ space. The backtracking recursion uses $\Theta(n)$ stack space (or can be unrolled iteratively).

> [!TIP]
> **Variants worth knowing for KTU viva:**
> - **O(n²) memory + O(n³) time** — the standard DP.
> - **Hu–Shing algorithm** (1979) achieves **O(n²) memory + O(n log n) time** but is **not in KTU syllabus**.
> - **Knuth's optimization** (1971) can reduce to **O(n²)** time when the quadrangle inequality holds — also **not in KTU syllabus**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DP Control Abstraction — Block-Level Architecture Flow

This block diagram captures the four canonical stages of any DP solution (matrix chain multiplication used as a working example).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: Matrices A1..An with dimension vector p] --> B[Stage 1: Characterize Optimal Substructure]
    B --> C[Identify optimal subproblem metric m i j = min cost to multiply Ai..Aj]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Recurrence Definition]
    D --> E["m i j = min over k of m i k + m k+1 j + p i-1 x p k x p j"]
    E --> F[Stage 3: Bottom-Up Tabulation]
    F --> G[For length = 2 to n: fill diagonal m table]
    G --> H[For length = 2 to n: fill split s table]
    H --> I[Final cost stored in m 1 n]
    I --> J[Stage 4: Backtracking via s table]
    J --> K[Recursive Print Optimal: traverse s 1 n to s 1 j to yield parenthesization]
    K --> L[Output: Minimum cost m 1 n and optimal parenthesization]
```

### 4.2 Bellman's Optimality Principle — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Multistage Decision Problem P] --> D1[Stage 1: Decision x1]
    D1 --> S[State Transition: S0 to S1]
    S --> D2[Stage 2: Decision x2]
    D2 --> S2[State Transition: S1 to S2]
    S2 --> D3[Stage 3 onwards: Continue optimally from new state]
    D3 --> OPT[OPTIMALITY HOLDS if subsequent decisions from S1 yield optimal sub-policy]
    OPT --> DP[APPLICABLE: Use Dynamic Programming]
```
