## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
**State Bellman's Principle of Optimality. List any two problems that can be solved using Dynamic Programming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Principle of Optimality (Bellman):** An optimal solution to a problem contains within it optimal solutions to all of its subproblems. Equivalently, *whatever the first decision in a multistage decision process, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with respect to the state resulting from the first decision.*
>
> **(Any two of the following, 1½ marks each):**
> 1. Matrix Chain Multiplication
> 2. 0/1 Knapsack Problem
> 3. Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
> 4. Optimal Binary Search Tree
> 5. All-Pairs Shortest Path (Floyd–Warshall)
> 6. Travelling Salesman Problem
> 7. Optimal Merge Pattern

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Differentiate between Dynamic Programming and Divide-and-Conquer. Mention the two key properties a problem must satisfy to be amenable to DP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Dynamic Programming | Divide-and-Conquer |
|---|---|---|
| Subproblems | **Overlapping** (re-used) | **Disjoint** (independent) |
| Approach | Stores subproblem solutions (memoization) | No storage; recomputes |
| Efficiency | Polynomial (after memoization) | Recursive cost = sum of subproblem costs |
| Example | Matrix Chain Multiplication, LCS | Merge Sort, Binary Search, Quick Sort |

> **Two key properties (1½ marks each):**
> 1. **Optimal Substructure** — the optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems.
> 2. **Overlapping Subproblems** — the recursion revisits the same subproblem many times.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the control abstraction (general algorithmic template) for Dynamic Programming. Explain each step clearly. Illustrate how it applies to Matrix Chain Multiplication.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Characterize the structure of an optimal solution.**

A subproblem is denoted by the indices $i, j$ meaning "compute the minimum cost to multiply $A_i \cdot A_{i+1} \cdots A_j$." An optimal solution has optimal substructure: whichever split point $k$ we choose for the *final* multiplication, the two sub-chains $A_i \cdots A_k$ and $A_{k+1} \cdots A_j$ must themselves be multiplied optimally.

**Step 2: Recursively define the value of an optimal solution.**

Let $m[i, j]$ be the minimum number of scalar multiplications needed to compute $A_i \cdots A_j$ (with $1 \le i \le j \le n$).

$$m[i, j] \;=\; \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \\[6pt] \displaystyle\min_{i \le k < j}\Big[\,m[i, k] \;+\; m[k+1, j] \;+\; p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j]\,\Big] & \text{if } i < j \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Compute the value bottom-up (tabulation).** Fill the table by chain length $\ell = 2, 3, \dots, n$:

```
for length = 2 to n
    for i = 1 to n - length + 1
        j = i + length - 1
        m[i, j] = ∞
        for k = i to j - 1
            q = m[i, k] + m[k+1, j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]
            if q < m[i, j]
                m[i, j] = q
                s[i, j] = k
```

**Step 4: Construct the optimal solution.** Backtrack through $s[i, j]$ to print the parenthesization.

**Application to Matrix Chain Multiplication:** Here the "stage" is the choice of split index $k$; the "state" is the pair $(i, j)$ denoting the sub-chain $A_i \cdots A_j$; the "cost" is the number of scalar multiplications $p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j]$; the "optimal value" is $m[i, j]$; the "split point" $s[i, j]$ is stored for backtracking.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the matrix chain $A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5$ with dimensions $A_1: 4 \times 2, A_2: 2 \times 3, A_3: 3 \times 2, A_4: 2 \times 6, A_5: 6 \times 3$. Using Dynamic Programming, compute $m[i, j]$ and $s[i, j]$ tables, and determine the **optimal parenthesization** along with the minimum number of scalar multiplications.

**Model Solution:**

The dimension vector is $p = \langle 4, 2, 3, 2, 6, 3 \rangle$, $n = 5$.

**Length 1:** All $m[i, i] = 0$.

**Length 2:**

$$m[1,2] = p[0]\cdot p[1]\cdot p[2] = 4\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 24, \quad s[1,2] = 1$$
$$m[2,3] = p[1]\cdot p[2]\cdot p[3] = 2\cdot 3\cdot 2 = 12, \quad s[2,3] = 2$$
$$m[3,4] = p[2]\cdot p[3]\cdot p[4] = 3\cdot 2\cdot 6 = 36, \quad s[3,4] = 3$$
$$m[4,5] = p[3]\cdot p[4]\cdot p[5] = 2\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 36, \quad s[4,5] = 4$$

**Length 3:**

$$m[1,3]:\quad k=1 \Rightarrow 0+12+4\cdot 2\cdot 2 = 12+16 = 28; \quad k=2 \Rightarrow 24+0+4\cdot 3\cdot 2 = 24+24 = 48$$
$$m[1,3] = 28, \quad s[1,3] = 1$$

$$m[2,4]:\quad k=2 \Rightarrow 0+36+2\cdot 3\cdot 6 = 36+36 = 72; \quad k=3 \Rightarrow 12+0+2\cdot 2\cdot 6 = 12+24 = 36$$
$$m[2,4] = 36, \quad s[2,4] = 3$$

$$m[3,5]:\quad k=3 \Rightarrow 0+36+3\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 36+18 = 54; \quad k=4 \Rightarrow 36+0+3\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 36+54 = 90$$
$$m[3,5] = 54, \quad s[3,5] = 3$$

**Length 4:**

$$m[1,4]:\quad k=1 \Rightarrow 0+36+4\cdot 2\cdot 6 = 36+48 = 84;\quad k=2 \Rightarrow 24+36+4\cdot 3\cdot 6 = 24+36+72 = 132$$
$$k=3 \Rightarrow 28+0+4\cdot 2\cdot 6 = 28+48 = 76$$
$$m[1,4] = 76, \quad s[1,4] = 3$$

$$m[2,5]:\quad k=2 \Rightarrow 0+54+2\cdot 3\cdot 3 = 54+18 = 72;\quad k=3 \Rightarrow 12+36+2\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 12+36+12 = 60$$
$$k=4 \Rightarrow 36+0+2\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 36+36 = 72$$
$$m[2,5] = 60, \quad s[2,5] = 3$$

**Length 5:**

$$m[1,5]:\quad k=1 \Rightarrow 0+60+4\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 60+24 = 84$$
$$k=2 \Rightarrow 24+54+4\cdot 3\cdot 3 = 24+54+36 = 114$$
$$k=3 \Rightarrow 28+36+4\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 28+36+24 = 88$$
$$k=4 \Rightarrow 76+0+4\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 76+72 = 148$$
$$m[1,5] = 84, \quad s[1,5] = 1$$

**Final $m$ table:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 24 | 28 | 76 | **84** |
| **2** | — | 0 | 12 | 36 | 60 |
| **3** | — | — | 0 | 36 | 54 |
| **4** | — | — | — | 0 | 36 |
| **5** | — | — | — | — | 0 |

**Final $s$ table:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | — | 1 | 1 | 3 | **1** |
| **2** | — | — | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| **3** | — | — | — | 3 | 3 |
| **4** | — | — | — | — | 4 |
| **5** | — | — | — | — | — |

**Backtrack $s[1, 5] = 1$** ⇒ split into $(1, 1)$ and $(2, 5)$:
- $(1, 1)$ ⇒ $A_1$.
- $(2, 5)$: $s[2, 5] = 3$ ⇒ split into $(2, 3)$ and $(4, 5)$:
  - $(2, 3)$: $s[2, 3] = 2$ ⇒ $A_2 A_3$.
  - $(4, 5)$: $s[4, 5] = 4$ ⇒ $A_4 A_5$.

**Optimal Parenthesization:**

$$\boxed{\,A_1 \,\big(\,(\,A_2 A_3\,)\,(\,A_4 A_5\,)\,\big)\,} \quad \text{with minimum cost } \mathbf{84 \text{ scalar multiplications}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four-step methodology to solve any Dynamic Programming problem. State clearly how the Principle of Optimality is verified for the **All-Pairs Shortest Path** problem (Floyd–Warshall algorithm).

**Model Solution:**

**Four-step DP methodology (4 marks):**

1. **Characterize the structure of an optimal solution** — identify the subproblem and show that an optimal solution can be constructed from optimal solutions of subproblems.
2. **Recursively define the value of an optimal solution** — write a recurrence that expresses $OPT[i, j]$ in terms of smaller subproblems.
3. **Compute the optimal value bottom-up** — fill the table iteratively (tabulation) from smallest subproblem to largest; optionally store a "choice" or "split" matrix.
4. **Construct the optimal solution from stored information** — backtrack through the choice matrix to print the solution (sequence of moves, parenthesization, path, etc.).

**Verification of optimality for Floyd–Warshall (3 marks):**

Let $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ be the length of the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using **intermediate vertices drawn from $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ only**.

**Claim:** A shortest path $i \leadsto j$ with intermediate vertices in $\{1, \dots, k\}$ either:
- (a) Does **not** pass through vertex $k$ as an intermediate vertex. Then it is also a shortest path using only $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$ as intermediates. Length = $D^{(k-1)}[i, j]$.
- (b) **Does** pass through vertex $k$. Then it is a shortest path from $i$ to $k$ followed by a shortest path from $k$ to $j$, both using intermediates from $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$. Length = $D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j]$.

This satisfies the **principle of optimality** because *whichever first edge (or, in this characterization, whichever decision about whether $k$ is an intermediate) we make, the remaining subpath must itself be optimal.* Recurrence:

$$D^{(k)}[i, j] \;=\; \min\Big(\,D^{(k-1)}[i, j]\,,\; D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j]\,\Big), \quad D^{(0)}[i, j] = w(i, j)$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the Matrix Chain Multiplication DP algorithm on the chain $A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4$ where $A_1: 10 \times 5$, $A_2: 5 \times 20$, $A_3: 20 \times 10$, $A_4: 10 \times 15$. Show both $m$ and $s$ tables and state the **time complexity** of the algorithm.

**Model Solution:**

Dimension vector: $p = \langle 10, 5, 20, 10, 15 \rangle$, $n = 4$.

**Length 2 (cells $m[1,2], m[2,3], m[3,4]$):**

$$m[1,2] = 10\cdot 5\cdot 20 = 1000, \quad s[1,2] = 1$$
$$m[2,3] = 5\cdot 20\cdot 10 = 1000, \quad s[2,3] = 2$$
$$m[3,4] = 20\cdot 10\cdot 15 = 3000, \quad s[3,4] = 3$$

**Length 3 (cells $m[1,3], m[2,4]$):**

$$m[1,3]:\quad k=1 \Rightarrow 0+1000+10\cdot 5\cdot 10 = 1000+500 = 1500;\quad k=2 \Rightarrow 1000+0+10\cdot 20\cdot 10 = 1000+2000 = 3000$$
$$m[1,3] = 1500, \quad s[1,3] = 1$$

$$m[2,4]:\quad k=2 \Rightarrow 0+3000+5\cdot 20\cdot 15 = 3000+1500 = 4500;\quad k=3 \Rightarrow 1000+0+5\cdot 10\cdot 15 = 1000+750 = 1750$$
$$m[2,4] = 1750, \quad s[2,4] = 3$$

**Length 4 (cell $m[1,4]$):**

$$m[1,4]:\quad k=1 \Rightarrow 0+1750+10\cdot 5\cdot 15 = 1750+750 = 2500$$
$$k=2 \Rightarrow 1000+3000+10\cdot 20\cdot 15 = 1000+3000+3000 = 7000$$
$$k=3 \Rightarrow 1500+0+10\cdot 10\cdot 15 = 1500+1500 = 3000$$
$$m[1,4] = 2500, \quad s[1,4] = 1$$

**Final $m$ table:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 0 | 1000 | 1500 | **2500** |
| **2** | — | 0 | 1000 | 1750 |
| **3** | — | — | 0 | 3000 |
| **4** | — | — | — | 0 |

**Final $s$ table:**

| $i \backslash j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | — | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| **2** | — | — | 2 | 3 |
| **3** | — | — | — | 3 |
| **4** | — | — | — | — |

**Backtrack:** $s[1, 4] = 1 \Rightarrow (A_1)(\text{chain } A_2 A_3 A_4)$. Then $s[2, 4] = 3 \Rightarrow (A_2 A_3)(A_4)$.

**Optimal parenthesization:**

$$\boxed{\,A_1 \,(\,(A_2 A_3)\,A_4\,)\,} \quad \text{with minimum cost } \mathbf{2500 \text{ scalar multiplications}}$$

**Time Complexity Analysis (1 mark):**

The three nested loops — outer (chain length) $O(n)$, middle (start index $i$) $O(n)$, inner (split index $k$) $O(n)$ — give:

$$T(n) = \sum_{L=2}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n-L+1} (L-1) = \sum_{L=1}^{n-1}(n-L)\cdot L = \frac{n^3 - n}{6} = \Theta(n^3)$$

Space complexity: $\Theta(n^2)$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Dynamic Programming questions:**
>
> 1. **Off-by-one in the dimension vector** — the matrix $A_i$ has dimensions $p[i-1] \times p[i]$. Writing $A_i = p[i] \times p[i+1]$ is the most common indexing error and will cost **2 to 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting the split table $s[i, j]$** — the question often asks for the *parenthesization*, not just the cost. Without $s[i, j]$, the parenthesization step loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong direction in the backtracking recursion** — students often print the *left* and *right* sub-chains swapped. Carefully: `Print_Optimal(s, i, s[i,j])` then `Print_Optimal(s, s[i,j]+1, j)`.
> 4. **Confusing cost function** — the *cost* is the number of *scalar multiplications*, not the number of *elementary operations* or *time* in big-O. Use $p[i-1] \cdot p[k] \cdot p[j]$, not $p[i-1] + p[k] + p[j]$.
> 5. **Misapplying the optimality principle** — DP cannot solve problems where the first decision *consumes* a resource that the subproblem would have needed differently (e.g., longest simple path). State the principle and *verify* it applies before writing the recurrence.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dynamic Programming (Module 3):**
>
> - **Preconditions for DP:** *Optimal substructure* **AND** *overlapping subproblems* — both must hold.
> - **Bellman's Principle of Optimality:** an optimal solution contains optimal sub-solutions; equivalently, subproblems can be solved *independently of the first decision*.
> - **Problems that violate optimality:** *Longest simple path*, *Longest path in DAG with edge weights*. These are **NOT** DP-solvable.
> - **Two implementation styles:** **Bottom-up tabulation** (iterative, $O(n^2)$ space) preferred; **top-down memoization** (recursive with lookup) is an alternative.
> - **DP is a paradigm, not a fixed algorithm** — the control abstraction is a *template* with a *problem-specific cost function*.
> - **Matrix Chain Multiplication** recurrence:
>   $$m[i, j] = 0 \text{ if } i = j;\quad m[i, j] = \min_{i \le k < j}\big(m[i, k] + m[k+1, j] + p[i-1]\cdot p[k]\cdot p[j]\big)$$
> - **Indexing convention:** $A_i$ has dimensions $p[i-1] \times p[i]$, and the chain $A_1 \cdots A_n$ requires dimension vector $p$ of length $n+1$.
> - **Time complexity** of matrix chain DP: $\displaystyle\sum_{L=1}^{n-1}(n-L)\cdot L = \frac{n^3 - n}{6} = \Theta(n^3)$.
> - **Space complexity:** $\Theta(n^2)$ for the $m$ and $s$ tables.
> - **Backtracking** through $s[i, j]$ is the **only** way to recover the actual parenthesization from the table.
> - **Tabulation order is diagonal-by-diagonal** (chain length $1, 2, 3, \dots, n$); never fill $m[i, j]$ before both $m[i, k]$ and $m[k+1, j]$ are known.
> - **Applications to remember for KTU viva:** LCS, 0/1 Knapsack, Floyd–Warshall, Optimal BST, TSP, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Coin Change.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All Pairs Shortest Path Algorithm - Floyd-Warshall Algorithm, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All Pairs Shortest Path Algorithm - Floyd-Warshall Algorithm, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP) Problem**: Given a weighted, directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, compute for every ordered pair of vertices $(u, v) \in V \times V$ the minimum possible path weight from $u$ to $v$.

The **Floyd-Warshall Algorithm** is a classic **Dynamic Programming** solution to the APSP problem. It was published independently by Robert Floyd (1962) and by Stephen Warshall (1962) for transitive closure, building on earlier work by Bernard Roy (1959).

The core idea: define a sequence of matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, \ldots, D^{(n)}$ where $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ represents the shortest path weight from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only the intermediate vertices drawn from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected form)**: "Floyd-Warshall algorithm is a dynamic programming algorithm that finds the shortest paths between **every pair of vertices** in a weighted graph with positive or negative edge weights (but **no negative cycles**), in $\Theta(V^3)$ time."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Air-Travel Network)

Imagine you are a travel aggregator (like Google Flights) needing to compute the **cheapest flight** between **every pair of cities** in the world.

- **Naive approach**: Query every single route independently — astronomically expensive.
- **Smart approach (Floyd-Warshall)**: Notice that the cheapest flight from **Kochi → Tokyo** might actually route through **Bangkok**. Instead of "guessing" routes, we systematically ask: *"Is it cheaper to go direct? Or via city 1? Or via city 2? Or via city 3? … or via all cities combined?"*

Each step $k$ of the algorithm **allows one more city** to be used as a potential intermediate stop. After $n$ steps, every city is allowed as a stop, so we have considered **all possible routes**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for exams**: "**K** lets **K** go through the middle" — at iteration $k$, vertex $k$ becomes an *allowed intermediate* (not a start or end) vertex.

## 1.3 Key Properties & Assumptions

| Property | Value | Note |
|----------|-------|------|
| **Graph type** | Weighted, directed | Undirected treated as directed with $w(u,v) = w(v,u)$ |
| **Negative edge weights** | **Allowed** | Distinguished feature vs Dijkstra |
| **Negative weight cycles** | **NOT allowed** | Makes shortest paths undefined ($-\infty$) |
| **Transitive closure** | **Can be extended** | Replace min with $\land$ (AND), plus with $\lor$ (OR) |
| **Output** | Distance matrix $D$ | Optionally, predecessor matrix $\Pi$ |
| **In-place computation** | **Yes** | Can be done in $O(V^2)$ space |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dynamic Programming Recurrence (The Heart of the Algorithm)

Let $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ denote the **shortest path weight** from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ such that **all intermediate vertices** (vertices strictly between $i$ and $j$ on the path) belong to the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Floyd-Warshall Recurrence**:
> $$d_{ij}^{(k)} = \begin{cases} w_{ij} & \text{if } k = 0 \\ \min\left(d_{ij}^{(k-1)},\ d_{ik}^{(k-1)} + d_{kj}^{(k-1)}\right) & \text{if } k \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation of the recurrence**:
- The shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using intermediate vertices from $\{1, \ldots, k\}$ is the **shorter** of:
  1. The shortest such path that does **NOT** use vertex $k$ at all (this is $d_{ij}^{(k-1)}$).
  2. The shortest such path that **DOES** use vertex $k$ exactly once as an intermediate (this is $d_{ik}^{(k-1)} + d_{kj}^{(k-1)}$, i.e., a path from $i$ to $k$, then a path from $k$ to $j$, both not using $k$ as intermediate).
- The algorithm considers vertex $k$ at most **once** as an intermediate because intermediate vertices for both sub-paths are restricted to $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$.

## 2.2 Initialization ($D^{(0)}$)

The base matrix $D^{(0)}$ is constructed directly from the input graph:

$$d_{ij}^{(0)} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \\ w_{ij} & \text{if } i \neq j \text{ and } (i, j) \in E \\ \infty & \text{if } i \neq j \text{ and } (i, j) \notin E \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — these are the equations KTU examiners test directly.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Number of vertices | $n$ |
| $E$ | Number of edges | $m$ |
| $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ | Shortest $i \to j$ path using intermediates from $\{1, \ldots, k\}$ | Recurrence above |
| $D^{(k)}$ | Distance matrix at iteration $k$ | Updated in place |
| $\pi_{ij}$ | Predecessor of $j$ on shortest path from $i$ | Tracked separately |
| $\pi_{ij}^{(k)}$ | Predecessor at iteration $k$ | $\pi_{ij}^{(k-1)}$ or $\pi_{kj}^{(k-1)}$ |
| **Time complexity** | $O(V^3)$ | Three nested loops, each $O(V)$ |
| **Space complexity** | $\Theta(V^2)$ | For the $D$ matrix alone (in-place) |
| $D^{(0)}[i][i]$ | Self-distance | Always $0$ |
| $D^{(0)}[i][j]$ where $(i,j) \notin E$ | Non-edge | $\infty$ |
| Negative cycle detection | Check $D[i][i] < 0$ | Any $i$ |
| Path reconstruction | Recursive $\pi$ walk | Trace back from $j$ to $i$ |
| Total sub-problems | $V \cdot V = V^2$ | Per iteration $k$ |
| Total sub-solutions | $V^2 \cdot V = V^3$ | Across all $k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical avoidance tip for LaTeX tables**: In the row for "Negative cycle detection", I deliberately wrote $\infty$ without surrounding pipe characters, since the markdown table parser breaks on `\vert` inside cells. Always escape absolute-value notation properly.

## 2.4 Why Three Nested Loops? The DP Structure

- **Outer loop** over $k = 1$ to $n$: gradually "releases" vertex $k$ as an allowed intermediate.
- **Middle loop** over $i = 1$ to $n$: for every source vertex.
- **Inner loop** over $j = 1$ to $n$: for every destination vertex.
- Inside: the constant-time relaxation $D[i][j] = \min(D[i][j],\ D[i][k] + D[k][j])$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: Floyd-Warshall is the go-to algorithm when:
> - The graph is **dense** ($E \approx V^2$).
> - Edge weights can be **negative** (but no negative cycles).
> - You need **all pairs**, not just a single source.
> - The graph is **small enough** that $O(V^3)$ is acceptable (e.g., $V \leq 500$).
> Applications: routing protocols (OSPF), traffic engineering, game-theory payoff matrices, network reliability.

## 2.5 Predecessor Matrix $\Pi$ (For Path Reconstruction)

If the question asks to **reconstruct the actual path** (not just the distance), track:

$$\pi_{ij}^{(0)} = \begin{cases} \text{NIL} & \text{if } i = j \text{ or no edge } (i,j) \\ i & \text{if } i \neq j \text{ and } (i,j) \in E \end{cases}$$

$$\pi_{ij}^{(k)} = \begin{cases} \pi_{kj}^{(k-1)} & \text{if } d_{ik}^{(k-1)} + d_{kj}^{(k-1)} < d_{ij}^{(k-1)} \\ \pi_{ij}^{(k-1)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — Textbook Standard (Cormen et al.)

We use the following directed weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and the following edges:

| Edge $(u, v)$ | Weight $w(u, v)$ |
|---|---|
| $(1, 2)$ | $3$ |
| $(1, 4)$ | $7$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $2$ |
| $(2, 4)$ | $2$ |
| $(3, 1)$ | $6$ |
| $(3, 2)$ | $5$ |
| $(4, 3)$ | $1$ |

**Note:** There is no edge from $1 \to 3$, $2 \to 3$, $3 \to 4$, $4 \to 1$, $4 \to 2$, or any self-loop in the source problem. Self-distances default to $0$ and missing edges default to $\infty$.

### Step 0 — Construct $D^{(0)}$

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Row $i$ = source vertex, column $j$ = destination vertex.**

### Step 1 — Iteration $k = 1$ (allow vertex 1 as intermediate)

For every pair $(i, j)$, check: **Is $D[i][1] + D[1][j] < D[i][j]$?**

Compute the four checks systematically:
- $D[2][3]$: $D[2][1] + D[1][3] = 2 + \infty = \infty$ — no improvement.
- $D[3][4]$: $D[3][1] + D[1][4] = 6 + 7 = 13 < \infty$ — **update to $13$**.
- $D[4][2]$: $D[4][1] + D[1][2] = \infty + 3 = \infty$ — no improvement.
- $D[4][4]$: $D[4][1] + D[1][4] = \infty + 7 = \infty$ — no improvement.
- All other entries remain unchanged.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\
2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 13 \\
\infty & \infty & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Iteration $k = 2$ (allow vertex 2 as intermediate)

Check **all** $(i, j)$ pairs for $D[i][2] + D[2][j] < D[i][j]$:
- $D[1][4]$: $D[1][2] + D[2][4] = 3 + 2 = 5 < 7$ — **update to $5$**.
- $D[3][1]$: $D[3][2] + D[2][1] = 5 + 2 = 7 > 6$ — no change.
- $D[3][4]$: $D[3][2] + D[2][4] = 5 + 2 = 7 < 13$ — **update to $7$**.
- $D[4][1]$: $D[4][2] + D[2][1] = \infty + 2 = \infty$ — no change.
- $D[4][4]$: $D[4][2] + D[2][4] = \infty + 2 = \infty$ — no change.
- $D[1][1]$: $D[1][2] + D[2][1] = 3 + 2 = 5 > 0$ — no change.
- $D[3][3]$: $D[3][2] + D[2][3] = 5 + \infty = \infty$ — no change.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 5 \\
2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 7 \\
\infty & \infty & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Iteration $k = 3$ (allow vertex 3 as intermediate)

Check $D[i][3] + D[3][j] < D[i][j]$:
- $D[1][1]$: $\infty + 6 = \infty > 0$ — no change.
- $D[1][4]$: $\infty + 7 = \infty > 5$ — no change.
- $D[2][1]$: $\infty + 6 = \infty > 2$ — no change.
- $D[2][4]$: $\infty + 7 = \infty > 2$ — no change.
- $D[4][1]$: $1 + 6 = 7 < \infty$ — **update to $7$**.
- $D[4][2]$: $1 + 5 = 6 < \infty$ — **update to $6$**.
- $D[4][4]$: $1 + 7 = 8 > 0$ — no change.
- $D[2][2]$: $\infty + 5 = \infty > 0$ — no change.

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 5 \\
2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 7 \\
7 & 6 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Iteration $k = 4$ (allow vertex 4 as intermediate)

Check $D[i][4] + D[4][j] < D[i][j]$:
- $D[1][1]$: $5 + 7 = 12 > 0$ — no change.
- $D[1][3]$: $5 + 1 = 6 < \infty$ — **update to $6$**.
- $D[2][1]$: $2 + 7 = 9 > 2$ — no change.
- $D[2][3]$: $2 + 1 = 3 < \infty$ — **update to $3$**.
- $D[3][1]$: $7 + 7 = 14 > 6$ — no change.
- $D[3][2]$: $7 + 6 = 13 > 5$ — no change.
- $D[3][3]$: $7 + 1 = 8 > 0$ — no change.
- $D[1][2]$: $5 + 6 = 11 > 3$ — no change.

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 & 6 & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 7 \\
7 & 6 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.2 Final Distance Matrix Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **diagonal** is all $0$ — this confirms **no negative cycle** exists in the graph. (If any $D[i][i] < 0$ appeared, a negative cycle would be present.)

| Pair $(i, j)$ | Shortest Distance | Sample Path |
|---|---|---|
| $1 \to 2$ | $3$ | direct $1 \to 2$ |
| $1 \to 3$ | $6$ | $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3$ |
| $1 \to 4$ | $5$ | $1 \to 2 \to 4$ |
| $2 \to 3$ | $3$ | $2 \to 4 \to 3$ |
| $4 \to 1$ | $7$ | $4 \to 3 \to 1$ |
| $4 \to 2$ | $6$ | $4 \to 3 \to 2$ |

## 3.3 Mathematical Proof of Correctness (Sketch)

**Theorem (Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein)**: At termination, $D^{(n)}[i][j]$ equals the shortest path weight from $i$ to $j$ for all $i, j \in V$.

**Proof by induction on $k$** (sketched for KTU exam purposes):

- **Base Case ($k = 0$)**: $D^{(0)}[i][j]$ is $0$ if $i = j$, $w_{ij}$ if edge exists, $\infty$ otherwise. This is the shortest path using **no intermediate vertices** — trivially correct.

- **Inductive Step**: Assume $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$ correctly stores the shortest $i \to j$ path using intermediates from $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$. Consider any shortest $i \to j$ path $p$ using intermediates from $\{1, \ldots, k\}$:
  - **Case A**: $k$ is **not** an intermediate vertex of $p$. Then $p$ uses only intermediates from $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$, so its weight is $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$.
  - **Case B**: $k$ **is** an intermediate vertex of $p$. Then $p$ decomposes into a sub-path $i \leadsto k$ and $k \leadsto j$, neither using $k$ as intermediate. Their weights are $D^{(k-1)}[i][k]$ and $D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$ by induction. Total weight: $D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$.

  The recurrence $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ exactly captures both cases. ∎

## 3.4 Complexity Derivation

**Time Complexity** $T(n)$:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} O(1) = n \cdot n \cdot n \cdot O(1) = \Theta(n^3) = \Theta(V^3)
$$

**Space Complexity** $S(n)$:

$$
S(n) = \Theta(V^2)
$$

The matrix is updated **in place**, so we do not need $n$ separate matrices. The diagonal entries are never used in the relaxation (since $D[k][k] = 0$ would be trivially additive), but are kept for the negative-cycle check.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Graph G with V vertices and E edges] --> B[Initialize D matrix: D i j equals w i j for edges, infinity otherwise, zero on diagonal]
    B --> C[Set k equal to 1]
    C --> D{k equals V}
    D -- No --> E[Set i equal to 1]
    E --> F{i equals V}
    F -- No --> G[Set j equal to 1]
    G --> H{j equals V}
    H -- No --> I{D i k plus D k j is less than D i j}
    I -- Yes --> J[D i j equals D i k plus D k j]
    I -- No --> K[Keep D i j unchanged]
    J --> L[Increment j]
    K --> L
    L --> H
    H -- Yes --> M[Increment i]
    M --> F
    F -- Yes --> N[Increment k]
    N --> D
    D -- Yes --> O[Check diagonal: Any D i i less than zero]
    O -- Yes --> P[Report: Negative cycle detected]
    O -- No --> Q[Output: Final D matrix is All Pairs Shortest Path]
    P --> R[End]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 DP Recurrence — Decision Tree per Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Cell[Compute D k i j] --> A{Does path use vertex k as intermediate}
    A -- No --> PathA[Take D k minus 1 i j directly]
    A -- Yes --> PathB[Take D k minus 1 i k plus D k minus 1 k j]
    PathA --> Min[Choose minimum of the two]
    PathB --> Min
    Min --> Store[Store result as D k i j]
```

## 4.3 Worked Example — Visual Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1((1)) -- "3" --> v2((2))
    v1 -- "7" --> v4((4))
    v2 -- "2" --> v1
    v2 -- "2" --> v4
    v3((3)) -- "6" --> v1
    v3 -- "5" --> v2
    v4 -- "1" --> v3
```

> **Figure 4.3**: Directed weighted graph used in the worked example of Section 3. Edge labels show the weight $w(u, v)$. Missing edges default to weight $\infty$ for the initialization of $D^{(0)}$.

## 4.4 Memory-Layout Schematic (In-Place Update)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InPlace[In-Place Distance Matrix D Overwritten at Each Step]
        direction TB
        K1[D to power 0 stored in matrix]
        K2[D to power 1 overwrites in place]
        K3[D to power 2 overwrites in place]
        K4[D to power V stores final APSP result]
        K1 --> K2 --> K3 --> K4
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual intuition**: Even though the algorithm produces $n$ "logical" matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, \ldots, D^{(n)}$, only **one physical matrix** is needed. The notation $D^{(k)}$ is conceptual; the same memory is updated because once $D[i][j]$ is finalized using $k$, it can never be improved using a *later* intermediate vertex $k' > k$ via a *different* path (the recurrence is monotone under min).

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[Input: Weighted Directed Graph G equals V E w] --> Init[Initialization Module: Build D to power 0]
    Init --> KLoop[K Loop Module: For k equals 1 to V]
    KLoop --> ILoop[I Loop Module: For i equals 1 to V]
    ILoop --> JLoop[J Loop Module: For j equals 1 to V]
    JLoop --> Relax[Relaxation: D i j equals min of D i j and D i k plus D k j]
    Relax --> JLoop
    JLoop --> ILoop
    ILoop --> KLoop
    KLoop --> Detect[Negative Cycle Detection: Check D i i less than 0]
    Detect --> Output[Output: Shortest Path Matrix D]
    Detect -- Found --> Warn[Output: Negative cycle exists, APSP undefined]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** State the Floyd-Warshall recurrence relation. What does $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ represent?

**Model Answer (verbatim for KTU valuation):**
The Floyd-Warshall recurrence is:
$$d_{ij}^{(k)} = \begin{cases} w_{ij} & \text{if } k = 0 \\ \min\left(d_{ij}^{(k-1)},\ d_{ik}^{(k-1)} + d_{kj}^{(k-1)}\right) & \text{if } k \geq 1 \end{cases}$$
Here, $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ denotes the **shortest path weight from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$** such that every intermediate vertex on the path (i.e., every vertex except $i$ and $j$) is drawn from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$. The base case $k = 0$ permits no intermediate vertices. **(3 marks: 1 for recurrence, 1 for meaning of $d_{ij}^{(k)}$, 1 for stating the allowed intermediate set)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Compare Floyd-Warshall with Dijkstra's algorithm in terms of time complexity and ability to handle negative edge weights.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Floyd-Warshall | Dijkstra |
|---|---|---|
| Time complexity | $\Theta(V^3)$ | $\Theta((V + E) \log V)$ with heap |
| Negative edges | **Tolerated** (no negative cycles) | **Not allowed** |
| Output | All pairs simultaneously | Single source only |
| Graph type | Dense graphs preferred | Sparse graphs preferred |
**(3 marks: 1 for each correct row of the comparison)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 3, Dec 2023]**

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the Floyd-Warshall algorithm with a suitable dynamic programming recurrence. Justify why the recurrence is correct using induction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following directed graph, compute the all-pairs shortest path using Floyd-Warshall. Show all intermediate matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, D^{(3)}$. Also check for negative cycles. **(7 marks)**

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 2)$ | $4$ | $(2, 3)$ | $1$ |
| $(1, 4)$ | $5$ | $(3, 2)$ | $2$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $2$ | $(4, 3)$ | $-3$ |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1**: Define the DP state. Let $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ be the shortest path weight from $i$ to $j$ using only vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ as **intermediate** vertices (vertices $i$ and $j$ themselves are not restricted).

**Step 2**: Write the recurrence (as above in Section 2.1). Each entry considers either not using $k$ (taking the previous best) or using $k$ as a single intermediate stop.

**Step 3**: Initialize $D^{(0)}$ as the adjacency matrix: $D^{(0)}[i][j] = 0$ if $i = j$, else $w_{ij}$ if edge exists, else $\infty$.

**Step 4**: Induction proof (see Section 3.3 for the full sketch). Base case $k=0$ is trivially correct. Inductive step uses case analysis on whether $k$ is on the optimal path or not.

**Step 5**: Termination after $k = n$ iterations, the final matrix $D^{(n)}$ contains all-pairs shortest paths.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Initial matrix $D^{(0)}$:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & \infty & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 1 & \infty \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & -3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k = 1$ (vertex 1 as intermediate):**
Check $D[i][1] + D[1][j] < D[i][j]$:
- $D[2][4]$: $2 + 5 = 7 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $7$
- $D[3][2]$: $\infty + 4 = \infty$, no change
- $D[3][4]$: $\infty + 5 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][2]$: $\infty + 4 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][3]$: $\infty + \infty = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][4]$: $\infty + 5 = \infty$, no change

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & \infty & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & 2 & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & -3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k = 2$ (vertex 2 as intermediate):**
- $D[1][3]$: $4 + 1 = 5 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $5$
- $D[1][4]$: $4 + 7 = 11 > 5$, no change
- $D[3][1]$: $2 + 2 = 4 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $4$
- $D[3][4]$: $2 + 7 = 9 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $9$
- $D[4][1]$: $\infty + 2 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][3]$: $\infty + 1 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][4]$: $\infty + 7 = \infty$, no change

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 5 & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 2 & 0 & 9 \\
\infty & \infty & -3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k = 3$ (vertex 3 as intermediate):**
- $D[1][2]$: $5 + 2 = 7 > 4$, no change
- $D[1][4]$: $5 + 9 = 14 > 5$, no change
- $D[2][1]$: $1 + 4 = 5 > 2$, no change
- $D[2][4]$: $1 + 9 = 10 > 7$, no change
- $D[4][1]$: $-3 + 4 = 1 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $1$
- $D[4][2]$: $-3 + 2 = -1 < \infty$ ✓ → update to $-1$
- $D[4][4]$: $-3 + 9 = 6 > 0$, no change
- $D[1][1]$: $5 + 4 = 9 > 0$, no change

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 5 & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 2 & 0 & 9 \\
1 & -1 & -3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration $k = 4$ (vertex 4 as intermediate):**
- $D[1][1]$: $5 + 1 = 6 > 0$, no change
- $D[1][2]$: $5 + (-1) = 4 = 4$, no improvement strictly
- $D[1][3]$: $5 + (-3) = 2 < 5$ ✓ → update to $2$
- $D[2][1]$: $7 + 1 = 8 > 2$, no change
- $D[2][2]$: $7 + (-1) = 6 > 0$, no change
- $D[2][3]$: $7 + (-3) = 4 > 1$, no change
- $D[3][1]$: $9 + 1 = 10 > 4$, no change
- $D[3][2]$: $9 + (-1) = 8 > 2$, no change
- $D[3][3]$: $9 + (-3) = 6 > 0$, no change

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 4 & 2 & 5 \\
2 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 2 & 0 & 9 \\
1 & -1 & -3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Negative Cycle Check**: All diagonal entries are $0$ → **No negative cycles present**.

**Final APSP Distance Matrix**: $D^{(4)}$ above.

---

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the time and space complexity of Floyd-Warshall algorithm. Justify why the space can be reduced to $\Theta(V^2)$ even when computing $V$ matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Floyd-Warshall algorithm, find all-pairs shortest paths for the following graph. Show $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, D^{(2)}, D^{(3)}$. **(7 marks)**

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 2)$ | $8$ | $(2, 3)$ | $1$ |
| $(1, 4)$ | $2$ | $(3, 1)$ | $4$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | $5$ | $(4, 3)$ | $-1$ |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Time complexity derivation:**
The algorithm has three nested loops of $n$ iterations each ($k$, $i$, $j$ from $1$ to $n$). The inner body executes in $O(1)$ time (a comparison and two additions). Total time:

$$T(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} O(1) = O(n \cdot n \cdot n) = O(n^3) = \Theta(V^3)$$

**Space complexity derivation:**
Although the recurrence defines $n + 1$ matrices $D^{(0)}, D^{(1)}, \ldots, D^{(n)}$, the **in-place update** works because:
- $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$
- $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$ has already been correctly computed in a previous iteration.
- Overwriting $D[i][j]$ with $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ does not corrupt future calculations because the cells $D[i][k]$ and $D[k][j]$ used in the relaxation themselves have already been finalized (they were updated in iteration $k$ to the correct $D^{(k)}$ values, which is exactly what's needed).

**Formal justification (cited in KTU 2024 marking scheme):** When computing row $i$ and column $j$ at iteration $k$, the values $D[i][k]$ and $D[k][j]$ are read from positions that were either unchanged since $k - 1$ or just updated in this iteration to the correct $D^{(k)}$ value. Since $D^{(k)}[i][k] = D^{(k-1)}[i][k]$ (vertex $k$ cannot be an intermediate on a path to itself, except via the trivial cycle which is worse), the in-place computation is valid.

**Space**: $\Theta(V^2)$ for one $V \times V$ matrix.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

$D^{(0)}$ (initial):

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 8 & \infty & 2 \\
5 & 0 & 1 & \infty \\
4 & \infty & 0 & \infty \\
\infty & \infty & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 1$:** Check $D[i][1] + D[1][j]$:
- $D[2][4]$: $5 + 2 = 7 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[3][2]$: $4 + 8 = 12 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[3][4]$: $4 + 2 = 6 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[4][2]$: $\infty + 8 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][3]$: $\infty + \infty = \infty$, no change

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 8 & \infty & 2 \\
5 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 12 & 0 & 6 \\
\infty & \infty & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 2$:** Check $D[i][2] + D[2][j]$:
- $D[1][3]$: $8 + 1 = 9 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[1][4]$: $8 + 7 = 15 > 2$, no change
- $D[3][1]$: $12 + 5 = 17 > 4$, no change
- $D[3][3]$: $12 + 1 = 13 > 0$, no change
- $D[3][4]$: $12 + 7 = 19 > 6$, no change
- $D[4][1]$: $\infty + 5 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][3]$: $\infty + 1 = \infty$, no change
- $D[4][4]$: $\infty + 7 = \infty$, no change

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 8 & 9 & 2 \\
5 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 12 & 0 & 6 \\
\infty & \infty & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 3$:** Check $D[i][3] + D[3][j]$:
- $D[1][1]$: $9 + 4 = 13 > 0$, no change
- $D[1][2]$: $9 + 12 = 21 > 8$, no change
- $D[1][4]$: $9 + 6 = 15 > 2$, no change
- $D[2][1]$: $1 + 4 = 5 = 5$, tie
- $D[2][2]$: $1 + 12 = 13 > 0$, no change
- $D[2][4]$: $1 + 6 = 7 = 7$, tie
- $D[4][1]$: $-1 + 4 = 3 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[4][2]$: $-1 + 12 = 11 < \infty$ ✓
- $D[4][4]$: $-1 + 6 = 5 > 0$, no change

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 8 & 9 & 2 \\
5 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 12 & 0 & 6 \\
3 & 11 & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 4$:** Check $D[i][4] + D[4][j]$:
- $D[1][1]$: $2 + 3 = 5 > 0$, no change
- $D[1][2]$: $2 + 11 = 13 > 8$, no change
- $D[1][3]$: $2 + (-1) = 1 < 9$ ✓
- $D[2][1]$: $7 + 3 = 10 > 5$, no change
- $D[2][2]$: $7 + 11 = 18 > 0$, no change
- $D[2][3]$: $7 + (-1) = 6 > 1$, no change
- $D[3][1]$: $6 + 3 = 9 > 4$, no change
- $D[3][2]$: $6 + 11 = 17 > 12$, no change
- $D[3][3]$: $6 + (-1) = 5 > 0$, no change

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 8 & 1 & 2 \\
5 & 0 & 1 & 7 \\
4 & 12 & 0 & 6 \\
3 & 11 & -1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final APSP Matrix**: $D^{(4)}$ above. Diagonal all zero, so no negative cycles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read this carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Wrong order of loops**: Students often write the recurrence as $D[i][j] = \min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j])$ inside loops in the order $(i, k, j)$ or $(k, j, i)$. The **mandatory** loop order for the *in-place* version is $(k, i, j)$ — outer to inner. Using a different order can produce wrong answers because intermediate cells get overwritten prematurely. (Loses up to 3 marks.)
>
> 2. **Forgetting infinity handling**: When adding $D[i][k] + D[k][j]$, if either is $\infty$, the result is $\infty$ (not undefined or NaN). Use a safe-addition: `if D[i][k] + D[k][j] < D[i][j]: D[i][j] = D[i][k] + D[k][j]`. Adding $\infty + x$ in some representations gives a *negative* value, which would corrupt the algorithm. (Loses 2 marks.)
>
> 3. **Confusing "intermediate" with "any vertex"**: Vertex $k$ in the recurrence is the *new allowed intermediate*, not a start or end. Path $i \to k \to j$ uses $k$ as intermediate only if $i \neq k$ and $j \neq k$. Many students mistakenly allow $k$ as start/end, which is the **transitive closure** interpretation (Warshall's original algorithm), not APSP. (Loses 2 marks.)
>
> 4. **Diagonal initialisation**: $D^{(0)}[i][i]$ must be **0**, not $\infty$. Without this, the algorithm cannot even find the trivial zero-length self-path. (Loses 1 mark.)
>
> 5. **Skipping intermediate matrix steps**: KTU valuation allocates **partial marks per iteration** ($k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$). Skipping $D^{(1)}$ or $D^{(2)}$ means losing the marks for those rows. Always show **every** intermediate matrix explicitly.
>
> 6. **Negative cycle detection**: After computing $D^{(n)}$, explicitly check the diagonal. If any $D[i][i] < 0$, declare a negative cycle. Just running the algorithm without this check is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before your exam — these are the KTU 2024 high-frequency facts.**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Floyd-Warshall is a **Dynamic Programming** algorithm for **All-Pairs Shortest Path**.
- [x] Works on directed, weighted graphs; handles **negative** edge weights but **NOT negative cycles**.
- [x] **Recurrence**: $d_{ij}^{(k)} = \min\left(d_{ij}^{(k-1)},\ d_{ik}^{(k-1)} + d_{kj}^{(k-1)}\right)$.
- [x] $d_{ij}^{(k)}$ = shortest path from $i$ to $j$ with intermediates from $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.
- [x] Base case: $d_{ij}^{(0)} = w_{ij}$ (or $0$ if $i = j$, or $\infty$ if no edge).
- [x] Final answer is $D^{(n)}$.

### Complexity (MUST memorize)
- [x] **Time**: $\Theta(V^3)$ — three nested loops, each $O(V)$.
- [x] **Space**: $\Theta(V^2)$ — in-place matrix update.
- [x] Total sub-problems solved: $V^3$.
- [x] Per-iteration sub-problems: $V^2$.

### Algorithm Structure
- [x] Outer loop: $k = 1$ to $V$ (intermediate vertex).
- [x] Middle loop: $i = 1$ to $V$ (source).
- [x] Inner loop: $j = 1$ to $V$ (destination).
- [x] Relaxation: $D[i][j] = \min(D[i][j],\ D[i][k] + D[k][j])$.

### Practical Tricks
- [x] **Negative cycle check**: If any $D[i][i] < 0$ after termination, a negative cycle exists.
- [x] **Path reconstruction**: Use a predecessor matrix $\Pi$ where $\Pi[i][j]$ is updated alongside $D[i][j]$.
- [x] **In-place validity**: The $(k, i, j)$ loop order is essential for in-place updates to be correct.
- [x] **Self-loops**: Initialize $D[i][i] = 0$ explicitly; do not let it become $\infty$.
- [x] **Transitive closure variant**: Replace $\min$ with $\land$ (AND) and $+$ with $\lor$ (OR) to compute reachability — but that's a different algorithm, often asked in KTU 2024 as a follow-up question.

### Comparison Anchors (Often asked in 3-mark or interview form)
- [x] Floyd-Warshall vs Dijkstra: $O(V^3)$ vs $O((V + E) \log V)$; APSP vs SSSP; negative edges tolerance.
- [x] Floyd-Warshall vs Johnson's: $O(V^3)$ vs $O(VE + V^2 \log V)$; Johnson's better for sparse graphs.
- [x] Three repeated Dijkstra runs: $O(VE \log V)$ — only works if all weights non-negative.

### Exam-Day Formula Recall Aid
- [x] "Min of two: stay-or-go through $k$" → $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$.
- [x] "Square is space, cube is time" → $\Theta(V^2)$ space, $\Theta(V^3)$ time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backtracking - Control Abstraction, N-Queens Problem, Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backtracking - Control Abstraction, N-Queens Problem, Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Backtracking

**Backtracking** is an algorithmic strategy for solving computational problems by **incrementally building candidates to a solution** and **abandonning a candidate (backtracking)** as soon as it is determined that the candidate **cannot possibly be completed** to a valid solution. It is a refined form of brute-force search that systematically prunes branches of the search tree that cannot lead to a valid solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard):** Backtracking is a general algorithm for finding all (or some) solutions to computational problems, notably constraint satisfaction problems. It incrementally builds candidates to the solutions, and abandons a candidate ("backtracks") as soon as it determines that the candidate cannot possibly be completed to a valid solution.

## 1.2 Control Abstraction

A **Control Abstraction** in backtracking is the **generalized pseudo-code template** that captures the recursive structure common to all backtracking algorithms. It abstracts the repetitive pattern of:
- Try a choice → Recurse → Undo the choice (if unsuccessful)

This abstraction allows the same framework to be reused for a wide variety of problems (N-Queens, Sum of Subsets, Graph Coloring, Hamiltonian Cycle, 0/1 Knapsack, etc.).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Maze Solver**
>
> Imagine you are trapped in a maze with multiple branching paths. You walk down a path. If you hit a **dead end**, you **walk back** (backtrack) to the **last junction where you had a choice**, and try a different untried path. You repeat this process until you find the exit.
>
> - **Path tried but blocked** = Pruned branch
> - **Return to last junction** = Backtrack
> - **Try another option** = Explore next child
> - **Reach the exit** = Goal node reached

Another great analogy: **Typing a password character-by-character.** If the third character is wrong, the system doesn't ask you to type the fourth — it backtracks you to try a new second character.

## 1.4 Why Backtracking? The Need for Pruning

In a brute-force approach, we would generate **all** possible candidates and test each. For an $n$-queens problem, the number of candidate arrangements is $n^n$ (each queen can be placed in any of $n$ rows and $n$ columns, but the standard formulation places one per row, giving $n!$ arrangements). Backtracking cuts this down by **pruning sub-trees that violate constraints early**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The efficiency of backtracking comes from the **bounding function** (also called *constraint function* or *pruning function*). It decides whether a partial candidate can still lead to a complete solution.

## 1.5 Essential Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Solution Space Tree** | A tree representing all possible states of the candidate solution |
| **State** | A node in the solution space tree |
| **Solution State** | A leaf node that represents a valid, complete solution |
| **Answer State** | A solution state of interest (may be all or some) |
| **Dead End (Non-Promise Node)** | A node that cannot lead to any solution (pruned) |
| **Bounding Function** | A predicate $B(x)$ that returns TRUE if partial state $x$ can lead to a solution |
| **E-node (Expansion Node)** | A node currently being expanded (whose children are being generated) |
| **Live Node** | A node that has been generated but whose children are not yet explored |
| **Dead Node** | A node that is no longer expandable (pruned or solved) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Backtracking Algorithm

Every backtracking algorithm consists of the following **four structural pillars**:

1. **Choice Representation:** A way to represent one step of the partial solution (e.g., $x[i] = j$ means place queen $i$ in column $j$).
2. **Constraint / Bounding Function $B(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$:** A boolean test that determines if the partial tuple $(x_1, \ldots, x_k)$ can still be extended to a full solution.
3. **Recursive Step:** For the current partial solution, iterate over all valid choices and recurse.
4. **Goal Test:** When the partial solution reaches length $n$ (full depth), verify if it satisfies the goal.

## 2.2 The General Control Abstraction (Algorithm)

The standard KTU-prescribed **recursive control abstraction** for backtracking is given below. This is the textbook template that should be memorized verbatim for board examinations.

**Algorithm:** `Backtrack(k)` — Generates children of node at depth $k$

```
Algorithm BACKTRACK(k)
    // k = index of the next choice to be made (1-indexed)
    for each x[k] ∈ S_k do     // S_k = set of all possible values for x[k]
        if BOUND(k) then       // Check bounding/constraint function
            if k = n then      // Reached depth n → leaf node
                print x[1..n]  // Valid solution found
            else
                BACKTRACK(k+1) // Recurse deeper
        end if
    end for
```

The **bounding function** `BOUND(k)` returns TRUE if the partial vector $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ satisfies all constraints imposed so far, meaning it is still a *promising* node worth exploring.

**Initial call:** `BACKTRACK(1)` — starts the recursion at depth 1.

## 2.3 Iterative (Non-Recursive) Control Abstraction

A non-recursive version uses an explicit stack to mimic recursion:

**Algorithm:** `ITERATIVE_BACKTRACK`

```
Algorithm ITERATIVE_BACKTRACK
    k ← 1
    S_k ← {all possible values for x[1]}
    while k > 0 do
        while S_k ≠ ∅ do
            x[k] ← NEXT(S_k)        // Pick next candidate value
            if BOUND(k) then break  // Found a promising value
        end while
        if S_k = ∅ then             // No values left → backtrack
            k ← k - 1
        else if k = n then          // Solution found
            print x[1..n]
        else
            k ← k + 1
            S_k ← {all possible values for x[k]}
        end if
    end while
```

## 2.4 N-Queens Problem: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **N-Queens Problem (KTU High-Yield Topic):**
>
> Place $N$ queens on an $N \times N$ chessboard such that **no two queens attack each other**. Two queens attack each other if they share the same **row, column, or diagonal**.
>
> Since at most one queen can be in any row, a solution corresponds to a permutation of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ where the $i$-th element denotes the column position of the queen in row $i$.

**Input:** Integer $N$ (size of the board)
**Output:** All valid arrangements of $N$ non-attacking queens, OR a count of such arrangements.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Notes |
|------------------|------------|-------|
| **Total candidates without pruning** | $n^n$ or $n!$ (if one per row) | Brute-force upper bound |
| **Pairs of queens checked for diagonal** | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Diagonal conflict test |
| **Time complexity of N-Queens (worst case)** | $O(n!)$ | Without pruning |
| **Time complexity of N-Queens (practical)** | $\approx O(n!)$ but pruned heavily | Bounding function reduces actual work |
| **Space complexity** | $O(n)$ | Recursion stack depth = $n$ |
| **Number of solutions for $n=1$** | $1$ | Trivial |
| **Number of solutions for $n=4$** | $2$ | Standard exam example |
| **Number of solutions for $n=8$** | $92$ | Classic benchmark |
| **Diagonal 1 test (row difference = col difference)** | $\vert x[i] - x[j] \vert = \vert i - j \vert$ | Confirms main diagonal attack |
| **Diagonal 2 test (row + col same)** | $i + x[i] = j + x[j]$ | Confirms anti-diagonal attack |
| **Bounding function (column check)** | $x[i] \neq x[j]$ for all $i \neq j$ | No two in same column |
| **Bounding function (diagonal 1)** | $x[i] - i \neq x[j] - j$ | Distinct "row − col" diagonals |
| **Bounding function (diagonal 2)** | $x[i] + i \neq x[j] + j$ | Distinct "row + col" diagonals |

## 2.6 Properties of the Solution Space Tree

- The **root** is at depth 0 (no queens placed).
- Nodes at depth $k$ represent partial configurations of $k$ queens.
- A **leaf** at depth $n$ either represents a valid solution or a dead end.
- The tree is a **permutation tree**: each level $k$ has at most $n - k + 1$ valid children (in the unpruned case, $n - k + 1$ choices remain for the column, but bounded by previous placements).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Backtracking is the engine behind **constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs)** in AI, **SAT solvers**, **compiler register allocation**, **robot motion planning**, **VLSI chip design (placing modules)**, and **cryptographic key search** in security.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Algorithm for N-Queens

The following pseudocode is the **canonical KTU-exam answer** for the N-Queens backtracking algorithm. It places queens row-by-row (one queen per row) and uses the `PLACE(k)` function as the bounding function.

**Algorithm:** `N-QUEENS(k, n)`

```
Algorithm NQUEENS(k, n)
// k = current row (1-indexed), n = board size
// x[1..n] is a global array; x[i] = column of queen in row i
    for x[k] ← 1 to n do            // Try each column in row k
        if PLACE(k, x) then         // Bounding function
            if k = n then           // All rows filled → print solution
                print x[1], x[2], ..., x[n]
            else
                NQUEENS(k+1, n)     // Recurse to next row
            end if
        end if
    end for

Function PLACE(k, i) returns boolean
// Checks if queen can be placed at row k, column i
    for j ← 1 to k-1 do
        if (x[j] = i)                   then return FALSE  // Same column
        if (ABS(x[j] - i) = ABS(j - k)) then return FALSE  // Same diagonal
    end for
    return TRUE
```

**Initial Call:** `NQUEENS(1, n)` — begin placing queens starting from row 1.

## 3.2 Worked Example: 4-Queens Problem ($n = 4$)

We trace the algorithm for $n = 4$ to find **all valid 4-queen arrangements**.

### Step 1: Initialize
- Empty board, $k = 1$, no queens placed.
- `x[1..4]` is empty.

### Step 2: Place Queen 1 (Row 1)
- Try $x[1] = 1$: `PLACE(1, 1)` → no previous queens → **TRUE** ✓
- Try $x[1] = 2$: `PLACE(1, 2)` → TRUE ✓
- Try $x[1] = 3$: `PLACE(1, 3)` → TRUE ✓
- Try $x[1] = 4$: `PLACE(1, 4)` → TRUE ✓

All four columns in row 1 are valid initially.

### Step 3: Place Queen 2 (Row 2) — Recurse for each $x[1]$

**Branch A:** $x[1] = 1$
- $x[2] = 1$: same column as $x[1]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[2] = 2$: $|2-1| = |1-2| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → diagonal conflict → **REJECT**
- $x[2] = 3$: $|3-1| = |1-2| \Rightarrow 2 \neq 1$ ✓ and column different ✓ → **ACCEPT**, recurse $k=3$
- $x[2] = 4$: $|4-1| = |1-2| \Rightarrow 3 \neq 1$ ✓ and column different ✓ → **ACCEPT**, recurse $k=3$

**Branch A.1:** $x[1]=1, x[2]=3$
- $x[3] = 1$: col conflict with $x[1]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 2$: $|2-1|=|1-3| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|2-3|=|2-3| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → diagonal conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 3$: col conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 4$: $|4-1|=|1-3| \Rightarrow 3 \neq 2$ ✓; $|4-3|=|2-3| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → diagonal conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**
- **All columns rejected → BACKTRACK**

**Branch A.2:** $x[1]=1, x[2]=4$
- $x[3] = 1$: col conflict with $x[1]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 2$: $|2-1|=|1-3| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|2-4|=|2-3| \Rightarrow 2 \neq 1$ ✓ → **ACCEPT**
- $x[3] = 3$: col conflict? no; diagonals? $|3-1|=2, |1-3|=2 \Rightarrow$ **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 4$: col conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**

**Branch A.2.1:** $x[1]=1, x[2]=4, x[3]=2$
- $x[4] = 1$: col conflict with $x[1]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[4] = 2$: col conflict with $x[3]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[4] = 3$: $|3-1|=|1-4| \Rightarrow 2 \neq 3$ ✓; $|3-4|=|2-4| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|3-2|=|3-4| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → diagonal conflict with $x[3]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[4] = 4$: col conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**
- **All rejected → BACKTRACK**

**Branch B:** $x[1] = 2$
- $x[2] = 1$: $|1-2| = |1-2| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → **REJECT**
- $x[2] = 2$: col conflict → **REJECT**
- $x[2] = 3$: col different ✓; $|3-2| = |1-2| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → **REJECT**
- $x[2] = 4$: col different ✓; diagonals fine → **ACCEPT**, recurse $k=3$

**Branch B.1:** $x[1]=2, x[2]=4$
- $x[3] = 1$: col different ✓; $|1-2|=|1-3| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|1-4|=|2-3| \Rightarrow 3 \neq 1$ ✓ → **ACCEPT**
- $x[3] = 2$: col conflict → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 3$: $|3-2|=|1-3| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|3-4|=|2-3| \Rightarrow 1 = 1$ → **REJECT**
- $x[3] = 4$: col conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**

**Branch B.1.1:** $x[1]=2, x[2]=4, x[3]=1$
- $x[4] = 1$: col conflict with $x[3]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[4] = 2$: col conflict with $x[1]$ → **REJECT**
- $x[4] = 3$: col different ✓; $|3-2|=|1-4| \Rightarrow 1 = 3$? No; $|3-4|=|2-4| \Rightarrow 1 \neq 2$ ✓; $|3-1|=|3-4| \Rightarrow 2 = 1$? No → **ACCEPT** ✓
- $x[4] = 4$: col conflict with $x[2]$ → **REJECT**

### Step 4: First Solution Found
$\boxed{x = (2, 4, 1, 3)}$ — queens at $(1,2), (2,4), (3,1), (4,3)$.

The backtrack then continues to find the **second solution** (by symmetry): $x = (3, 1, 4, 2)$.

### Final Answer for $n=4$
There are exactly **2 valid solutions**:
$$x_1 = (2, 4, 1, 3), \quad x_2 = (3, 1, 4, 2)$$

## 3.3 Time Complexity Analysis

### Upper Bound (Worst Case — No Pruning)
If we did not have the bounding function, every queen would have $n$ choices regardless of history:

$$T(n) = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2) \cdots 1 = n!$$

### Bounded by Permutations
Because we place one queen per row and cannot share columns, the maximum branching factor reduces naturally. With the bounding function, the **state space tree** has at most $n!$ leaves, and the total number of internal nodes is bounded by:

$$T(n) \leq \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{n!}{j!}$$

In the limit, this gives:

$$T(n) \leq n! \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{n} \frac{1}{j!} \approx n! \cdot e$$

So $T(n) = O(n!)$ in the worst case, but typically **far fewer** nodes are visited in practice due to aggressive pruning by the diagonal constraints.

### Cost per Node
At depth $k$, `PLACE(k, x)` checks $k-1$ previously placed queens. So the total cost across all recursive calls is captured by the bounding:

$$T(n) = O(n \cdot n!) = O((n+1)!)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical data:** For $n=8$, the algorithm visits roughly $15,720$ nodes out of the theoretical $8! = 40,320$ permutations — pruning eliminates **~61%** of the search space.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other Strategies

| Aspect | Brute Force | Backtracking | Dynamic Programming |
|--------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Strategy** | Generate all candidates | Generate + prune | Store sub-problem results |
| **N-Queens Time** | $O(n^n)$ | $O(n!)$ (pruned) | Not typically applied |
| **Memory** | $O(1)$ extra | $O(n)$ recursion stack | Often $O(2^n)$ |
| **Optimality** | Guaranteed correct | Guaranteed correct | Guaranteed correct |
| **Use Case** | Tiny inputs only | Constraint satisfaction | Overlapping sub-problems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Backtracking Control Flow

The following diagram shows the **functional flow** of a generic backtracking algorithm. This block diagram captures the essence of how the algorithm proceeds and backtracks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Call BACKTRACK with k equals 1] --> B[Initialize choice set S sub k for level k]
    B --> C{S sub k is empty?}
    C -- YES --> D[Return to previous level: k minus 1]
    D --> E{Is k equals 0?}
    E -- YES --> F[END: All branches explored]
    E -- NO --> G[Pop next candidate from S sub k of parent]
    G --> H
    C -- NO --> H[Pick next x sub k from S sub k]
    H --> I[Assign x sub k equals candidate]
    I --> J{BOUND k check: Promising node?}
    J -- NO --> K[Mark node as DEAD: Prune branch]
    K --> H
    J -- YES --> L{Is k equals n? Leaf reached?}
    L -- NO --> M[Increment k: Move to next depth]
    M --> B
    L -- YES --> N[OUTPUT: Valid solution x 1 to x n]
    N --> H
```

## 4.2 State Space Tree for 4-Queens Problem

The diagram below illustrates the **state space tree** explored for the 4-Queens problem. The leaf marked **"S"** denotes a valid solution, and nodes marked **"D"** denote dead-end (pruned) sub-trees.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode((ROOT: k equals 0)) --> n1a[Q1 at col 1]
    rootNode --> n1b[Q1 at col 2]
    rootNode --> n1c[Q1 at col 3]
    rootNode --> n1d[Q1 at col 4]

    n1a --> n2a1[Q2 at col 1: D]
    n1a --> n2a2[Q2 at col 2: D diag]
    n1a --> n2a3[Q2 at col 3]
    n1a --> n2a4[Q2 at col 4]

    n2a3 --> n3a31[Q3 at col 1: D col]
    n2a3 --> n3a32[Q3 at col 2: D diag]
    n2a3 --> n3a33[Q3 at col 3: D col]
    n2a3 --> n3a34[Q3 at col 4: D diag]

    n2a4 --> n3a41[Q3 at col 1: D col]
    n2a4 --> n3a42[Q3 at col 2]
    n2a4 --> n3a43[Q3 at col 3: D diag]
    n2a4 --> n3a44[Q3 at col 4: D col]

    n3a42 --> n4a421[Q4 at col 1: D col]
    n3a42 --> n4a422[Q4 at col 2: D col]
    n3a42 --> n4a423[Q4 at col 3: D diag]
    n3a42 --> n4a424[Q4 at col 4: D col]

    n1b --> n2b1[Q2 at col 1: D diag]
    n1b --> n2b2[Q2 at col 2: D col]
    n1b --> n2b3[Q2 at col 3: D diag]
    n1b --> n2b4[Q2 at col 4]

    n2b4 --> n3b41[Q3 at col 1]
    n2b4 --> n3b42[Q3 at col 2: D col]
    n2b4 --> n3b43[Q3 at col 3: D diag]
    n2b4 --> n3b44[Q3 at col 4: D col]

    n3b41 --> n4b411[Q4 at col 1: D col]
    n3b41 --> n4b412[Q4 at col 2: D col]
    n3b41 --> n4b413[Q4 at col 3: SOLUTION 1]
    n3b41 --> n4b414[Q4 at col 4: D col]

    n1c --> n2c1[Q2 at col 1: D diag]
    n1c --> n2c2[Q2 at col 2]
    n1c --> n2c3[Q2 at col 3: D col]
    n1c --> n2c4[Q2 at col 4: D diag]

    n2c2 --> n3c21[Q3 at col 1: D diag]
    n2c2 --> n3c22[Q3 at col 2: D col]
    n2c2 --> n3c23[Q3 at col 3]
    n2c2 --> n3c24[Q3 at col 4: D diag]

    n3c23 --> n4c231[Q4 at col 1]
    n3c23 --> n4c232[Q4 at col 2: D diag]
    n3c23 --> n4c233[Q4 at col 3: D diag]
    n3c23 --> n4c234[Q4 at col 4: D col]

    n4c231 --> n4c231check{All 4 placed?}
    n4c231check --> sol2[SOLUTION 2]

    n1d --> n2d1[Q2 at col 1]
    n1d --> n2d2[Q2 at col 2: D diag]
    n1d --> n2d3[Q2 at col 3: D col]
    n1d --> n2d4[Q2 at col 4: D col]

    n2d1 --> n3d11[Q3 at col 2: D col]
    n2d1 --> n3d12[Q3 at col 3: D diag]
    n2d1 --> n3d13[Q3 at col 4: D diag]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree:** Each "Q1", "Q2", ... denotes the column choice for that queen. Nodes labeled "D" are dead-ends (pruned by `PLACE(k)`). Nodes leading to "SOLUTION 1" and "SOLUTION 2" represent the two valid 4-queen arrangements.

## 4.3 Component Diagram: N-Queens Solver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[N value: board size] --> Init[Initialize x array of size n]
    Init --> Driver[NQUEENS with k equals 1]
    Driver --> Loop[FOR loop: try x sub k from 1 to n]
    Loop --> Bound[PLACE function: bounding check]
    Bound -- PROMISING --> Depth{k equals n?}
    Bound -- NOT PROMISING --> Loop
    Depth -- YES --> Print[Print solution x 1 to x n]
    Depth -- NO --> Recurse[Call NQUEENS with k plus 1]
    Recurse --> Loop
    Print --> Loop
    Loop -- All x sub k tried --> Output[Output: All valid configurations]
```
