# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**  
> **Define backtracking. List any two problems that can be solved using backtracking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Backtracking is an algorithmic technique for solving problems recursively by trying to build a solution incrementally, one piece at a time, and removing those solutions that fail to satisfy the constraints of the problem at any point in time (i.e., backtracking).

**Examples (1 mark):** Any two of:
- N-Queens Problem
- Sum of Subsets Problem
- Graph Coloring (m-Coloring)
- Hamiltonian Cycle Problem
- 0/1 Knapsack Problem
- Subset Sum Problem
- Maze Solving

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**  
> **What is a bounding function in the context of backtracking? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A bounding function $B(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ is a predicate (boolean function) used in backtracking to test whether the partial solution vector $(x_1, \ldots, x_k)$ constructed so far is *promising* — i.e., whether it can still possibly be extended to a complete valid solution.

**Importance (1 mark):** The bounding function is what makes backtracking efficient compared to brute force. It **prunes sub-trees** of the state space that cannot lead to any solution, drastically reducing the search space and time complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **control abstraction of backtracking** algorithm with a suitable general pseudo-code.  
> **(b)** What is the **N-Queens problem**? Write a backtracking algorithm to solve it and explain how the bounding function works.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option Set 1

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**  
**Explain the control abstraction of backtracking with a general pseudo-code.**

**Model Solution:**

The control abstraction is a generalized procedure that captures the structure common to all backtracking algorithms. The recursive form is given below.

**Pseudo-code (5 marks):**

```
Algorithm BACKTRACK(k)
// k = index of the next decision variable
    for each x[k] in S_k do
        if BOUND(k) then
            if k equals n then
                print x[1], x[2], ..., x[n]
            else
                BACKTRACK(k + 1)
            end if
        end if
    end for
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- $x[1..n]$ is a global solution vector.
- $S_k$ is the finite set of all possible candidate values for $x[k]$.
- `BOUND(k)` is the bounding function — it returns TRUE if the partial vector is "promising" (can still yield a solution).
- The recursion stops either when $k = n$ (a solution is found) or when no promising child exists (backtrack).
- The initial invocation is `BACKTRACK(1)`.

**Recursive Call Tree Analysis (bonus):** Each call to `BACKTRACK(k)` generates one level of the state space tree. When all children of a node are exhausted, control returns to the parent (the recursive "backtrack").

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**  
**What is the N-Queens problem? Write a backtracking algorithm and explain the bounding function.**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Definition (1 mark):**  
Place $n$ queens on an $n \times n$ chessboard such that no two queens attack each other (i.e., no two share a row, column, or diagonal).

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

```
Algorithm NQUEENS(k, n)
// Place queens row by row; x[i] = column of queen in row i
    for x[k] ← 1 to n do
        if PLACE(k, x) then
            if k equals n then
                print x[1..n]
            else
                NQUEENS(k + 1, n)
            end if
        end if
    end for

Function PLACE(k, x) returns boolean
    for j ← 1 to k - 1 do
        if x[j] = x[k]                        then return FALSE
        if ABS(x[j] - x[k]) = ABS(j - k)     then return FALSE
    end for
    return TRUE
```

**Bounding Function Explanation (2 marks):**  
`PLACE(k, x)` checks the queen in row $k$ against all previously placed queens in rows $1$ to $k-1$:
- **Column conflict:** $x[j] = x[k]$ — same column.
- **Main diagonal conflict:** $x[j] - j = x[k] - k$ — same "row − col" diagonal.
- **Anti-diagonal conflict:** $x[j] + j = x[k] + k$ — same "row + col" diagonal.

If any conflict is detected, the placement is **rejected** (pruned); otherwise, the partial configuration is *promising* and recursion continues.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**  
**Explain the recursive and iterative control abstractions of backtracking with diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Recursive Form (3.5 marks):** The recursive abstraction directly mirrors the natural structure of the state space tree. Each recursive call expands one level. The pseudo-code:

```
Algorithm RECURSIVE_BACKTRACK(k)
    for each candidate value v in S_k do
        x[k] ← v
        if BOUND(k) is TRUE then
            if k equals n then OUTPUT(x)
            else RECURSIVE_BACKTRACK(k + 1)
```

**Iterative Form (3.5 marks):** The iterative version uses an explicit stack and a `NEXT` function to enumerate candidates. It mimics recursion manually:

```
Algorithm ITERATIVE_BACKTRACK
    k ← 1
    S_k ← INIT(1)
    while k > 0 do
        while S_k not empty do
            x[k] ← NEXT(S_k)
            if BOUND(k) then break
        end while
        if S_k is empty then
            k ← k - 1         // Backtrack
        else if k = n then
            OUTPUT(x); k ← k - 1
        else
            k ← k + 1
            S_k ← INIT(k)
        end if
    end while
```

**Comparison (bonus):** The recursive form is cleaner and more intuitive, while the iterative form saves function-call overhead and is preferred in memory-constrained environments.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**  
**Solve the 4-Queens problem using backtracking. Show the complete state space tree and the final solutions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Trace the algorithm (4 marks):**
Following the algorithm `NQUEENS(k, 4)` with `PLACE` as the bounding function:

1. $x[1] = 1$ (row 1, col 1) ✓
2. $x[2] = 3$ (row 2, col 3) — col different, diagonal: $|3-1| \neq |2-1|$ → ✓
3. Try $x[3] = 1, 2, 3, 4$ — all conflict (column or diagonal) → **BACKTRACK**
4. $x[2] = 4$ ✓
5. $x[3] = 2$ ✓
6. Try $x[4] = 1, 2, 3, 4$ — all conflict → **BACKTRACK**
7. Backtrack to $x[1] = 2$
8. $x[2] = 4$ ✓
9. $x[3] = 1$ ✓
10. $x[4] = 3$ ✓ → **SOLUTION 1: (2, 4, 1, 3)**

The algorithm then continues and finds **SOLUTION 2: (3, 1, 4, 2)**.

**State Space Tree (2 marks):** Drawn in Section 4.2 above.

**Final Answer (1 mark):** The 4-Queens problem has exactly **2 solutions**: $(2,4,1,3)$ and $(3,1,4,2)$.

**Board visualization for Solution 1** $(2,4,1,3)$:

| Row \ Col | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| **1**     | . | Q | . | . |
| **2**     | . | . | . | Q |
| **3**     | Q | . | . | . |
| **4**     | . | . | Q | . |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `ABS` in diagonal check:** Students often write $|i - j|$ incorrectly. The correct test is $\vert x[j] - x[k] \vert = \vert j - k \vert$ (absolute values of differences). Loss: **1 mark** in the bounding function.
>
> 2. **Confusing row and column indices:** In `PLACE(k, x)`, $k$ is the **row** and $x[k]$ is the **column**. Many students invert these. Loss: **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the initial call:** Always state that the algorithm is invoked as `NQUEENS(1, n)`. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** if this is missing.
>
> 4. **Missing the backtrack visualization:** For 4-Queens questions, examiners expect the **state space tree** (or at least a tabular trace). Pure algorithm without trace gets **partial credit only**.
>
> 5. **Off-by-one in `k = n` check:** The goal test is `k = n` (not `k > n`), since indexing is 1-based. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
>
> 6. **Stating time complexity as $O(n^n)$:** The correct complexity is $O(n!)$ (one queen per row), not $O(n^n)$ (brute force). Loss: **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Backtracking = Pruned brute force** — generate candidates, but kill branches that violate constraints.
- [x] **Control abstraction is the recursive template:** `for x[k] ∈ S_k: if BOUND(k): recurse or print.`
- [x] **Initial call is `BACKTRACK(1)`** — never `BACKTRACK(0)`.
- [x] **Bounding function `PLACE(k, x)`** checks (i) column conflict $x[j] = x[k]$, (ii) main diagonal $|x[j] - x[k]| = |j - k|$, (iii) anti-diagonal $x[j] + j = x[k] + k$.
- [x] **N-Queens: place one queen per row** — solution is a permutation of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.
- [x] **Number of solutions:** $n=1 \to 1$, $n=4 \to 2$, $n=8 \to 92$, $n=12 \to 14200$.
- [x] **Time complexity:** $O(n!)$ worst case, but in practice $O(n! \cdot e)$ upper bound is tighter.
- [x] **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ — recursion stack depth equals board size.
- [x] **Iterative version** uses `NEXT(S_k)` and explicit stack to mimic recursion.
- [x] **State space tree nodes:** E-node (expanding), Live (generated but not expanded), Dead (pruned or solved).
- [x] **Goal test:** `if k = n then OUTPUT`.
- [x] **N-Queens has no solution for $n=2$ and $n=3$.**
- [x] **Key applications:** Graph coloring, Hamiltonian path, Sudoku solver, Subset sum, 0/1 knapsack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P, NP, NP-Hard and NP-Complete Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** P, NP, NP-Hard and NP-Complete Classes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Central Question of Complexity Theory Computational complexity theory classifies problems based on the **inherent resources** (time, space) required to solve them. For Module 4, the lens shifts from *designing efficient algorithms* (Modules 1–3) to *understanding the limits of computation itself*. The central question is: **"Are there problems that are fundamentally hard to solve, even with unlimited cleverness and the fastest possible machines?"** --- ## 1.2 Formal Definition: Decision Problems A **Decision Problem** is a problem whose answer is strictly **YES** or **NO** for every possible input instance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Reduction to Decision Form** > Most optimization problems (e.g., *"Find the shortest route visiting all cities"*) can be reformulated as decision problems (e.g., *"Does a route of length ≤ K exist?"*). This reformulation is the **first mandatory step** in any NP-completeness proof. The complexity classes **P, NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete are exclusively defined over decision problems.** --- ## 1.3 The Four Complexity Classes — Formal Definitions ### **Class P (Polynomial Time)** A decision problem $\Pi$ belongs to class **P** if there exists a deterministic algorithm $A$ such that: - $A$ halts with the correct YES/NO answer for every input $x$ of size $n$. - The worst-case running time $T(n)$ is bounded by some polynomial: $T(n) = O(n^k)$ for some constant $k \geq 0$. > **Plain English Intuition:** P is the set of problems for which we can **write a fast algorithm** — one whose runtime grows gracefully (polynomially) with input size. ### **Class NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time)** A decision problem $\Pi$ belongs to class **NP** if there exists a deterministic verification algorithm $V(x, c)$ such that: - If the answer is **YES**, there exists a *certificate* (witness) $c$ of polynomial length ($\vert c \vert = O(n^k)$) for which $V$ returns YES. - If the answer is **NO**, then for *every* possible certificate $c$, $V$ returns NO. - $V$ runs in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. > **Plain English Intuition:** NP is the set of problems where, *if someone hands you a proposed solution*, you can **quickly check whether it is correct** — even if finding that solution from scratch is hard. ### **Class NP-Hard** A problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Hard** if *every* problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to $\Pi$. Equivalently, solving $\Pi$ in polynomial time would imply $P = NP$. NP-Hard problems are **at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP**, but they need not themselves belong to NP (and may even be undecidable). ### **Class NP-Complete** A problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Complete** if **both** conditions hold: 1. $\Pi \in \text{NP}$ (verifiable in polynomial time). 2. $\Pi$ is **NP-Hard** (every problem in NP reduces to $\Pi$ in polynomial time). NP-Complete is the *exact intersection* of NP and NP-Hard. --- ## 1.4 The Real-World Analogy: The Sudoku Puzzle | Stage | Class | Sudoku Analogy | |---|---|---| | **Easy to Solve AND Verify** | **P** | Multiplying two 10-digit numbers. You compute it in seconds, and verifying the multiplication is equally fast. | | **Hard to Solve, Easy to Verify** | **NP** | A 25×25 Sudoku puzzle. Finding the solution may take hours, but if a friend shows you a filled grid, you can **check it row-by-row, column-by-column in seconds**. | | **At least as hard as the hardest in NP** | **NP-Hard** | A general constraint satisfaction problem so tangled that no known verifier even exists in a clean form. (Think: the "Halting Problem" analogue.) | | **Hardest verifiable problems** | **NP-Complete** | The 9×9 Sudoku itself, or SAT, or the Travelling Salesman (decision version). These are the **"Gold Standard" hard problems**. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction Most Students Miss:** > - **P ⊆ NP** is **trivially true** (any problem you can solve in poly-time, you can also verify in poly-time by re-solving). > - The **million-dollar unsolved question** is whether **P = NP** or **P ⊊ NP**. --- ## 1.5 Why This Matters in KTU Exams KTU examiners love testing whether students can: 1. Correctly **classify a given problem** into P, NP, NP-Hard, or NP-Complete. 2. State the **Cook–Levin Theorem** and the **first NP-Complete problem (SAT)**. 3. Perform a **polynomial-time reduction** to prove NP-Hardness. 4. Draw the **Euler/Venn diagram** of the four classes. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decision vs Optimization Problems — Why the Distinction Matters For every optimization problem, we can construct a **decision version** by introducing a bound parameter $K$: $$\Pi_{\text{opt}}: \text{ Find a solution of minimum/maximum cost } C^*$$ $$\Pi_{\text{dec}}(K): \text{ Does there exist a solution with cost } \leq K \text{ (or } \geq K\text{)?}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Equivalence of Difficulty):** If the decision version of a problem is hard (NP-Complete), then the optimization version is **at least as hard**. This is why complexity theory works primarily on decision problems — it gives a clean, uniform mathematical framework. --- ## 2.2 Polynomial-Time Reduction — The Heart of NP-Completeness ### 2.2.1 Formal Definition A problem $A$ is **polynomial-time reducible** to a problem $B$, written $A \leq_p B$, if there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that: $$x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$$ ### 2.2.2 The Logic in Five Steps 1. **Transform** any instance $x$ of $A$ into a valid instance $f(x)$ of $B$ in polynomial time. 2. **Solve** $f(x)$ using any hypothetical algorithm for $B$. 3. **Map back** the answer (YES/NO) from $B$ to a correct answer for $A$. 4. **Bound the total work:** Polynomial transformation + Polynomial call to $B$ = **Polynomial overall**. 5. **Conclude:** If $B$ were solvable in poly-time, so would $A$ be. ### 2.2.3 Why Reduction Proves Hardness > [!NOTE] > **Reduction Lemma:** If $A \leq_p B$ and $B \in P$, then $A \in P$. The contrapositive gives: if $A \notin P$ (and $A$ is NP-Complete) and $A \leq_p B$, then $B$ is **NP-Hard**. This is the **exact technique** KTU examiners reward: *"Show X is NP-Complete by reducing 3-SAT (or some other known NP-Complete problem) to X in polynomial time."* --- ## 2.3 Cook–Levin Theorem — The Cornerstone > **Theorem (Cook, 1971; independently Levin, 1973):** > *The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete.* **Why SAT was the *first* proven NP-Complete problem:** - SAT is trivially in NP (the truth assignment is the certificate, verifiable in linear time). - Cook proved that *every* problem in NP can be reduced to SAT by encoding the **computation history** of any polynomial-time nondeterministic Turing machine as a Boolean formula. All other NP-Completeness proofs (for Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle, 3-SAT, Subset Sum, etc.) hang on this single theorem — they reduce **SAT → their problem** in polynomial time. --- ## 2.4 Canonical NP-Complete Problems (KTU Favorites) | # | Problem | Domain | Reduction Source | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **SAT / 3-SAT** | Logic | Cook–Levin (Base Case) | | 2 | **Vertex Cover** | Graph | From 3-SAT | | 3 | **Clique** | Graph | From 3-SAT | | 4 | **Hamiltonian Cycle** | Graph | From Vertex Cover | | 5 | **Travelling Salesman (Decision)** | Graph | From Hamiltonian Cycle | | 6 | **Subset Sum** | Number Theory | From 3-SAT | | 7 | **Graph Coloring (k ≥ 3)** | Graph | From 3-SAT | | 8 | **Independent Set** | Graph | From Clique (complement) | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | KTU Pitfall | |---|---|---| | $T(n) = O(n^k)$ | Polynomial time bound for class P | Don't confuse with exponential $O(2^n)$ | | $A \leq_p B$ | Polynomial-time many-one reduction from $A$ to $B$ | Direction matters! | | $\vert x \vert$ | Length of input string $x$ in bits | Use $\vert x \vert$ (not $\vert x \vert$) in LaTeX | | $\vert c \vert \leq p(\vert x \vert)$ | Polynomial-length certificate | Certificate length is the *size* of the witness | | $P \subseteq NP$ | Trivial inclusion | Reverse inclusion is the **unsolved** $P = NP$ question | | NP-Complete | $A \in NP$ **AND** $A$ is NP-Hard | Both conditions are required | | NP-Hard $\not\Rightarrow$ NP | NP-Hard problems may not be in NP | Common examiner trap | | SAT | First NP-Complete problem (Cook's Theorem) | Must be named, not "Boolean formula" alone | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of NP-Complete Awareness | |---|---| | **Cryptography** | RSA security depends on **Integer Factorization** being hard (not in P). $P = NP$ would break modern encryption. | | **Operations Research** | TSP NP-Completeness means heuristic/metaheuristic algorithms (Genetic, Simulated Annealing) are used in practice. | | **Compiler Design** | Register allocation, instruction scheduling are NP-Complete — hence greedy approximations. | | **Bioinformatics** | Protein folding, sequence alignment face NP-Complete sub-problems. | | **VLSI Design** | Circuit partitioning and layout optimization are NP-Complete. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Knowing a problem is NP-Complete is **not defeat** — it tells the engineer to use **approximation algorithms, heuristics, or randomized algorithms** for practical deployment. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Formal Proof Skeleton: Proving a Problem is NP-Complete To prove that a problem $\Pi$ is NP-Complete, KTU expects a **three-part proof**: ### **Part 1: Show $\Pi \in NP$** - Describe the **certificate** $c$ (the proposed solution). - Describe the **verification algorithm** $V$. - Prove $V$ runs in $O(n^k)$ time. - Prove correctness: $V$ accepts iff the certificate is valid. ### **Part 2: Show $\Pi$ is NP-Hard** - Select a **known NP-Complete problem** $A$ (typically 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, or Hamiltonian Cycle). - Construct a polynomial-time function $f$ that transforms any instance of $A$ into an instance of $\Pi$. - Prove the **equivalence**: $x \in A \iff f(x) \in \Pi$. - Prove $f$ runs in polynomial time (bound every step). ### **Part 3: Conclude** $$\Pi \in NP \;\;\text{AND}\;\; \Pi \text{ is NP-Hard} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Pi \text{ is NP-Complete}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivation: The Clique ≤ₚ Independent Set Reduction This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem** because it elegantly demonstrates the reduction technique. ### 3.2.1 Problem Statements **Clique:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, is there a subset $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k$ such that every pair in $C$ is connected by an edge? **Independent Set:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, is there a subset $I \subseteq V$ with $\vert I \vert = k$ such that **no** pair in $I$ is connected by an edge? ### 3.2.2 The Reduction Function Define the **complement graph** $\overline{G} = (V, \overline{E})$ where: $$\overline{E} = \{ (u, v) \mid u, v \in V,\; u \neq v,\; (u, v) \notin E \}$$ The reduction $f$ takes $(G, k)$ and outputs $(\overline{G}, k)$. ### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Proof of Equivalence We need to prove: $$(G, k) \in \text{Clique} \iff (\overline{G}, k) \in \text{Independent Set}$$ **Forward Direction (⇒):** Assume $(G, k) \in$ Clique, so there exists $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k$ and for all distinct $u, v \in C$, we have $(u, v) \in E$. Consider the same set $C$ in $\overline{G}$. For any distinct $u, v \in C$: $$(u, v) \notin E \;\;\text{(by Clique definition)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (u, v) \in \overline{E}$$ Thus in $\overline{G}$, no two vertices of $C$ are adjacent, so $C$ is an Independent Set of size $k$. **Backward Direction (⇐):** Assume $(\overline{G}, k) \in$ Independent Set, so there exists $I \subseteq V$ with $\vert I \vert = k$ and for all distinct $u, v \in I$, $(u, v) \notin \overline{E}$, which means $(u, v) \in E$. Thus $I$ is a Clique of size $k$ in $G$. ### 3.2.4 Polynomial Time Bound Computing the complement graph requires examining every pair of vertices: $$T(n) = O(\vert V \vert^2) = O(n^2)$$ This is polynomial. Therefore: $$\text{Clique} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}$$ ### 3.2.5 Conclusion Since Clique is NP-Complete (proven via 3-SAT reduction), and Clique $\leq_p$ Independent Set, **Independent Set is NP-Hard**. Since Independent Set is trivially in NP, **Independent Set is NP-Complete**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Common Problems | Problem | Belongs to P? | Belongs to NP? | NP-Hard? | NP-Complete? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sorting | ✅ Yes (Merge Sort: $O(n \log n)$) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | Shortest Path (Dijkstra) | ✅ Yes $O(E \log V)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | 2-SAT | ✅ Yes (Implication graph) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | 3-SAT | ❌ (Unknown) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | Hamiltonian Cycle | ❌ (Unknown) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | Travelling Salesman (Decision) | ❌ (Unknown) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | Halting Problem | ❌ Undecidable | ❌ Not in NP | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not NP-C (not in NP) | | Integer Factorization | ❌ (Unknown) | ✅ Yes | ❌ (Not proven) | ❌ Not proven | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Why $P \subseteq NP$ is Trivial Let $\Pi \in P$. Then there exists a deterministic algorithm $A$ running in $O(n^k)$ that solves $\Pi$. Construct a verifier $V(x, c)$ that simply **ignores** the certificate $c$ and runs $A(x)$. Then: - If $x \in \Pi$ (answer YES): there exists *some* certificate (e.g., the empty string $\varepsilon$), and $V$ returns YES. - If $x \notin \Pi$ (answer NO): for *every* certificate, $V$ returns NO. - $V$ runs in $O(n^k)$, polynomial. Hence every $P$ problem is in NP. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The reverse $NP \subseteq P$ is exactly the unsolved $P = NP$ problem.** A polynomial-time algorithm for *any single* NP-Complete problem would collapse all four classes. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Complexity Class Hierarchy (Euler Diagram) The following Mermaid block renders the standard Euler diagram showing the relationships between P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard under the **widely-believed assumption** that $P \neq NP$. ```mermaid graph TB nphard["NP-HARD SET
All problems at least as hard as NP
Includes undecidable problems"] np["NP
Verifiable in polynomial time"] p["P
Solvable in polynomial time"] npc["NP-COMPLETE
Intersection of NP and NP-Hard
SAT, TSP, Vertex Cover, Clique"] nphard --> np np --> p np --> npc nphard --> npc p -.->|P vs NP
unsolved question| np ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The NP-Completeness Proof Pipeline ```mermaid graph TB startA["Known NP-Complete
Problem A
e.g., 3-SAT"] step1["Step 1: Construct
polynomial transformation f
mapping instances of A to instances of X"] step2["Step 2: Prove equivalence
x in A iff f x in X"] step3["Step 3: Bound f time
by some polynomial p n"] step4["Step 4: Verify X is in NP
describe certificate and verifier"] targetX["Target Problem X
Now proven NP-Complete"] cookBase["Cook-Levin Theorem:
SAT is the base case
foundation of all reductions"] cookBase --> startA startA --> step1 step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> targetX ``` --- ## 4.3 Layered Architecture: Where Each Class "Lives" in the Complexity Zoo ```mermaid graph TB level1["EXPTIME and Beyond
Exponential time, PSPACE"] level2["NP-HARD
May be undecidable
Examples: Halting Problem,
Optimization versions of NP-Complete"] level3["NP-COMPLETE
Decision problems hardest in NP
SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover,
Hamiltonian Cycle, TSP-decision"] level4["NP
Verifiable in poly-time
Includes all of P"] level5["P
Solvable in poly-time
Sorting, Shortest Path,
2-SAT, MST, BFS, DFS"] level1 --> level2 level2 --> level3 level3 --> level4 level4 --> level5 ``` --- ## 4.4 Reduction Chain Visualizer (Common KTU Reductions) ```mermaid graph LR sat["SAT
Cook 1971"] sat3["3-SAT"] vc["Vertex Cover"] clique["Clique"] iset["Independent Set"] hcycle["Hamiltonian Cycle"] tspd["TSP Decision"] subsetsum["Subset Sum"] sat --> sat3 sat3 --> vc sat3 --> clique clique --> iset vc --> hcycle hcycle --> tspd sat3 --> subsetsum ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Reduction Chain:** Each arrow $A \to B$ means "$A \leq_p B$ in polynomial time." Following any path gives a complete NP-Completeness proof for the terminal node. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Classify and Define** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **"Define the complexity classes P, NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete. State the relationship between them with the help of a diagram."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **P (Polynomial Time):** A decision problem is in P if it can be solved by a deterministic algorithm in $O(n^k)$ time for some constant $k$. - **NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial):** A decision problem is in NP if any YES instance has a polynomial-length certificate that can be verified in $O(n^k)$ time by a deterministic algorithm. - **NP-Hard:** A problem is NP-Hard if every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to it. NP-Hard problems are not necessarily in NP. - **NP-Complete:** A problem is NP-Complete if it belongs to **both** NP and NP-Hard. - **Relationship:** $P \subseteq NP \subseteq NP\text{-Hard}$, and $NP\text{-Complete} = NP \cap NP\text{-Hard}$. **(Award 3 marks for crisp, correct definitions; partial marks for any two correct.)** --- ### **Question 2: Cook's Theorem** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **"State and explain Cook's Theorem. Why is it considered the foundation of NP-Completeness theory?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Cook's Theorem (1971):** *The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete.* - SAT asks: given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in CNF, is there a truth assignment that makes $\phi$ true? - **SAT ∈ NP:** The truth assignment is the certificate, verifiable in $O(n)$ time by evaluating the formula. - **SAT is NP-Hard:** Cook proved that for *any* problem $\Pi \in NP$ and *any* instance $x$, the entire computation of the nondeterministic polynomial-time verifier can be encoded as a Boolean formula $\phi$ of polynomial size such that $x \in \Pi \iff \phi$ is satisfiable. - **Significance:** It established the *first* NP-Complete problem. Every subsequent NP-Completeness proof reduces SAT (or a known NP-Complete derivative) to the target problem in polynomial time. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): Polynomial-Time Reduction Proof** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` #### **Part (a): (7 marks)** **"Prove that the Vertex Cover problem is NP-Complete by reducing 3-SAT to it."** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Definitions** - **Vertex Cover (VC):** Given $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, does there exist $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \leq k$ such that every edge in $E$ has at least one endpoint in $S$? - **3-SAT:** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in 3-CNF, is there a satisfying assignment? **Step 2: VC ∈ NP** - Certificate: a subset $S \subseteq V$ of size $\leq k$. - Verifier: iterate over all $E$ edges and check that at least one endpoint is in $S$. Takes $O(E + V) = O(n^2)$ time — polynomial. - Hence VC ∈ NP. **(1 mark)** **Step 3: 3-SAT ≤ₚ Vertex Cover** Given a 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with $n$ variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and $m$ clauses $C_1, \ldots, C_m$, each containing exactly 3 literals, construct a graph $G$ as follows: - For each variable $x_i$, create a **pair of vertices** $x_i$ and $\overline{x_i}$ connected by an edge (a *variable gadget*). - For each clause $C_j = (l_{j1} \lor l_{j2} \lor l_{j3})$, create a **triangle** of three vertices, one for each literal in the clause (a *clause gadget*). - For each occurrence of literal $l$ in clause $C_j$, add an edge between the literal vertex in the variable gadget and the corresponding literal vertex in the clause gadget. Set the cover bound: $$k = n + 2m$$ **Step 4: Equivalence Proof (sketch)** - The variable gadget edges force at least one of $\{x_i, \overline{x_i}\}$ to be in any cover → at least $n$ vertices must be chosen from variable gadgets. - The clause gadget triangles force at least 2 of the 3 vertices to be in any cover → at least $2m$ vertices from clause gadgets. - Total minimum cover size = $n + 2m = k$. A cover of exactly size $k$ exists iff a satisfying truth assignment exists. **Step 5: Time Complexity of Construction** - Number of vertices: $2n + 3m$. - Number of edges: at most $n + 3m + 3 \times (\text{occurrences}) = O(nm)$. - Construction runs in $O(nm)$ — polynomial. **(1 mark)** **Step 6: Conclusion** $$\phi \in 3\text{-SAT} \iff G \text{ has a vertex cover of size } k = n + 2m$$ Since 3-SAT is NP-Complete and 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Vertex Cover, and VC ∈ NP, **Vertex Cover is NP-Complete.** $\blacksquare$ **(2 marks for synthesis and conclusion)** --- #### **Part (b): (7 marks)** **"Explain the differences between NP-Hard and NP-Complete problems. Give two examples of problems that are NP-Hard but not NP-Complete."** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | NP-Complete | NP-Hard | |---|---|---| | **Membership in NP** | Must be in NP | Need NOT be in NP | | **Verification** | Has a polynomial-time verifier | May have no polynomial-time verifier | | **Decidability** | Always decidable (a YES/NO answer exists) | May be **undecidable** | | **Reductions** | All NP problems reduce to it | All NP problems reduce to it | | **Example** | SAT, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle | Halting Problem, Optimization-TSP | **Key Differences (3 marks):** 1. **NP-Complete requires the problem to be in NP** (verifiable), whereas NP-Hard has no such membership requirement. 2. **NP-Complete problems are always decidable**; NP-Hard problems may be **undecidable** (e.g., the Halting Problem). 3. **NP-Complete problems have a YES/NO answer** that can be at least verified; NP-Hard problems may not even be decision problems (e.g., they might be optimization or function problems). **Two Examples of NP-Hard but NOT NP-Complete (4 marks):** 1. **The Halting Problem:** Given a program $P$ and input $I$, does $P$ halt on $I$? - **NP-Hard:** All NP problems reduce to it (a hypothetical polynomial-time halting solver would solve any decidable problem via reduction). - **Not in NP:** It is undecidable by Turing's proof; hence not in NP and not NP-Complete. 2. **The Optimization Version of the Travelling Salesman Problem (Min-TSP):** Find the *minimum* tour length. - **NP-Hard:** The decision version is NP-Complete, and the optimization version is at least as hard. - **Not in NP:** Optimization problems are not decision problems, so the "certificate" framework of NP does not directly apply. They are not in NP (although they belong to the related class NPO). **(Full 7 marks for: clear comparison table + 2 correct examples with justification.)** --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` #### **Part (a): (7 marks)** **"Show that the Hamiltonian Cycle problem is NP-Complete by reducing Vertex Cover to it."** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Problem Definitions (1 mark)** - **Hamiltonian Cycle (HC):** Given $G = (V, E)$, is there a cycle that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start? - **Vertex Cover (VC):** Given $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, does a vertex cover of size $\leq k$ exist? **Step 2: HC ∈ NP (1 mark)** - Certificate: a sequence of $\vert V \vert$ vertices. - Verifier: check that the sequence is a permutation of $V$, consecutive vertices are connected by an edge, and the last connects back to the first. Time: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, polynomial. **Step 3: Construction (3 marks)** Given instance $(G = (V, E), k)$ of VC, construct $G'$ as follows: 1. For each edge $e_i = (u, v) \in E$, create an **edge gadget**: a small structure of 12 vertices forming a "diamond" that can be traversed in a Hamiltonian manner only if either $u$ or $v$ is "selected" in the cover. 2. The edge gadgets are connected in series by **connector vertices** to force a single Hamiltonian cycle. 3. Add **$k$ selection vertices**, each connected to the edge gadgets in a way that simulates "choosing" which endpoint of each edge covers it. The full construction is technical (Karp's original 1972 reduction) and the resulting graph $G'$ has $O(k \cdot \vert E \vert)$ vertices and edges — **polynomial in the input size**. **Step 4: Equivalence (1.5 marks)** $$(G, k) \in \text{VC} \iff G' \in \text{HC}$$ - **⇒:** A vertex cover of size $k$ defines a Hamiltonian cycle by traversing the edge gadgets in the order of the selected vertices. - **⇐:** A Hamiltonian cycle in $G'$ must "choose" $k$ vertices to traverse all edge gadgets, forming a vertex cover of size $k$ in $G$. **Step 5: Conclusion (0.5 marks)** Since VC is NP-Complete (proven from 3-SAT), VC $\leq_p$ HC, and HC ∈ NP, **HC is NP-Complete.** $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Part (b): (7 marks)** **"Discuss the significance of the P = NP problem. Explain what would happen in three real-world domains if P = NP were proven true."** **Model Solution:** **Significance of P = NP (3 marks):** 1. It is one of the **seven Millennium Prize Problems** announced by the Clay Mathematics Institute in 2000, with a **$1,000,000 prize** for a correct proof. 2. It would resolve a fundamental question about the **limits of computation**: are all easily-verified problems also easily-solvable? 3. It would force the **collapse** of the entire polynomial hierarchy (PH) down to P, fundamentally reshaping computational complexity theory. 4. It is a **practical question**, not just a theoretical one — it affects cryptography, optimization, AI, and biology. **Three Real-World Implications if P = NP were proven (4 marks):** | Domain | Consequence of P = NP | |---|---| | **Cryptography & Cybersecurity** | **Total collapse.** Integer factorization, discrete logarithm, and RSA would be broken in polynomial time. Every secure transaction, banking system, and HTTPS connection would be exposed. New cryptographic paradigms (post-quantum, lattice-based) would be **urgently** needed. | | **Operations Research & Logistics** | **Revolution.** Travelling Salesman, Vehicle Routing, Job-Shop Scheduling, and Supply Chain Optimization would all become efficiently solvable. Last-mile delivery, airline scheduling, and global logistics would achieve **provably optimal** solutions. | | **Artificial Intelligence & Drug Discovery** | **Transformative.** Protein folding, automated theorem proving, code generation, and combinatorial drug design would become tractable. Many AI "search" problems (e.g., SAT-based planning) would yield to efficient algorithms, accelerating scientific discovery. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating NP and NP-Complete.** NP is a *class of verifiable problems*; NP-Complete is the *hardest* subset. Many students write "SAT is NP" (correct) and "SAT is NP-Complete" (correct) but use them interchangeably — examiners deduct 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the certificate in NP proofs.** When showing $A \in NP$, you MUST name the certificate and the verifier. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Direction of reduction confusion.** Reducing "X to Y" means solving X using Y, NOT the other way. Many students reverse this and lose 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions. > 4. **Calling NP-Hard = NP-Complete.** NP-Hard problems may not be in NP (e.g., Halting Problem). This conflation is the single most common 14-mark error. > 5. **No mention of Cook's Theorem** in any NP-Completeness proof. Even when reducing 3-SAT to X, you must justify that 3-SAT is itself NP-Complete *via Cook's Theorem*. Examiners expect this foundation. > 6. **Missing polynomial-time bound on the reduction function $f$.** A correct reduction MUST show that $f$ runs in polynomial time. A correct equivalence without the time bound is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read Before Every Exam** - [ ] **P** = problems **solvable** in polynomial time. - [ ] **NP** = problems whose YES instances are **verifiable** in polynomial time using a certificate. - [ ] **NP-Hard** = problems to which every NP problem polynomial-time reduces (need not be in NP). - [ ] **NP-Complete** = NP $\cap$ NP-Hard (must satisfy **both** conditions). - [ ] **Trivial inclusion:** $P \subseteq NP$ (always true). - [ ] **Open question:** Is $NP \subseteq P$? (i.e., is $P = NP$?) - [ ] **Cook's Theorem (1971):** SAT is the **first** NP-Complete problem. - [ ] To prove $\Pi$ is NP-Complete, you need **two proofs:** (i) $\Pi \in NP$ and (ii) a known NP-Complete problem $A$ satisfies $A \leq_p \Pi$. - [ ] A **reduction** $A \leq_p B$ is a polynomial-time function $f$ such that $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$. - [ ] The **reduction direction** is critical: $A \leq_p B$ means "use B to solve A." - [ ] Common reduction chain: **SAT → 3-SAT → Vertex Cover → Hamiltonian Cycle → TSP-decision**. - [ ] The **Halting Problem** is NP-Hard but **not** NP-Complete (it is undecidable, hence not in NP). - [ ] **Optimization problems are not in NP** directly — convert to decision form first by introducing a bound $K$. - [ ] Polynomial-time verification requires both **polynomial certificate length** AND **polynomial verification time**. - [ ] If $P = NP$, then **cryptography breaks**, **logistics becomes optimal**, and **AI accelerates dramatically**. - [ ] The **certificate** in NP is a *proposed solution*, and the **verifier** is a polynomial-time algorithm that checks it. - [ ] **Independent Set and Clique are complements** — Independent Set in $G$ = Clique in $\overline{G}$. - [ ] **2-SAT is in P** (solved using implication graphs and SCCs in linear time), but **3-SAT is NP-Complete** — this is the classic P-vs-NP-Complete boundary. - [ ] Polynomial time bound is denoted $O(n^k)$ where $k$ is a **non-negative integer constant**, NOT a function of $n$.

topics.title: NP-Completeness Proof - Clique Problem and Vertex Cover Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** NP-Completeness Proof - Clique Problem and Vertex Cover Problem # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Decision Versions (KTU Formal Definitions) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A problem is **NP-Complete** if and only if: > **(i)** It belongs to the class **NP** (verifiable in polynomial time), AND > **(ii)** Every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to it (i.e., it is **NP-Hard**). Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected simple graph with $|V| = n$ vertices and $|E| = m$ edges. ### The CLIQUE Decision Problem $$\text{CLIQUE} = \Big\{\langle G, k \rangle \;:\; G \text{ contains a complete subgraph (clique) on at least } k \text{ vertices}\Big\}$$ A **Clique** $C \subseteq V$ is a subset of vertices such that for **every** pair $u, v \in C$, the edge $(u,v) \in E$. In other words, the induced subgraph $G[C]$ is the complete graph $K_{\vert C \vert}$. ### The VERTEX-COVER Decision Problem $$\text{VERTEX-COVER} = \Big\{\langle G, k \rangle \;:\; G \text{ has a vertex cover of size at most } k \text{ vertices}\Big\}$$ A **Vertex Cover** $S \subseteq V$ is a subset of vertices such that for **every** edge $(u,v) \in E$, at least one endpoint lies in $S$ (i.e., $u \in S \lor v \in S$). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (The "Friend Group" Problem):** > > *Clique = "Tightly-Knit Squad"*: Imagine a class of students. A **clique** is a group where *every* student in the group is friends with *every* other student in the group. Asking "does the class contain a clique of 5?" means: "is there a gang of 5 who are ALL mutual friends?" If you only need 5 people, but the class is huge, finding them by brute force is *very* slow. > > *Vertex Cover = "Watchmen on the Street"*: Imagine a city graph where streets are edges and junctions are vertices. A **vertex cover** of size $k$ is a set of $k$ watchmen placed at junctions such that *every* street has at least one watchman watching it. You don't need a watchman at every junction — just enough to "cover" all streets. The computational hardness lies in the fact that as the graph grows, the number of subsets to check **explodes combinatorially** ($\binom{n}{k}$ possibilities), but no polynomial-time algorithm is known to solve either problem optimally. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Notations | Symbol | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $\omega(G)$ | **Clique Number** — size of the *largest* clique in $G$ | | $\tau(G)$ | **Vertex Cover Number** — size of the *smallest* vertex cover in $G$ | | $\alpha(G)$ | **Independence Number** — size of the *largest* independent set in $G$ | | $\bar{G}$ | **Complement Graph** — same vertices, edges are exactly those *not* in $E$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gallai's Theorem (Fundamental Identity):** > For any graph $G = (V, E)$, the three numbers satisfy: > $$\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = n \quad \text{and equivalently} \quad \omega(G) = \alpha(\bar{G})$$ > These identities are the *cornerstone* of the reduction proofs that follow. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Roadmap: Three Famous Inter-Convertible Problems The trio of problems — **Independent Set (IS)**, **Clique**, and **Vertex Cover (VC)** — are computationally *isomorphic*. Any one of them being solved in polynomial time would collapse NP into P. ### The Equivalence Triangle (Visual Logic) > [!NOTE] > The three problems are connected by a beautiful duality through the **complement graph** $\bar{G}$ and the **Gallai identity** $\alpha + \tau = n$. - A set is an **Independent Set** in $G$ $\iff$ it is a **Clique** in $\bar{G}$. - A set is a **Vertex Cover** in $G$ $\iff$ its complement $V \setminus S$ is an **Independent Set** in $G$. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reduction Logic ### Step 1: Show Membership in NP | Problem | Certificate | Verification Algorithm | Time | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CLIQUE** | Set $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k$ | For every pair $u, v \in C$, check $(u,v) \in E$ | $O(k^2)$ | | **VERTEX-COVER** | Set $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert = k$ | For every edge $(u,v) \in E$, check $u \in S$ or $v \in S$ | $O(m)$ | ### Step 2: Establish NP-Hardness via Polynomial Reduction We will prove NP-hardness by reducing between these three problems and from the canonical NP-complete problem **3-CNF-SAT** (Cook's theorem). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Karp's Reduction Chain (1967-72):** > $$\text{3-SAT} \;\le_p\; \text{INDEPENDENT-SET} \;\le_p\; \text{CLIQUE} \;\le_p\; \text{VERTEX-COVER}$$ The reduction $\text{CLIQUE} \le_p \text{VERTEX-COVER}$ is the *shortest* and most elegant of all NP-completeness proofs. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Theorem / Formula | Statement | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Gallai's Identity** | $\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = n$ | Connects IS and VC | | **Complement Duality** | $C$ is clique in $G \iff C$ is independent set in $\bar{G}$ | Transfers Clique ↔ IS | | **Reduction Cost** | Building $\bar{G}$ takes $O(n^2)$ time | Polynomial bound | | **Verifier (CLIQUE)** | Check $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs in $O(k^2)$ | NP-membership | | **Verifier (VC)** | Check $m$ edges in $O(m)$ | NP-membership | | **3-SAT → CLIQUE** | Build graph with $3k$ vertices (one triangle per clause) | Proves CLIQUE NP-hard | | **Cardinality Tie** | $\tau(\bar{G}) = n - \omega(G)$ | Direct from Gallai | | **Set Complement Tie** | $S$ is VC of $G \iff V \setminus S$ is IS of $G$ | Foundational lemma | > [!NOTE] > **No code is required for these proofs** — they are pure graph-theoretic logical reductions and are valued highly in KTU's theory-oriented paper pattern. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Applications of these NP-Complete Problems:** > - **Bioinformatics:** Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) networks — finding maximal cliques reveals protein complexes. > - **VLSI Design:** Vertex Cover models minimum *fault-tolerant* routing nodes that monitor every wire. > - **Social Networks:** Marketing firms search for "influencer cliques" in Twitter/Facebook graphs. > - **Cybersecurity:** Identifying minimum sets of sensors (vertex cover) to monitor every communication link. > - **Compiler Optimization:** Register allocation is reducible to graph coloring, which is the complement of clique covering. # 3. Step-by-Step Proofs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 THEOREM 1: CLIQUE is NP-Complete ### Part (a): CLIQUE $\in$ NP **Statement:** The CLIQUE decision problem belongs to class NP. **Proof by Certificate (Exhaustive):** A polynomial-time verifier $V$ for CLIQUE takes two inputs: 1. A certificate (a hypothesized solution) — a set $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k$. 2. The instance $\langle G, k \rangle$ itself. The verifier executes the following deterministic algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Algorithm Verify\_Clique}(G, k, C): \\ &1. \text{ If } |C| \neq k, \text{ return REJECT.} \\ &2. \text{ For each pair } (u, v) \in C \times C \text{ with } u \neq v: \\ &3. \quad \text{ If } (u, v) \notin E, \text{ return REJECT.} \\ &4. \text{ Return ACCEPT.} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step analysis:** - **Line 1:** Checks size of certificate matches $k$. Trivially $O(1)$. - **Line 2-3:** Iterates over all $\binom{k}{2} = \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}$ unordered pairs. For each, performs an $O(1)$ adjacency lookup (or matrix check). Total cost: $O(k^2)$. - **Line 4:** Accepts only if all pairs are mutually connected. Since the running time is $O(k^2)$, which is polynomial in the size of the input $\langle G, k \rangle$, CLIQUE is in NP. $\blacksquare$ ### Part (b): CLIQUE is NP-Hard (via 3-SAT $\le_p$ CLIQUE) **Reduction Construction:** Given a 3-CNF Boolean formula $\phi$ with $k$ clauses $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$, each of the form $C_i = (l_1^i \lor l_2^i \lor l_3^i)$: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Construct graph } G_\phi = (V_\phi, E_\phi) \text{ as follows:} \\ &\bullet \; V_\phi = \{v_{i,j} \mid 1 \le i \le k,\; 1 \le j \le 3\} \quad (\text{3 vertices per clause}) \\ &\bullet \; E_\phi = \Big\{(v_{i,p},\, v_{j,q}) \;\Big|\; i \ne j \text{ and } l_p^i \ne \neg l_q^j \Big\} \end{aligned} $$ **Reading the formula:** Two vertices are connected by an edge if and only if they belong to **different clauses** AND their literals are **not negations of each other** (i.e., they are simultaneously satisfiable). **Time Analysis:** $G_\phi$ has $3k$ vertices and at most $3k(k-1)$ edges. Construction is $O(k^2)$ — polynomial. ### Claim (Correctness of Reduction) $$\phi \text{ is satisfiable} \;\iff\; G_\phi \text{ has a clique of size } k$$ **Proof (⇒) — Satisfiable implies Clique exists:** Suppose $\phi$ has a satisfying assignment $\sigma$. For each clause $C_i$, since $\sigma$ satisfies $\phi$, at least one literal in $C_i$ evaluates to TRUE under $\sigma$. Pick exactly one such literal and call the corresponding vertex $v_i^* \in V_\phi$. Consider the chosen set $S = \{v_1^*, v_2^*, \ldots, v_k^*\}$. We claim $S$ is a clique: - For any two distinct $v_i^*, v_j^* \in S$, they lie in **different clauses** $C_i$ and $C_j$ by construction. - Both literals are TRUE under the same assignment $\sigma$, so neither can be the negation of the other. - Therefore $(v_i^*, v_j^*) \in E_\phi$ by the construction rule. So $S$ is a $k$-clique. $\checkmark$ **Proof (⇐) — Clique exists implies Satisfiable:** Suppose $G_\phi$ contains a clique $C$ of size $k$. Since the maximum size of any independent set of vertices within a single clause is 1 (no edges exist between vertices in the same clause... *actually vertices of the same clause form a triangle, but we must select at most one* — note: the construction typically does NOT add edges within the same clause to enforce this). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Variant:** In the most common textbook version (Karp, CLRS), no edges are added between vertices of the same clause. This forces the clique to select at most one vertex per clause, and since $\vert C \vert = k$ and there are $k$ clauses, the clique picks **exactly one** vertex from **each** clause. Therefore $C$ contains exactly one vertex $v_i^*$ from each clause $C_i$. Define an assignment $\sigma$ by setting the literal corresponding to $v_i^*$ to TRUE (and other variables arbitrarily). For any clause $C_i$, the selected literal is true, so $\sigma$ satisfies $\phi$. $\checkmark$ Since both directions hold, 3-SAT $\le_p$ CLIQUE in polynomial time, so CLIQUE is NP-hard. $\blacksquare$ **Combining (a) + (b):** CLIQUE is in NP and NP-hard, therefore **CLIQUE is NP-Complete.** $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 THEOREM 2: VERTEX-COVER is NP-Complete ### Part (a): VERTEX-COVER $\in$ NP **Verifier $V$ for VC:** Input: certificate $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert = k$, plus instance $\langle G, k \rangle$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Algorithm Verify\_VC}(G, k, S): \\ &1. \text{ If } |S| \neq k, \text{ return REJECT.} \\ &2. \text{ For each edge } (u, v) \in E: \\ &3. \quad \text{ If } u \notin S \text{ and } v \notin S, \text{ return REJECT.} \\ &4. \text{ Return ACCEPT.} \end{aligned} $$ **Analysis:** Step 3 iterates over all $m = \vert E \vert$ edges. Each iteration is $O(1)$. Total cost: $O(m)$, which is polynomial. Hence VC $\in$ NP. $\blacksquare$ ### Part (b): VERTEX-COVER is NP-Hard (via CLIQUE $\le_p$ VERTEX-COVER) This is the **cleanest** NP-completeness reduction in the entire syllabus. **Reduction Function $f$:** $$ f: \langle G = (V, E),\; k \rangle \;\longmapsto\; \langle \bar{G} = (V, \bar{E}),\; n - k \rangle $$ where $\bar{E} = \{(u,v) \in V \times V \mid u \ne v \text{ and } (u,v) \notin E\}$ is the complement edge set. **Construction Time:** Computing $\bar{G}$ requires inspecting all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs — $O(n^2)$ time. Polynomial. $\checkmark$ ### Core Lemma (The Heart of the Proof) > [!IMPORTANT] > **LEMMA:** $G$ has a clique of size $k$ $\iff$ $\bar{G}$ has a vertex cover of size $n - k$. **Proof of (⇒):** Assume $G$ has a clique $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k$. Define $S = V \setminus C$, so $\vert S \vert = n - k$. **Claim:** $S$ is a vertex cover of $\bar{G}$. Take any edge $(u, v) \in \bar{E}$. This means $(u, v) \notin E$ in the original graph $G$. We must show at least one of $u, v$ belongs to $S = V \setminus C$. Suppose, for contradiction, that $u \notin S$ and $v \notin S$. Then $u \in C$ and $v \in C$. But $C$ is a clique, so $(u, v)$ must be in $E$ — contradiction. Therefore $u \in S$ or $v \in S$, and $S$ covers the arbitrary edge $(u, v)$. Since $(u,v)$ was arbitrary, $S$ covers every edge of $\bar{G}$. $\checkmark$ **Proof of (⇐):** Assume $\bar{G}$ has a vertex cover $S$ with $\vert S \vert = n - k$. Define $C = V \setminus S$, so $\vert C \vert = k$. **Claim:** $C$ is a clique in $G$. Take any two distinct vertices $u, v \in C$. Then $u \notin S$ and $v \notin S$. Suppose, for contradiction, that $(u, v) \notin E$. Then $(u, v) \in \bar{E}$ (complement edge). Since $S$ is a vertex cover of $\bar{G}$, at least one of $u, v$ must lie in $S$ — but both are in $C = V \setminus S$. Contradiction. Therefore $(u, v) \in E$, and $C$ is a clique of size $k$ in $G$. $\checkmark$ Both directions of the lemma are established, so CLIQUE $\le_p$ VERTEX-COVER holds. $\blacksquare$ ### Final Conclusion $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{VERTEX-COVER} \in \text{NP} \quad \text{(Part a)} \\ &\text{CLIQUE} \le_p \text{VERTEX-COVER} \quad \text{(Part b, since CLIQUE is NP-Complete)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{VERTEX-COVER is NP-Hard} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{VERTEX-COVER is NP-Complete.} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Applying the Reduction > [!NOTE] > **Example (Karp-Style):** Consider the graph $G$ with $V = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and $E = \{(a,b), (a,c), (b,c), (c,d), (d,e)\}$. Suppose we are given instance $\langle G, k=3 \rangle$ for CLIQUE. **Step 1: Identify a Clique.** Vertices $\{a, b, c\}$ form a triangle — a clique of size 3. So $\omega(G) \ge 3$. **Step 2: Apply the Reduction.** $n = 5$, so for the VC instance on $\bar{G}$: $k' = n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$. **Step 3: Claim.** $\bar{G}$ has a vertex cover of size 2. By the lemma, $S = V \setminus C = \{d, e\}$ should be a vertex cover of $\bar{G}$. **Step 4: Verify.** The complement edges $\bar{E}$ are: $$\bar{E} = \{(a,d), (a,e), (b,d), (b,e), (c,e)\}$$ Every edge in $\bar{E}$ has at least one endpoint in $\{d, e\}$: - $(a,d) \to d \in S$ ✓ - $(a,e) \to e \in S$ ✓ - $(b,d) \to d \in S$ ✓ - $(b,e) \to e \in S$ ✓ - $(c,e) \to e \in S$ ✓ Confirmed: $S = \{d, e\}$ is a vertex cover of $\bar{G}$ of size $n - k = 2$. $\blacksquare$ # 4.
