KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** ### Q1. Define the CLIQUE problem formally. Show that CLIQUE ∈ NP. (3 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, the CLIQUE problem asks whether $G$ contains a clique of size at least $k$ — i.e., a subset $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert \ge k$ such that every pair of vertices in $C$ is joined by an edge. **NP-Membership:** The certificate is the vertex set $C$ itself. A polynomial-time verifier checks: 1. $\vert C \vert \ge k$ in $O(1)$ time. 2. For every pair $u, v \in C$, verifies $(u,v) \in E$ — costs $O(k^2)$. Total verification time: $O(k^2)$, which is polynomial. Therefore CLIQUE $\in$ NP. $\blacksquare$ --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]** ### Q2. State and prove the relationship between Independent Set, Clique, and Vertex Cover. (3 marks) **Model Answer:** For any graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$: **Theorem:** A subset $S \subseteq V$ is a vertex cover of $G$ if and only if $V \setminus S$ is an independent set of $G$. **Proof:** - (⇒) If $S$ is a VC, then for every edge $(u,v)$, at least one endpoint is in $S$. So no edge has both endpoints in $V \setminus S$, making it independent. - (⇐) If $V \setminus S$ is independent, no edge has both endpoints in $V \setminus S$. So every edge has at least one endpoint in $S$, making $S$ a VC. **Corollary:** $\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = n$, and $\omega(G) = \alpha(\bar{G})$ (a clique in $G$ is an independent set in $\bar{G}$). $\blacksquare$ --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > *(Note: KTU Part B offers internal choice — student attempts ONE of Q11A or Q11B)* --- ### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q11A.** (a) Prove that the **CLIQUE problem is NP-Complete** by reducing from 3-CNF-SAT. (7 marks)       (b) Hence prove that the **VERTEX-COVER problem is NP-Complete**. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Q11A(a): **Step 1 — CLIQUE ∈ NP:** Certificate is the vertex set $C$. Verifier checks all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs are edges in $O(k^2)$ time. (Marks: 1) **Step 2 — Reduction 3-SAT ≤ₚ CLIQUE:** Given 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with $k$ clauses, construct $G_\phi$ with $3k$ vertices, one per literal in each clause. Place edge between $v_{i,p}$ and $v_{j,q}$ (for $i \ne j$) iff literal $l_p^i$ is NOT the negation of $l_q^j$. Construction is $O(k^2)$. (Marks: 2) **Step 3 — Correctness (⇒):** Given satisfying assignment $\sigma$, pick one true literal per clause. The $k$ chosen vertices form a clique since no two can be complementary (both true). (Marks: 2) **Step 4 — Correctness (⇐):** Given a $k$-clique in $G_\phi$, it must contain exactly one vertex per clause (no edges within a clause). Set those literals TRUE — this satisfies $\phi$. (Marks: 2) **Conclusion:** CLIQUE is NP-Hard + in NP = NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$ #### Model Solution for Q11A(b): **Step 1 — VC ∈ NP:** Certificate is the set $S$. Verifier checks every edge has an endpoint in $S$ in $O(m)$ time. (Marks: 1) **Step 2 — Reduction CLIQUE ≤ₚ VC:** Map instance $\langle G, k \rangle$ to $\langle \bar{G}, n-k \rangle$. Construction is $O(n^2)$. (Marks: 1) **Step 3 — Lemma:** $G$ has a $k$-clique $\iff$ $\bar{G}$ has a $(n-k)$-vertex cover. (Marks: 1) **Step 4 — Proof of (⇒):** Let $C$ be a $k$-clique in $G$. Set $S = V \setminus C$ with $\vert S \vert = n - k$. For any $(u,v) \in \bar{E}$ (meaning $(u,v) \notin E$), at least one of $u, v \notin C$ (else $C$ wouldn't be a clique). So $S$ covers $\bar{E}$. (Marks: 2) **Step 5 — Proof of (⇐):** Let $S$ be a $(n-k)$-VC of $\bar{G}$. Set $C = V \setminus S$ with $\vert C \vert = k$. For any distinct $u, v \in C$, if $(u,v) \notin E$, then $(u,v) \in \bar{E}$ and $S$ must cover it — contradiction. So $C$ is a $k$-clique in $G$. (Marks: 2) **Conclusion:** VC is NP-Hard + in NP = NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$ --- ### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **Q11B.** (a) Define the VERTEX-COVER problem. Show that it belongs to the class NP and outline a polynomial-time reduction from CLIQUE to VERTEX-COVER. (7 marks)       (b) Consider a graph $G$ with $V = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ and $E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5), (5,6), (4,6)\}$. Determine the clique number $\omega(G)$ and the vertex cover number $\tau(\bar{G})$. Show all working using the reduction. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Q11B(a): **Definition:** Given $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, VERTEX-COVER asks if $G$ has a set $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \le k$ such that every edge $(u,v) \in E$ has $u \in S$ or $v \in S$. (Marks: 1) **NP-Membership:** Certificate = set $S$. Verifier checks: - $\vert S \vert \le k$ in $O(1)$ time. - For each of the $m$ edges, check at least one endpoint lies in $S$ in $O(m)$ time. Total: $O(m)$ — polynomial. So VC $\in$ NP. (Marks: 2) **Reduction Outline:** Map $\langle G, k \rangle$ (CLIQUE instance) to $\langle \bar{G}, n-k \rangle$ (VC instance). (Marks: 1) **Correctness Sketch:** $C$ is a $k$-clique in $G$ $\iff$ $V \setminus C$ is a $(n-k)$-VC in $\bar{G}$. (Marks: 2) **Polynomial Time:** Computing $\bar{G}$ requires $O(n^2)$ edge inspections. Valid. (Marks: 1) #### Model Solution for Q11B(b): **Step 1 — Find the Clique in G:** Inspect edges: vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$ are all mutually connected $(1,2), (1,3), (2,3) \in E$. This is a triangle = clique of size 3. Check if size 4 possible: need 4 vertices all mutually adjacent. Adding vertex 4: edge $(2,4) \notin E$ — fails. So $\omega(G) = 3$. (Marks: 2) **Step 2 — Apply Reduction:** Since $n = 6$ and $k = 3$, the VC instance on $\bar{G}$ asks for a vertex cover of size $n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$. (Marks: 1) **Step 3 — Find the VC in $\bar{G}$:** By the lemma, $S = V \setminus C = \{4, 5, 6\}$ should be a VC of $\bar{G}$ of size 3. (Marks: 1) **Step 4 — Verification:** Compute $\bar{E} = V \times V \setminus \{(v,v)\} \setminus E$. The missing edges of $G$ form $\bar{E}$. Verify each edge in $\bar{E}$ touches $\{4, 5, 6\}$: - $(1,4) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 4 ✓ - $(1,5) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 5 ✓ - $(1,6) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 6 ✓ - $(2,4) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 4 ✓ - $(2,5) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 5 ✓ - $(2,6) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 6 ✓ - $(3,5) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 5 ✓ - $(3,6) \in \bar{E}$: vertex 6 ✓ All covered. So $\tau(\bar{G}) = 3 = n - \omega(G)$. (Marks: 3) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Certificate Confusion:** Students often forget to mention the *certificate* (the subset $C$ or $S$) when proving NP-membership. Always state: "A verifier, given the certificate, runs in polynomial time." **(−1 to −2 marks if omitted)** > > 2. **Direction of Reduction:** Writing "CLIQUE ≤ₚ VERTEX-COVER" means: given a CLIQUE instance, build a VERTEX-COVER instance. The direction of implication in the lemma must match. **(−2 marks if reversed)** > > 3. **Forgetting the "Set Complement" Step:** The reduction $C \to S = V \setminus C$ is the critical insight. Without it, you cannot bridge the sizes ($k$ to $n-k$). **(−2 marks)** > > 4. **Not Showing Polynomial Time:** KTU specifically awards marks for verifying the construction is polynomial. Always add: "This construction runs in $O(n^2)$ time, hence is polynomial." **(−1 mark)** > > 5. **Skipping the ⇐ Direction:** A common student error is proving only one direction of the equivalence. Both directions are required. **(−2 to −3 marks)** > > 6. **Mixing up the Gallai Identity:** $\alpha + \tau = n$ does NOT directly give $\omega + \tau = n$. The correct identity is $\omega(\bar{G}) + \tau(\bar{G}) = n$, or equivalently $\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = n$. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - 🎯 **CLIQUE** = complete subgraph. **Decision form**: does $G$ have a $k$-clique? **Verifier time**: $O(k^2)$. - 🎯 **VERTEX-COVER** = set $S$ covering all edges. **Decision form**: is there a VC of size $\le k$? **Verifier time**: $O(m)$. - 🎯 Both problems are **NP-Complete** (Karp, 1972; in the original 21 NP-complete problems). - 🎯 The **canonical reduction** for both NP-hardness proofs: - CLIQUE NP-hard $\Leftarrow$ 3-SAT $\le_p$ CLIQUE (build graph with 3 vertices per clause, edges between non-contradictory literals). - VC NP-hard $\Leftarrow$ CLIQUE $\le_p$ VC (use complement graph + set complement). - 🎯 **Gallai's Identity:** $\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = \vert V \vert = n$. **Remember:** applies to the *same* graph, not complement. - 🎯 **Complement Duality:** $C$ is clique in $G$ $\iff$ $C$ is independent set in $\bar{G}$. - 🎯 **Reduction Formula:** $\omega(G) + \tau(\bar{G}) = n$ (direct consequence of Gallai applied to $\bar{G}$). - 🎯 **Certificate = Verifier paradigm** is the textbook definition of class NP — never omit it. - 🎯 **Polynomial time** for reduction construction must be explicitly stated and bounded. - 🎯 The KTU 14-mark questions on this topic almost always split as **(a) NP-membership (3-4 marks) + (b) NP-hardness reduction (10-11 marks)**. - 🎯 The standard KTU question pattern asks to prove *one* of the two NP-complete; expect either Clique or Vertex Cover, with the other given as a tool. - 🎯 Always provide a **concrete worked example** in Part B — it demonstrates mastery and usually earns full method marks. - 🎯 The triangle-reduces-to-clique trick is unique to the 3-SAT → CLIQUE reduction; do not confuse it with the simpler CLIQUE → VC reduction. - 🎯 Final answer: **Always end with a boxed conclusion** like "Therefore VERTEX-COVER is NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$" — this signals completion to the examiner.

topics.title: Approximation Algorithms - Bin Packing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approximation Algorithms - Bin Packing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS - BIN PACKING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Bin Packing Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIN PACKING PROBLEM (BPP) — KTU 2024 Canonical Statement**
> Given a finite set of items $I = \{i_1, i_2, i_3, \ldots, i_n\}$ with associated sizes $s(i_k)$ such that $0 < s(i_k) \leq 1$ for every item, and an infinite supply of unit-capacity bins (each of capacity $C = 1$), the **Bin Packing Problem** asks for the **minimum number of bins** required to pack all the items such that no item is split across bins and the total size of items in any bin does not exceed 1.

Formally, we wish to minimize:

$$
B_{\text{OPT}} = \min \left\{ m \;\bigg|\; \text{partition } I \text{ into } m \text{ subsets } S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m \text{ with } \sum_{i \in S_j} s(i) \leq 1, \forall j \right\}
$$

The problem is a classic **NP-Hard Combinatorial Optimization Problem**. The decision version (Can we pack all items in at most $k$ bins?) is NP-Complete. Hence, exact polynomial-time algorithms are not expected to exist unless **P = NP**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):**
> Bin Packing is listed under "Approximation Algorithms" alongside Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), Set Cover, and Vertex Cover. Students must be able to: (1) state the problem, (2) describe the classical online algorithms (Next-Fit, First-Fit, Best-Fit), (3) state and prove their approximation ratios, and (4) describe the offline First-Fit Decreasing (FFD) algorithm.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Packing a Moving Truck**
> Imagine you have a set of cardboard boxes of different sizes and an infinite supply of identical moving-trucks, each with a fixed loading capacity. You want to load all boxes using the *fewest possible trucks*. A bin is a truck, and items are the boxes. Items cannot be broken apart, and the total weight in any truck must not exceed the truck's capacity. Since trying every possible combination of trucks is impossibly slow for many boxes, you use a *smart heuristic* (approximation algorithm) that almost always gives a near-optimal answer very quickly.

**Three key engineering applications:**

- **Logistics & Shipping** : Filling containers and cargo holds to minimize cost.
- **Cloud Computing** : Allocating virtual machines (VMs) of various sizes to physical servers.
- **Memory Management** : Allocating memory blocks of variable size to fixed-size memory pages.
- **Cutting Stock Problem** : Cutting pieces of required sizes from stock material of standard length.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Bin Capacity** | $C = 1$ (without loss of generality after scaling) |
| **Optimal Solution** | The minimum number of bins needed, denoted $B_{\text{OPT}}$ or simply $\text{OPT}$ |
| **Approximation Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{B_{\text{ALG}}}{B_{\text{OPT}}}$, where $B_{\text{ALG}}$ is the bins used by our algorithm |
| **Online Algorithm** | Items are processed one at a time in arrival order, no knowledge of future items |
| **Offline Algorithm** | All items are known in advance; the list can be pre-sorted before packing |
| **Asymptotic PTAS (APTAS)** | Algorithm whose ratio approaches $1 + \varepsilon$ as $\text{OPT} \to \infty$ |
| **Asymptotic FPTAS** | Fully polynomial-time APTAS |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bin Packing Algorithms

Bin packing algorithms are broadly classified into two families based on the knowledge of the input:

### A. Online Algorithms (No Pre-Sorting Allowed)
The algorithm sees items one by one and must decide immediately. The classical online algorithms are:

1. **Next-Fit (NF)**
2. **First-Fit (FF)**
3. **Best-Fit (BF)**
4. **Worst-Fit (WF)** (less common in KTU syllabus)

### B. Offline Algorithms (Items May Be Pre-Sorted)
1. **First-Fit Decreasing (FFD)** — sorts items in non-increasing order, then applies First-Fit.
2. **Best-Fit Decreasing (BFD)** — sorts items, then applies Best-Fit.

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 — NEXT-FIT (NF) — The Simplest Online Heuristic

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **NEXT-FIT RULE:** Keep exactly ONE open bin at any time. Place the next item into the currently open bin. If it does not fit, **close the current bin permanently**, open a new empty bin, and place the item into it.

This is the *greedy online* algorithm. It has a very fast $O(n)$ time complexity but pays for its simplicity with a relatively poor worst-case approximation.

### 2.2.2 Pseudocode (Exam-Friendly Format)

```
ALGORITHM NextFit(items[1..n])
1.  open a new bin (bin 1)
2.  current_bin ← 1
3.  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
4.      IF items[i] fits in current_bin THEN
5.          place items[i] in current_bin
6.      ELSE
7.          current_bin ← current_bin + 1
8.          place items[i] in current_bin
9.  END IF
10. END FOR
11. RETURN current_bin
```

### 2.2.3 Approximation Ratio of Next-Fit

**Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *The Next-Fit algorithm has an asymptotic approximation ratio of exactly 2, i.e.,* $B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}}$.

**Proof Idea (Standard KTU Pattern):** Consider the bins opened by Next-Fit. Let $B_j$ and $B_{j+1}$ be two *consecutive* bins opened by NF. Since the first item placed in $B_{j+1}$ did not fit into $B_j$, the total size of items in $B_j \cup B_{j+1}$ exceeds 1. Formally:

$$
s(B_j) + s(B_{j+1}) > 1
$$

where $s(B_j)$ denotes the total size of items in bin $B_j$. Hence, for any pair of consecutive bins, the **combined** content exceeds 1. So the average content per bin is more than $1/2$. This immediately gives:

$$
\frac{\text{Total size of all items}}{B_{\text{NF}}/2} > 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_{\text{NF}} < 2 \cdot (\text{Total size}) + 1
$$

Since $\text{Total size} \leq B_{\text{OPT}}$, we get $B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}} + 1$. The asymptotic ratio is therefore exactly 2.

### 2.2.4 Tight Example for Next-Fit

> [!TIP]
> **Worst-Case Instance for NF (Important for KTU):**
> Take $n$ items each of size $\frac{1}{2} + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon \to 0$. Then:
> - **Optimal:** Each bin holds 1 item → $\text{OPT} = n$ bins.
> - **Next-Fit:** Places item 1 in bin 1, item 2 doesn't fit (bin 1 has $\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon$), so it goes to bin 2, item 3 goes to bin 3, etc. So NF uses $n$ bins — actually matches optimal here.
>
> The **asymptotic** 2-approximation is tight: for $n$ items of size $\frac{1}{2}$ alternating with $\frac{1}{2} + \varepsilon$, NF uses $\approx n$ bins while OPT uses $\approx n/2$ bins (since two items of $\frac{1}{2}$ fit in one bin).

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 — FIRST-FIT (FF)

### 2.3.1 Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **FIRST-FIT RULE:** Keep ALL previously opened bins open. For each new item, scan the existing bins **in order** (1, 2, 3, ...) and place the item into the **first bin** that has enough remaining space. If no existing bin can hold it, open a new bin.

Unlike Next-Fit, First-Fit never closes a bin, so it has access to *all* previously opened bins when deciding where to place an item.

### 2.3.2 Pseudocode (Exam-Friendly Format)

```
ALGORITHM FirstFit(items[1..n])
1.  bins[] ← empty list of bins
2.  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
3.      placed ← FALSE
4.      FOR each bin b in bins[] (in order 1, 2, ...) DO
5.          IF items[i] fits in bin b THEN
6.              place items[i] in bin b
7.              placed ← TRUE
8.              BREAK
9.          END IF
10.     END FOR
11.     IF placed = FALSE THEN
12.         open new bin, add to bins[]
13.         place items[i] in new bin
14.     END IF
15. END FOR
16. RETURN number of bins
```

### 2.3.3 Time Complexity of First-Fit

For each of the $n$ items, FF may scan up to $B_{\text{FF}}$ bins. Therefore:

$$
T(n) = O(n \cdot B_{\text{FF}}) = O(n^2) \text{ in the worst case}
$$

(With clever data structures, this can be reduced to $O(n \log n)$.)

### 2.3.4 Approximation Ratio of First-Fit

**Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *The First-Fit algorithm has an asymptotic approximation ratio of exactly $\frac{17}{10} = 1.7$.* That is,

$$
B_{\text{FF}} \leq \left\lceil 1.7 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}} \right\rceil
$$

In practice, FF never uses more than $\frac{17}{10}\text{OPT} + 2$ bins.

**Proof Idea:** The proof is non-trivial. The classical proof by Johnson (1973) and later refined by Dósa and others works by considering an **invariant** on adjacent bins in the FF packing: if $B_i$ and $B_{i+1}$ are two consecutive FF bins, then $s(B_i) + s(B_{i+1}) > 1$ must hold (otherwise the second bin would never have been opened). This is similar to NF. By careful case analysis, the 1.7 bound emerges.

### 2.3.5 Comparison Summary Table

| Property | Next-Fit (NF) | First-Fit (FF) |
|---|---|---|
| Bins kept open | Only 1 (the last) | All bins |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ worst-case |
| Approximation ratio | 2 | 1.7 |
| Quality | Worse | Better |
| Simplicity | Very simple | Simple |

## 2.4 Algorithm 3 — BEST-FIT (BF)

### 2.4.1 Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **BEST-FIT RULE:** Like FF, all bins stay open. For each new item, scan all existing bins and place it into the bin where it leaves the **least empty space** (i.e., the bin with the largest current load that can still accommodate the item). If no bin fits, open a new bin.

### 2.4.2 Heuristic Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Best-Fit"?** By picking the *tightest* fit, BF attempts to leave large gaps in other bins, which are then hopefully filled by future large items. In practice, BF and FF use the same number of bins, but BF has a slightly higher time cost (must scan all bins, not just stop at the first fit).

### 2.4.3 Approximation Ratio of Best-Fit

**Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *Best-Fit also has an asymptotic approximation ratio of at most $\frac{17}{10} = 1.7$, i.e., the same as First-Fit.* There is no asymptotic improvement over FF, although it may perform better on specific instances.

## 2.5 Algorithm 4 — FIRST-FIT DECREASING (FFD) — The Standard Offline Algorithm

### 2.5.1 Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRST-FIT DECREASING (FFD) RULE:**
> 1. **Sort** the items in non-increasing order of size: $s_1 \geq s_2 \geq \ldots \geq s_n$.
> 2. Apply the **First-Fit** algorithm to this sorted list.

This is an *offline* algorithm because the items are sorted (we need to know all items in advance). The intuition is that large items should be packed first, leaving only small gaps for small items.

### 2.5.2 Approximation Ratio of FFD

**Theorem (Dósa, 2007 — KTU 2024 Result):** *The asymptotic approximation ratio of First-Fit Decreasing is exactly:*

$$
B_{\text{FFD}} \leq \frac{11}{9} \cdot B_{\text{OPT}} + \frac{6}{9}
$$

Or equivalently: $B_{\text{FFD}} \leq \left\lceil \frac{11}{9}\,B_{\text{OPT}} \right\rceil$.

This bound is **tight**: there exist instances for which FFD achieves exactly $\frac{11}{9}\,\text{OPT} + 1$ bins. Before Dósa's 2007 proof, a slightly weaker bound of $\frac{11}{9}\,\text{OPT} + 1$ was conjectured (proved by Simchi-Levi in 1994).

### 2.5.3 Pseudocode for FFD

```
ALGORITHM FirstFitDecreasing(items[1..n])
1.  Sort items in non-increasing order of size
2.  bins[] ← empty list of bins
3.  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
4.      placed ← FALSE
5.      FOR each bin b in bins[] (in order 1, 2, ...) DO
6.          IF items[i] fits in bin b THEN
7.              place items[i] in bin b
8.              placed ← TRUE
9.              BREAK
10.         END IF
11.     END FOR
12.     IF placed = FALSE THEN
13.         open new bin
14.         place items[i] in new bin
15.     END IF
16. END FOR
17. RETURN number of bins
```

## 2.6 Lower Bound: A Useful Optimality Benchmark

For any bin packing instance, the **sum of all item sizes** is a fundamental lower bound:

$$
\text{LB}_1 = \left\lceil \sum_{i=1}^{n} s(i) \right\rceil
$$

Another useful lower bound (used in proofs) is based on the *number of items larger than 1/2*. Since each bin can hold at most one such item:

$$
\text{LB}_2 = \left| \{i : s(i) > 1/2\} \right|
$$

The actual optimum is bounded below by $\text{LB} = \max(\text{LB}_1, \text{LB}_2, \text{LB}_3, \ldots)$ where $\text{LB}_k$ is the optimal packing of items $> 1/k$.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This for Exams)

| **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Bin Capacity | $C = 1$ | After scaling |
| Approximation Ratio | $\rho = B_{\text{ALG}} \,/\, B_{\text{OPT}}$ | $\rho \geq 1$ always |
| Next-Fit Ratio | $B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}}$ | Asymptotic, tight |
| First-Fit Ratio | $B_{\text{FF}} \leq \frac{17}{10}\,B_{\text{OPT}} + 2$ | Asymptotic, tight |
| Best-Fit Ratio | $B_{\text{BF}} \leq \frac{17}{10}\,B_{\text{OPT}} + 2$ | Same as FF asymptotically |
| FFD Ratio | $B_{\text{FFD}} \leq \frac{11}{9}\,B_{\text{OPT}} + \frac{6}{9}$ | Asymptotic, tight (Dósa 2007) |
| BFD Ratio | $B_{\text{BFD}} \leq \frac{11}{9}\,B_{\text{OPT}} + \frac{6}{9}$ | Same as FFD |
| Total size lower bound | $\text{OPT} \geq \sum s(i)$ | Trivial lower bound |
| Adjacent-bin invariant (NF/FF) | $s(B_j) + s(B_{j+1}) > 1$ | Critical for proofs |
| NF Time complexity | $O(n)$ | Single pass |
| FF / BF Time complexity | $O(n^2)$ | Naive implementation |
| FFD Time complexity | $O(n \log n) + O(n^2) = O(n^2)$ | Dominated by FF phase |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Proof — Approximation Ratio of NEXT-FIT

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** Let $B_{\text{NF}}$ be the number of bins used by Next-Fit and $B_{\text{OPT}}$ the optimal number. Then $B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}}$.

**Step 1 — Setup and definitions.**
Let the bins opened by Next-Fit be labeled $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_{B_{\text{NF}}}$. Let $s(B_j)$ denote the total size (sum of item sizes) packed in bin $B_j$. Define the *total size* of the input as:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{B_{\text{NF}}} s(B_j)
$$

**Step 2 — The key observation: adjacent-bin invariant.**
Consider any two consecutive bins $B_j$ and $B_{j+1}$ (for $1 \leq j < B_{\text{NF}}$). Suppose the very first item $x$ that was placed into $B_{j+1}$ has size $s(x)$. By the NF rule, $x$ did NOT fit into $B_j$, i.e., $s(B_j) + s(x) > 1$. Since $s(x) \leq s(B_{j+1})$ (it is only one item in $B_{j+1}$, possibly with more), we conclude:

$$
s(B_j) + s(B_{j+1}) \geq s(B_j) + s(x) > 1
$$

**Step 3 — Pair up the bins.**
For even-indexed bins, group them in pairs $(B_1, B_2), (B_3, B_4), \ldots, (B_{B_{\text{NF}}-1}, B_{B_{\text{NF}}})$. If $B_{\text{NF}}$ is odd, the last bin $B_{B_{\text{NF}}}$ stands alone but still contributes positively. From Step 2, each pair has total content $> 1$:

$$
\sum_{j \text{ in pair } k} s(B_j) > 1 \;\;\text{for every pair } k
$$

Let $P = \lfloor B_{\text{NF}} / 2 \rfloor$ be the number of complete pairs. Then:

$$
S = \sum_{j=1}^{B_{\text{NF}}} s(B_j) > P
$$

**Step 4 — Relate to optimal.**
Since the total size $S$ is a lower bound on the optimum (each bin can hold total content at most 1):

$$
B_{\text{OPT}} \geq S
$$

Combining with Step 3:

$$
B_{\text{OPT}} \geq S > P = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{NF}}}{2} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $B_{\text{NF}}$.**
Since $B_{\text{OPT}}$ and $P$ are integers, $B_{\text{OPT}} \geq P + 1$. Thus:

$$
B_{\text{OPT}} \geq \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{NF}}}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

If $B_{\text{NF}} = 2k$ is even, $P = k$, and we get $B_{\text{OPT}} \geq k + 1$, so $B_{\text{NF}} = 2k \leq 2(B_{\text{OPT}} - 1) < 2 B_{\text{OPT}}$.

If $B_{\text{NF}} = 2k+1$ is odd, $P = k$, and we get $B_{\text{OPT}} \geq k+1$, so $B_{\text{NF}} = 2k+1 \leq 2 B_{\text{OPT}} - 1 < 2 B_{\text{OPT}}$.

In both cases, $\boxed{B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 B_{\text{OPT}}}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Tracing FFD on a Sample Instance

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam Favourite):** Pack the items $\{0.5, 0.7, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 0.2, 0.1, 0.5\}$ using (a) Next-Fit, (b) First-Fit, and (c) First-Fit Decreasing. Compute the number of bins used in each case. Show that OPT = 3.

### 3.2.1 First, sort the items (for FFD):

$$
\text{Sorted: } 0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1
$$

The total size is $S = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.3 + 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.5 = 3.3$, so $\text{OPT} \geq \lceil 3.3 \rceil = 4$. Actually with the sorting insight, let me recompute — total is 3.3, so OPT ≥ 4. Let me redo with a cleaner example.

### 3.2.2 Cleaned example for trace:

**Items (unsorted):** $0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.7, 0.2, 0.6, 0.5, 0.1$. Sum = 3.3, so OPT ≥ 4.

### (a) NEXT-FIT trace

| Item | Action | Open Bin | Bin contents after |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | Place in Bin 1 | Bin 1 | $\{0.5\}$ (load 0.5) |
| 0.4 | Fits in Bin 1 (1 - 0.5 = 0.5 ≥ 0.4) | Bin 1 | $\{0.5, 0.4\}$ (load 0.9) |
| 0.3 | Doesn't fit in Bin 1 (0.1 < 0.3) | **Open Bin 2** | $\{0.3\}$ (load 0.3) |
| 0.7 | Doesn't fit in Bin 2 (0.3 < 0.7) | **Open Bin 3** | $\{0.7\}$ (load 0.7) |
| 0.2 | Fits in Bin 3 (0.3 ≥ 0.2) | Bin 3 | $\{0.7, 0.2\}$ (load 0.9) |
| 0.6 | Doesn't fit in Bin 3 (0.1 < 0.6) | **Open Bin 4** | $\{0.6\}$ (load 0.6) |
| 0.5 | Fits in Bin 4 (0.4 ≥ 0.5? NO, 0.4 < 0.5) → Open Bin 5 | **Open Bin 5** | $\{0.5\}$ (load 0.5) |
| 0.1 | Fits in Bin 5 (0.5 ≥ 0.1) | Bin 5 | $\{0.5, 0.1\}$ (load 0.6) |

**NF uses 5 bins.**

### (b) FIRST-FIT trace (original order: 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.7, 0.2, 0.6, 0.5, 0.1)

| Item | Try Bin 1 | Try Bin 2 | Try Bin 3 | ... | Open new? | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | Fits (0.5) | – | – | – | No | Bin 1: $\{0.5\}$, load 0.5 |
| 0.4 | Fits (0.5) | – | – | – | No | Bin 1: $\{0.5, 0.4\}$, load 0.9 |
| 0.3 | No (0.1) | Fits (1.0) | – | – | No | Bin 2: $\{0.3\}$, load 0.3 |
| 0.7 | No (0.3) | No (0.3) | – | – | **Yes** | Bin 3: $\{0.7\}$, load 0.7 |
| 0.2 | No (0.3) | No (0.1) | Fits (0.3) | – | No | Bin 3: $\{0.7, 0.2\}$, load 0.9 |
| 0.6 | No | No | No (0.1) | – | **Yes** | Bin 4: $\{0.6\}$, load 0.6 |
| 0.5 | No (0.3) | No (0.1) | No (0.1) | Fits (0.4) | No | Bin 4: $\{0.6, 0.5\}$, load 1.1? **No, exceeds 1** |

**Recompute Bin 4 attempt for 0.5:** Bin 4 has load 0.6, remaining = 0.4. 0.5 > 0.4, so 0.5 doesn't fit. Continue.

| Item | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | New? | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 0.5 (0.5 left) — NO fit (0.5 > 0.5? actually 0.5 ≤ 0.5, so YES fits!) | | | | | |

Wait, Bin 1 has load 0.9, remaining 0.1. So 0.5 does NOT fit. Let me redo carefully.

Final trace:

| Item | B1 load (0.9) | B2 load (0.3) | B3 load (0.9) | B4 load (0.6) | New? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 0.1 left → NO | 0.7 → NO | 0.1 → NO | 0.4 → NO | **Yes → Bin 5** |

Bin 5: $\{0.5\}$, load 0.5

| Item | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | B5 | New? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | NO (0.1) | 0.9 left → YES | – | – | – | No → Bin 2 |

Bin 2: $\{0.3, 0.1\}$, load 0.4

**FF uses 5 bins** for this instance. Let's check: Bins are
- Bin 1: $\{0.5, 0.4\}$ (load 0.9)
- Bin 2: $\{0.3, 0.1\}$ (load 0.4)
- Bin 3: $\{0.7, 0.2\}$ (load 0.9)
- Bin 4: $\{0.6\}$ (load 0.6)
- Bin 5: $\{0.5\}$ (load 0.5)

**But wait — OPT is better!** Let me find OPT:
- Bin 1: $\{0.5, 0.5\}$ (load 1.0) ✓
- Bin 2: $\{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$ (load 1.0) ✓
- Bin 3: $\{0.7, 0.3\}$ — but 0.3 is already used. 

Try again:
- Bin 1: $\{0.7, 0.3\}$ (load 1.0)
- Bin 2: $\{0.5, 0.5\}$ (load 1.0)
- Bin 3: $\{0.6, 0.4\}$ (load 1.0)
- Bin 4: $\{0.2, 0.1\}$ — leftover. Hmm still 4 bins.

**OPT = 4 bins.**

### (c) FFD trace (sorted: 0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)

| Item | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | New? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.7 | Place → $\{0.7\}$, load 0.7 | – | – | – | – |
| 0.6 | 0.3 left → NO | – | – | – | **Yes → Bin 2: $\{0.6\}$** |
| 0.5 | 0.3 → NO | 0.4 → NO | – | – | **Yes → Bin 3: $\{0.5\}$** |
| 0.5 | 0.3 → NO | 0.4 → NO | 0.5 → YES (fits exactly) | – | – → Bin 3: $\{0.5, 0.5\}$, load 1.0 |
| 0.4 | 0.3 → NO | 0.6 → NO | 0.0 → NO | – | **Yes → Bin 4: $\{0.4\}$** |
| 0.3 | 0.3 → NO (just barely fails) | 0.6 → NO | 0.0 → NO | 0.6 → YES | Bin 4: $\{0.4, 0.3\}$, load 0.7 |
| 0.2 | 0.3 → NO | 0.6 → NO | 0.0 → NO | 0.3 → NO | **Yes → Bin 5: $\{0.2\}$** |
| 0.1 | 0.3 → NO | 0.6 → NO | 0.0 → NO | 0.3 → NO | 0.8 → YES → Bin 5: $\{0.2, 0.1\}$, load 0.3 |

**FFD uses 5 bins for this particular instance.** (In this example, FFD ties with NF and FF, not the best — but FFD is usually better on average.)

### 3.2.3 Summary of this example

| Algorithm | Bins Used | Approximation Ratio (B/OPT) |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal (OPT) | 4 | 1.0 |
| Next-Fit (NF) | 5 | 1.25 |
| First-Fit (FF) | 5 | 1.25 |
| First-Fit Decreasing (FFD) | 5 | 1.25 |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Why FFD Achieves $\frac{11}{9}$ Approximation

The proof of FFD's ratio is one of the most celebrated results in approximation algorithms. Here is the high-level structure as required for KTU:

**Setup.** Let $k = B_{\text{OPT}}$. Then the optimal packing uses $k$ bins. For FFD, sort items as $1 \geq a_1 \geq a_2 \geq \ldots \geq a_n$.

**Key Lemma (KTU):** *In the FFD packing, for any bin $B_i$ (except the last few), we have $s(B_i) + a_1(B_{i+1}) > 1$, where $a_1(B_{i+1})$ is the largest item in bin $B_{i+1}$.*

**Definition:** Call a bin *large* if it contains an item of size $> 1/2$. Each such bin has exactly one such item.

**Case 1: Many large bins.** If there are many items $> 1/2$, then OPT is also large, and FFD's ratio is close to 1.

**Case 2: Few large bins.** If there are $\ell$ items of size $> 1/2$, then OPT ≥ $\ell$ and FFD also has at most $\ell$ "large" bins. The remaining bins in FFD are "small" and can be analyzed using the $a_1(B_{i+1})$ invariant.

**Counting argument (KTU):** Through a careful weighting function $w(x)$ that charges each "missing" unit of bin space, the proof shows that the total waste across all FFD bins is at most $\frac{2}{9} \cdot k + \frac{6}{9}$. Adding this waste to the optimal size gives:

$$
B_{\text{FFD}} \leq k + \frac{2}{9}k + \frac{6}{9} = \frac{11}{9}k + \frac{6}{9}
$$

This completes the asymptotic bound. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — How Bin Packing Algorithms Make Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionNode fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef processNode fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef algoNode fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputNode fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    startA([INPUT: List of N items with sizes]):::startNode

    branchNode{Pre-sorting<br/>allowed?}:::decisionNode

    onlinePath[ONLINE PATH<br/>No sorting]:::processNode
    offlinePath[OFFLINE PATH<br/>Sort items first]:::processNode

    nfAlgo[NEXT-FIT NF<br/>One open bin only]:::algoNode
    ffAlgo[FIRST-FIT FF<br/>Scan bins in order]:::algoNode
    bfAlgo[BEST-FIT BF<br/>Tightest fit]:::algoNode
    ffdAlgo[FIRST-FIT DECREASING FFD<br/>Sorted then FF]:::algoNode

    sortStep[Sort items in<br/>non-increasing order]:::processNode

    checkNF{Item fits in<br/>current bin?}:::decisionNode
    checkFF{Item fits in<br/>any existing bin?}:::decisionNode
    checkBF{Item fits in<br/>any existing bin?}:::decisionNode
    checkFFD{Item fits in<br/>any existing bin?}:::decisionNode

    nfPlace[Place in current bin]:::processNode
    nfNew[Close current, open new bin]:::processNode
    ffFirstFit[Place in FIRST bin that fits]:::processNode
    bfBestFit[Place in bin with MAX load]:::processNode
    ffdFirstFit[Place in FIRST bin that fits]:::processNode
    newBin[OPEN NEW BIN]:::outputNode

    outputA([OUTPUT: Number of bins used]):::outputNode
    cmpStep[Compare with OPTIMUM<br/>to compute approximation ratio]:::processNode

    startA --> branchNode
    branchNode -->|NO| onlinePath
    branchNode -->|YES| offlinePath

    onlinePath --> nfAlgo
    onlinePath --> ffAlgo
    onlinePath --> bfAlgo

    offlinePath --> sortStep
    sortStep --> ffdAlgo

    nfAlgo --> checkNF
    checkNF -->|YES| nfPlace
    checkNF -->|NO| nfNew

    ffAlgo --> checkFF
    checkFF -->|YES| ffFirstFit
    checkFF -->|NO| newBin

    bfAlgo --> checkBF
    checkBF -->|YES| bfBestFit
    checkBF -->|NO| newBin

    ffdAlgo --> checkFFD
    checkFFD -->|YES| ffdFirstFit
    checkFFD -->|NO| newBin

    nfPlace --> outputA
    nfNew --> outputA
    ffFirstFit --> outputA
    bfBestFit --> outputA
    ffdFirstFit --> outputA
    newBin --> outputA

    outputA --> cmpStep
```

## 4.3 Bin State Diagram — States of a Bin During Packing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR

    [*] --> EMPTY: Open new bin

    EMPTY --> PARTIALLY_FILLED: Place item, load becomes 0 to 1 exclusive
    EMPTY --> FULL: Place item that exactly fills bin, load equals 1

    PARTIALLY_FILLED --> PARTIALLY_FILLED: Place more items that fit
    PARTIALLY_FILLED --> FULL: Place item that fills the bin exactly
    PARTIALLY_FILLED --> CLOSED_BY_NF: Next item rejected by Next-Fit
    PARTIALLY_FILLED --> CLOSED_FINAL: All items packed algorithm terminates

    FULL --> CLOSED_FINAL: Algorithm terminates
    CLOSED_BY_NF --> [*]: Bin is sealed in Next-Fit
    CLOSED_FINAL --> [*]: Final bin count recorded
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** Each *bin* progresses through these states. Crucially, in **Next-Fit**, the transition "PARTIALLY FILLED → CLOSED BY NF" happens as soon as the next item fails to fit. In **First-Fit, Best-Fit, and FFD**, the bin never enters the "CLOSED BY NF" state; it remains in "PARTIALLY FILLED" and available for future items, until the algorithm terminates.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Bin Packing Problem. Why is it classified as an NP-hard problem? Mention at least two real-world applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bin Packing Problem (BPP)** is a combinatorial optimization problem in which we are given a finite set $I = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ of items, each with a size $s(i) \in (0, 1]$, and an unlimited supply of identical bins of unit capacity $C = 1$. The objective is to assign each item to a bin such that the total size of items in no bin exceeds 1, while minimizing the number of bins used. Formally, we want to find the minimum $m$ such that $I$ can be partitioned into $m$ subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m$ where $\sum_{i \in S_j} s(i) \leq 1$ for every $j$.

**Why NP-hard:** The decision version of BPP (does a packing using at most $k$ bins exist?) is in the class NP, and the problem is at least as hard as the **Partition Problem**, which is NP-complete. Equivalently, the partition problem reduces to bin packing in polynomial time. Since no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known (and none is expected to exist unless P = NP), heuristic approximation algorithms are used.

**Real-world applications:**
- **Logistics & Cargo Loading:** Packing boxes of varying sizes into the fewest containers.
- **Cloud Server Allocation:** Placing virtual machines of different sizes onto physical servers.
- **Cutting Stock:** Cutting required pieces from stock material of standard length with minimum waste.
- **Memory Allocation:** Placing variable-size memory blocks into fixed-size pages.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for NP-hard reasoning, 1 mark for applications.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**State the Next-Fit algorithm for bin packing. What is its approximation ratio and the corresponding worst-case bound?**

**Model Answer:**

**Next-Fit Algorithm:** For each item in the input sequence, attempt to place it in the **currently open bin**. If it fits, place it there. If it does not fit, **close the current bin permanently** and open a new bin, into which the item is placed.

**Approximation Ratio:** The Next-Fit algorithm has an asymptotic approximation ratio of exactly 2. That is, if $B_{\text{NF}}$ is the number of bins used by Next-Fit and $B_{\text{OPT}}$ is the optimal number, then:

$$
B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 \cdot B_{\text{OPT}}
$$

**Tight Example:** Consider $n$ items of size $\frac{1}{2} + \varepsilon$ (for small $\varepsilon > 0$) followed by $n$ items of size $\frac{1}{2} - \varepsilon$. Next-Fit opens a new bin for every item (no two can share a bin due to the size of the first items), giving $B_{\text{NF}} = 2n$, while the optimum packs one $\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon$ with one $\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon$ in each bin, giving $B_{\text{OPT}} = n + n/2$ bins (in the asymptotic limit, the ratio approaches 2).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Part B — Question A: (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **First-Fit (FF)** algorithm for the Bin Packing Problem in detail. State and prove its asymptotic approximation ratio of $\frac{17}{10}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following instance of the bin packing problem using **Next-Fit**, **First-Fit**, and **First-Fit Decreasing (FFD)**. Compute the number of bins used in each case and compare with the optimal solution.

Items (original order): $0.42,\ 0.67,\ 0.33,\ 0.25,\ 0.78,\ 0.10,\ 0.55,\ 0.91,\ 0.40,\ 0.15$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — First-Fit and Its Ratio

**Algorithm Description:**

The First-Fit algorithm maintains a list of *all* currently open bins. For each item in the input sequence, FF scans the bins in the order they were opened (bin 1, then bin 2, etc.) and places the item into the **first bin** that has sufficient remaining capacity to accommodate it. If no existing bin can hold the item, a new bin is opened and the item is placed in it. All previously opened bins remain open throughout the algorithm — they are never closed.

**Pseudocode:**

```
ALGORITHM FirstFit(items[1..n])
1.  binList ← empty
2.  FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
3.      placed ← FALSE
4.      FOR each bin b in binList (in order) DO
5.          IF s(items[i]) ≤ remaining_capacity(b) THEN
6.              place items[i] in bin b
7.              placed ← TRUE; BREAK
8.      IF placed = FALSE THEN
9.          open new bin; place items[i] in it; add to binList
10. RETURN size(binList)
```

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot B_{\text{FF}})$. In the worst case $B_{\text{FF}} = O(n)$, so time is $O(n^2)$. With balanced tree data structures, it can be reduced to $O(n \log n)$.

**Approximation Ratio Theorem:**

> The First-Fit algorithm satisfies:
> $$B_{\text{FF}} \leq \frac{17}{10} B_{\text{OPT}} + 2$$

**Proof Outline (Standard KTU Pattern):**

**Step 1 — Adjacent-bin invariant.** Let $B_i, B_{i+1}, \ldots, B_{i+k}$ be consecutive FF bins where $k \geq 1$ is the *largest* index such that every bin $B_{i+j}$ (for $0 \leq j \leq k$) contains an item of size $\leq 1/2$. Call such bins "type-A" bins. Then by a careful counting argument, the sum $s(B_i) + s(B_{i+1}) + \ldots + s(B_{i+k}) > (k+1)/2$.

**Step 2 — Weighting function.** Assign a "weight" $w(x) = \frac{6}{5} x$ to each item of size $x \leq 1/2$ and $w(x) = \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{2} + \frac{9}{5}\left(x - \frac{1}{2}\right)$ for $x > 1/2$. The total weight of any bin's content is at most $\frac{17}{10}$ (since the max is when the bin is fully packed).

**Step 3 — Bin classification.** Classify FF bins into (i) bins containing an item $> 1/2$, and (ii) bins with all items $\leq 1/2$. The first category has at most $B_{\text{OPT}}$ bins. The second category, by the weighting argument, contributes at most $\frac{17}{10} B_{\text{OPT}}$ extra. Combining, $B_{\text{FF}} \leq \frac{17}{10} B_{\text{OPT}} + 2$.

The bound is **tight**: a family of instances (e.g., 30 items of size $1/2 + \varepsilon$, 30 of size $1/4 + 2\varepsilon$, etc., as constructed by van Vliet) shows that no better constant is possible.

---

- Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.25, 0.10?} = 0.25 used; 0.10 used. {0.67, 0.33} = 0.33 used. {0.67, 0.15, 0.10?} = 1.02 no. **Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.25, 0.10?} no.** So **Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.25, ...}** with 0.25 in Bin 1 → conflict. So: **Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.33}** with 0.33 in Bin 1 → conflict. So: **Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.15, 0.10, 0.08?}=1.0?** 0.67+0.15+0.10 = 0.92, add 0.08? No 0.08. So Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.15, 0.10} = 0.92. But 0.10 already in Bin 2!

OK this is getting complicated. Let me restart OPT properly with backtracking.

**Items sorted decreasing:** $0.91, 0.78, 0.67, 0.55, 0.42, 0.40, 0.33, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10$.

**Greedy OPT:**
- Bin 1: take 0.91, room 0.09. None fits. → Bin 1 = {0.91}, load 0.91.
- Bin 2: take 0.78, room 0.22. None of {0.67, 0.55, 0.42, 0.40, 0.33, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10} fits in 0.22. → Bin 2 = {0.78}, load 0.78.
- Bin 3: take 0.67, room 0.33. None of remaining {0.55, 0.42, 0.40, 0.33, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10} fits in 0.33. 0.25 fits! Add 0.25, room 0.08. None fits. → Bin 3 = {0.67, 0.25}, load 0.92.
- Bin 4: take 0.55, room 0.45. Add 0.42, room 0.03. None fits. → Bin 4 = {0.55, 0.42}, load 0.97.
- Bin 5: take 0.40, room 0.60. Add 0.33, room 0.27. Add 0.15, room 0.12. Add 0.10, room 0.02. → Bin 5 = {0.40, 0.33, 0.15, 0.10}, load 0.98.

**OPT = 5 bins** ✓ Total load = 0.91+0.78+0.92+0.97+0.98 = 4.56 ✓

---

### Now the traces for Part (b):

#### (i) Next-Fit trace (original order)

| Item | Current Bin Load | Fits? | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.42 | Bin 1: 0 | YES | Place | Bin 1: {0.42}, load 0.42 |
| 0.67 | Bin 1: 0.42, room 0.58 | YES (0.67 ≤ 0.58? NO) | Close B1, Open B2 | Bin 2: {0.67}, load 0.67 |
| 0.33 | Bin 2: 0.67, room 0.33 | YES (0.33 ≤ 0.33) | Place | Bin 2: {0.67, 0.33}, load 1.00 |
| 0.25 | Bin 2: 1.00, room 0 | NO | Close B2, Open B3 | Bin 3: {0.25}, load 0.25 |
| 0.78 | Bin 3: 0.25, room 0.75 | NO (0.78 > 0.75) | Close B3, Open B4 | Bin 4: {0.78}, load 0.78 |
| 0.10 | Bin 4: 0.78, room 0.22 | YES | Place | Bin 4: {0.78, 0.10}, load 0.88 |
| 0.55 | Bin 4: 0.88, room 0.12 | NO | Close B4, Open B5 | Bin 5: {0.55}, load 0.55 |
| 0.91 | Bin 5: 0.55, room 0.45 | NO | Close B5, Open B6 | Bin 6: {0.91}, load 0.91 |
| 0.40 | Bin 6: 0.91, room 0.09 | NO | Close B6, Open B7 | Bin 7: {0.40}, load 0.40 |
| 0.15 | Bin 7: 0.40, room 0.60 | YES | Place | Bin 7: {0.40, 0.15}, load 0.55 |

**Bins used by NF = 7.**

#### (ii) First-Fit trace (original order)

| Item | B1 (0.42) | B2 (0.67,0.33) | B3 (0.25) | B4 (0.78,0.10) | B5 (0.55) | B6 (0.91) | B7 (0.40) | New? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.42 | place → B1 | | | | | | | – |
| 0.67 | 0.58 left, 0.67>0.58 NO | | | | | | | New B2 |
| 0.33 | 0.58 NO | 0.67 left, fits (0.33≤0.67) → B2 | | | | | | – |
| 0.25 | 0.58 left, fits → B1 | | | | | | | – |
| 0.78 | 0.33 left NO | 0.0 NO | 0.75 left, 0.78>0.75 NO | | | | | New B3? wait, what is the state |

Let me restart FF cleanly. At each step, scan bins in order B1, B2, ... and place in first that fits.

| Step | Item | State of bins | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.42 | (empty) | New Bin 1, place 0.42 |
| 2 | 0.67 | B1: {0.42} (room 0.58) | 0.67 > 0.58, no fit. New Bin 2, place 0.67 |
| 3 | 0.33 | B1: room 0.58, B2: room 0.33 | B1: 0.33 ≤ 0.58 → fits. Place in B1. |
| 4 | 0.25 | B1: {0.42, 0.33} (room 0.25), B2: {0.67} (room 0.33) | B1: 0.25 ≤ 0.25 → fits. Place in B1. |
| 5 | 0.78 | B1: {0.42,0.33,0.25} room 0, B2: {0.67} room 0.33 | B1: 0, no. B2: 0.78>0.33, no. New Bin 3, place 0.78. |
| 6 | 0.10 | B1: room 0, B2: room 0.33, B3: {0.78} room 0.22 | B1: no, B2: 0.10≤0.33 yes → place in B2. |
| 7 | 0.55 | B1: 0, B2: {0.67, 0.10} room 0.23, B3: {0.78} room 0.22 | B1: no, B2: 0.55>0.23 no, B3: 0.55>0.22 no. New Bin 4, place 0.55. |
| 8 | 0.91 | B1: 0, B2: 0.23, B3: 0.22, B4: {0.55} room 0.45 | All fail. New Bin 5, place 0.91. |
| 9 | 0.40 | B1: 0, B2: 0.23, B3: 0.22, B4: 0.45, B5: {0.91} room 0.09 | B1: no, B2: 0.40>0.23 no, B3: 0.40>0.22 no, B4: 0.40≤0.45 yes → place in B4. |
| 10 | 0.15 | B1: 0, B2: 0.23, B3: 0.22, B4: {0.55, 0.40} room 0.05, B5: 0.09 | B1-B4: no, B5: 0.15>0.09 no. New Bin 6, place 0.15. |

**Bins used by FF = 6.**

Final state:
- B1: {0.42, 0.33, 0.25}, load 1.00
- B2: {0.67, 0.10}, load 0.77
- B3: {0.78}, load 0.78
- B4: {0.55, 0.40}, load 0.95
- B5: {0.91}, load 0.91
- B6: {0.15}, load 0.15

#### (iii) First-Fit Decreasing trace

**Sort decreasing:** $0.91, 0.78, 0.67, 0.55, 0.42, 0.40, 0.33, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10$.

| Step | Item | Action | Bins State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.91 | New Bin 1, place | B1: {0.91}, room 0.09 |
| 2 | 0.78 | B1: 0.09 NO | New Bin 2, place | B2: {0.78}, room 0.22 |
| 3 | 0.67 | B1: 0.09 NO, B2: 0.22 NO | New Bin 3, place | B3: {0.67}, room 0.33 |
| 4 | 0.55 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.33 → 0.55>0.33 NO | New Bin 4, place | B4: {0.55}, room 0.45 |
| 5 | 0.42 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.33, B4: 0.45 → 0.42≤0.45 YES | B4: {0.55, 0.42}, room 0.03 |
| 6 | 0.40 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.33, B4: 0.03 NO | New Bin 5, place | B5: {0.40}, room 0.60 |
| 7 | 0.33 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.33 → YES | B3: {0.67, 0.33}, room 0.00 |
| 8 | 0.25 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.00, B4: 0.03, B5: 0.60 → YES | B5: {0.40, 0.25}, room 0.35 |
| 9 | 0.15 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22, B3: 0.00, B4: 0.03, B5: 0.35 → YES | B5: {0.40, 0.25, 0.15}, room 0.20 |
| 10 | 0.10 | B1: 0.09, B2: 0.22 → YES | B2: {0.78, 0.10}, room 0.12 |

**Bins used by FFD = 5.**

Final state:
- B1: {0.91}, load 0.91
- B2: {0.78, 0.10}, load 0.88
- B3: {0.67, 0.33}, load 1.00
- B4: {0.55, 0.42}, load 0.97
- B5: {0.40, 0.25, 0.15}, load 0.80

#### (iv) Summary table

| Algorithm | Bins Used | Ratio to OPT=5 |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal | 5 | 1.00 |
| Next-Fit (NF) | 7 | 1.40 |
| First-Fit (FF) | 6 | 1.20 |
| First-Fit Decreasing (FFD) | 5 | 1.00 |

**Observation:** FFD matched the optimal in this instance, demonstrating its strength. NF was the worst, FF was intermediate, FFD was the best — consistent with the theoretical ratios.

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice): (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative]`

**(a)** Describe the **Next-Fit (NF)** algorithm with pseudocode. Prove that the approximation ratio of Next-Fit is at most 2. Show with a counter-example that the bound is asymptotically tight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **First-Fit**, **Best-Fit**, and **First-Fit Decreasing** algorithms. State their approximation ratios and discuss which one performs best in practice. Show the FFD packing on the items: $0.6, 0.4, 0.5, 0.3, 0.8, 0.2, 0.1, 0.7$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Next-Fit Proof

**Algorithm:** Already covered in Section 2.2.2. Recap: keep one open bin, place item in it if possible, else close it and open a new one.

**Proof of $B_{\text{NF}} \leq 2 B_{\text{OPT}}$:**

1. Let NF's bins be $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_m$ with $m = B_{\text{NF}}$.
2. For any two consecutive bins $B_j, B_{j+1}$, the first item placed in $B_{j+1}$ did not fit in $B_j$, so $s(B_j) + s(B_{j+1}) > 1$.
3. Pair bins: $(B_1, B_2), (B_3, B_4), \ldots$ — at most $P = \lfloor m/2 \rfloor$ complete pairs.
4. Sum of all sizes $S = \sum_j s(B_j) > P \cdot 1 = P$.
5. Lower bound: $B_{\text{OPT}} \geq \lceil S \rceil \geq S > P$.
6. So $B_{\text{OPT}} \geq P + 1$, and $m \leq 2B_{\text{OPT}} - 1 < 2B_{\text{OPT}}$. $\blacksquare$

**Tight example:** Consider $2n$ items: $n$ items of size $1/2 + \varepsilon$ and $n$ items of size $1/2 - \varepsilon$. Then:
- OPT pairs them up: $n$ bins of load $\approx 1$, so OPT = $n$.
- NF places them alternately: $\{1/2+\varepsilon\}, \{1/2-\varepsilon\}, \{1/2+\varepsilon\}, \ldots$ — actually, both sizes don't fit with each other in NF if they arrive alternating. So NF = $2n$.

Ratio: $2n / n = 2$, so the bound is tight. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Comparison and FFD Trace

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | First-Fit (FF) | Best-Fit (BF) | First-Fit Decreasing (FFD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Online | Online | Offline (needs pre-sorting) |
| Bin selection rule | First bin in order with room | Bin with least remaining room after placement | First bin in order with room, on sorted list |
| Time complexity | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n) + O(n^2) = O(n^2)$ |
| Approximation ratio | 17/10 = 1.7 | 17/10 = 1.7 | 11/9 ≈ 1.222 |
| Practical quality | Good | Similar to FF | Best (among these three) |

**FFD packing of $0.6, 0.4, 0.5, 0.3, 0.8, 0.2, 0.1, 0.7$:**

**Sort decreasing:** $0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1$.

| Step | Item | Action | Bins |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8 | New Bin 1 | B1: {0.8}, room 0.20 |
| 2 | 0.7 | B1: 0.20 < 0.7 NO | New Bin 2 | B2: {0.7}, room 0.30 |
| 3 | 0.6 | B1: 0.20, B2: 0.30 → NO | New Bin 3 | B3: {0.6}, room 0.40 |
| 4 | 0.5 | B1: 0.20, B2: 0.30, B3: 0.40 → 0.5>0.40 NO | New Bin 4 | B4: {0.5}, room 0.50 |
| 5 | 0.4 | B1: 0.20, B2: 0.30, B3: 0.40 → 0.4≤0.40 YES | B3: {0.6, 0.4}, room 0.00 |
| 6 | 0.3 | B1: 0.20 NO, B2: 0.30 → 0.3≤0.30 YES | B2: {0.7, 0.3}, room 0.00 |
| 7 | 0.2 | B1: 0.20 → 0.2≤0.20 YES | B1: {0.8, 0.2}, room 0.00 |
| 8 | 0.1 | B1: 0.00, B2: 0.00, B3: 0.00, B4: 0.50 → 0.1≤0.50 YES | B4: {0.5, 0.1}, room 0.40 |

**FFD uses 4 bins.** OPT = ? Total size = 0.8+0.7+0.6+0.5+0.4+0.3+0.2+0.1 = 3.6, so OPT ≥ 4. Since FFD uses 4, **OPT = 4** and FFD is optimal here.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Bin Packing Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the algorithm clearly in pseudocode:** Even if you trace by hand, the examiner expects pseudocode in the 14-mark questions. Lose up to 2 marks if missing.
>
> 2. **Confusing the lower bound with the approximation ratio:** "OPT ≥ sum of sizes" is a *lower bound on OPT*, not an *approximation ratio*. Approximation ratio compares ALG output to OPT.
>
> 3. **Mixing up FF and BF:** "First-Fit picks the *first* bin that fits" — the bin order is the order they were opened, NOT sorted by remaining capacity. "Best-Fit picks the bin with the *least* remaining space" — that is, the tightest fit.
>
> 4. **Wrong tightness examples:** For NF, the 1/2 + ε / 1/2 - ε alternating instance is the standard tight example. Don't use a 1/3, 1/3, 1/3 instance — that's not a worst case.
>
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in traces:** Always re-verify each item: "remaining capacity" should be checked against the *new* item size. A single wrong placement cascades.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the asymptotic + constant:** FFD's bound is $\frac{11}{9}\,\text{OPT} + \frac{6}{9}$, NOT just $\frac{11}{9}\,\text{OPT}$. The additive constant matters for small instances.
>
> 7. **Not justifying asymptotic tightness:** When asked "is the bound tight?", you MUST give a specific infinite family of instances, not just claim "yes."
>
> 8. **Confusing online vs offline:** FFD and BFD are offline. FF, BF, NF are online. Examiners love testing this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — BIN PACKING (Module 4)**

- **Problem statement:** Pack items of sizes $\in (0,1]$ into unit-capacity bins. Minimize bins used. NP-hard.
- **Next-Fit (NF):** Single open bin. Ratio $\leq 2$. Tight. Time $O(n)$.
- **First-Fit (FF):** All bins open, pick first that fits. Ratio $\leq 1.7 + $ const. Time $O(n^2)$.
- **Best-Fit (BF):** All bins open, pick tightest fit. Ratio $\leq 1.7 + $ const. Time $O(n^2)$.
- **First-Fit Decreasing (FFD):** Sort descending, then FF. Ratio $\leq \frac{11}{9} + \frac{6}{9}$. Tight (Dósa 2007). Offline.
- **Best-Fit Decreasing (BFD):** Sort descending, then BF. Same ratio as FFD asymptotically.
- **Adjacent-bin invariant** (key proof tool): In NF, $s(B_j) + s(B_{j+1}) > 1$ for all $j$.
- **Total-size lower bound:** $\text{OPT} \geq \lceil \sum s(i) \rceil$.
- **Items > 1/2 lower bound:** $\text{OPT} \geq |\{i : s(i) > 1/2\}|$ (each in its own bin).
- **Quality ranking (best to worst in practice):** FFD $\approx$ BFD $>$ FF $\approx$ BF $>$ NF.
- **Tight example for NF:** $n$ items of $\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon$ alternating with $\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon$ — ratio 2.
- **KTU Favourite:** Trace Next-Fit, First-Fit, and FFD on a small instance (5-10 items) and compare.
- **Theorem to memorize (Dósa, 2007):** $B_{\text{FFD}} \leq \frac{11}{9} B_{\text{OPT}} + \frac{6}{9}$. This is the **CURRENT BEST KNOWN** asymptotic ratio for FFD.
- **Common pitfalls:** Confusing approximation ratio with lower bound, forgetting additive constants, mixing up online/offline classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized Algorithms - Definitions of Monte Carlo and Las Vegas Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCRT602 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Algorithms - Definitions of Monte Carlo and Las Vegas Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Randomized Algorithms — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigorously Tested):**
> A **Randomized Algorithm** is an algorithm that incorporates randomness (typically through a pseudo-random number generator, or PRNG) as a logical component of its operation. Unlike deterministic algorithms, which produce the same output for a given input on every execution, a randomized algorithm's **running time** or its **output** (or both) are random variables that depend on the choices made by the random number generator.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCRT602, Module 4), randomized algorithms are formally classified into two principal categories based on **where** the randomness manifests:

1. **Las Vegas Algorithms** — Randomness affects *running time*, but the *output is always correct*.
2. **Monte Carlo Algorithms** — Running time is *deterministic* (or bounded), but the *output may be incorrect* with some bounded probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study Randomization?**
> Randomization frequently allows us to design algorithms that are **simpler**, **faster**, and more **elegant** than their best-known deterministic counterparts. It also breaks the symmetry of worst-case adversarial inputs — a randomized algorithm has *no single input* on which it always performs poorly, only a *distribution* of behaviors.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — A Medical Diagnosis

Imagine two types of doctors treating patients:

- **Las Vegas Doctor** 🩺: She performs **multiple independent diagnostic tests** until she is **100% certain** of the diagnosis. She never gives a wrong diagnosis, but the **time taken** (number of tests) is unpredictable. Sometimes 2 tests, sometimes 10.

- **Monte Carlo Doctor** 💉: He always performs **exactly 3 fixed tests** in **exactly 30 minutes**, but the diagnosis carries a small **probability of error** (say, 1 in 20). Fast and predictable, but not infallible.

This perfectly mirrors the trade-off between **certainty of output** and **certainty of runtime**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Randomized Algorithm Design

The following KTU-highlighted metrics are used throughout this module:

- **Random Variable ($X$):** A variable whose value depends on the outcome of a random experiment.
- **Expected Value ($E[X]$):** The probability-weighted average of $X$ over all possible outcomes.
- **Probability of Error ($\epsilon$ or $\delta$):** The bound on the chance that the algorithm returns an incorrect result (Monte Carlo).
- **Expected Running Time ($E[T(n)]$):** The average time complexity over the random coin flips.
- **Confidence Amplification:** The technique of running a Monte Carlo algorithm multiple times and taking a majority vote to reduce the error probability.
- **One-sided vs Two-sided Error:** A Monte Carlo algorithm may err only in *one* direction (e.g., "accepting" but never "rejecting" wrongly) or in *both* directions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** A Las Vegas algorithm can be converted into a Monte Carlo algorithm by **truncating** its execution after a fixed time bound. Conversely, a Monte Carlo algorithm can be amplified into a "high-probability" Las Vegas style only if it has a **verifiable certificate** of correctness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Las Vegas Algorithms — Operational Logic

A Las Vegas algorithm is characterized by the following properties:

- **Always Correct:** The output produced is **provably correct** for every possible random choice that leads to termination.
- **Randomized Running Time:** The number of steps $T(n)$ is a random variable.
- **Bounded Expectation:** The expected running time $E[T(n)]$ is guaranteed to be polynomial (typically $O(n)$, $O(n \log n)$, etc.).
- **No Incorrect Output:** It is mathematically impossible to obtain a wrong answer; the algorithm may simply take longer.

### Operational Steps (KTU Board Pattern)
1. **Initialize** with the input and seed the PRNG.
2. **Execute** a randomized subroutine (e.g., randomized partitioning, randomized pivoting).
3. **Check** whether the current solution satisfies the correctness predicate.
4. **If correct:** terminate and return the solution.
5. **If incorrect:** re-randomize and re-execute from step 2.
6. **Continue** until correctness is achieved; the loop count is the random variable $T(n)$.

### Canonical KTU Example
**Randomized Quicksort** — The pivot is chosen uniformly at random. The output (sorted array) is *always* correct, but the partition balance (and hence the time) varies.

## 2.2 Monte Carlo Algorithms — Operational Logic

A Monte Carlo algorithm is characterized by the following properties:

- **Bounded Running Time:** Always terminates within a fixed, deterministic time bound $T(n)$.
- **Probabilistic Correctness:** The output is correct with probability $\geq 1 - \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon$ is a small error bound).
- **No Guarantee of Verification:** Often (but not always), the algorithm cannot efficiently *check* its own output.

### Operational Steps (KTU Board Pattern)
1. **Receive** input and the allowed error tolerance $\epsilon > 0$.
2. **Generate** random samples / random bits using a PRNG.
3. **Compute** the candidate answer using these random bits.
4. **Return** the candidate answer (which may be wrong with probability $\leq \epsilon$).

### Canonical KTU Example
**Solovay–Strassen Primality Test** — Returns "composite" or "prime" in deterministic polynomial time, but the "prime" verdict is wrong with probability $\leq 1/2$. The test can be repeated to amplify confidence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Interpretation |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Expected Value (Linearity)** | $E[X] = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot P(X = x_i)$ | Linear, so $E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y]$ |
| 2 | **Indicator Variable Trick** | $E[I_A] = P(A)$ | Use for "did event $A$ occur?" |
| 3 | **Geometric Distribution (Las Vegas loops)** | $P(T = k) = (1-p)^{k-1} p$ | Probability of $k$ trials until first success |
| 4 | **Expected Geometric Trials** | $E[T] = \dfrac{1}{p}$ | If each trial succeeds with prob. $p$ |
| 5 | **Variance of Bernoulli Sum** | $\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p)$ | For $n$ independent trials |
| 6 | **Markov's Inequality** | $P(X \geq a) \leq \dfrac{E[X]}{a}$ | Bounding tail probabilities |
| 7 | **Chebyshev's Inequality** | $P(\vert X - E[X] \vert \geq k) \leq \dfrac{\text{Var}(X)}{k^2}$ | Two-sided tail bound |
| 8 | **Chernoff Bound (Hoeffding)** | $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq \delta \mu) \leq 2 e^{-\delta^2 \mu / 3}$ | Tight exponential tail bound |
| 9 | **Confidence Amplification** | $P(\text{error after } k \text{ runs}) \leq \epsilon^k$ | Independent runs, same $\epsilon$ |
| 10 | **Majority-Vote Amplification** | $P(\text{majority wrong}) \leq \dfrac{1}{2^k}$ | $k$ repetitions, two-sided error |
| 11 | **Las Vegas → Monte Carlo** | $E[T(n)] = \text{fixed } T_{\max}$ | Truncate execution at expectation |
| 12 | **Monte Carlo → Las Vegas** | Requires verifiable certificate | Only possible if output is checkable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Las Vegas algorithms are deployed in **cryptographic key generation** (where correctness is non-negotiable) and **production-grade database engines** (where unpredictability prevents DoS attacks). Monte Carlo algorithms dominate in **scientific computing**, **Monte Carlo simulation** for financial risk, **probabilistic primality testing** in RSA key generation, and **approximate counting** (e.g., estimating the number of distinct elements in a streaming dataset using the Flajolet–Martin algorithm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Running Time of a Generic Las Vegas Algorithm

**Problem Setup (as in KTU Module 4 exercises):**
Consider a Las Vegas algorithm that, on each independent iteration, produces the *correct* answer with probability $p$ (where $0 < p \leq 1$). Each iteration costs exactly $c$ time units. The loop continues until correctness is achieved. We want to find $E[T]$, the expected total running time.

**Step 1 — Model the Number of Iterations as a Geometric Random Variable**

Let $T$ be the number of iterations until the first successful (correct) outcome. Since each iteration is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $p$, the random variable $T$ follows a **Geometric Distribution**:

$$
P(T = k) = (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Expected Value**

By the definition of expectation for a discrete random variable:

$$
E[T] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P(T = k)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Geometric PMF**

$$
E[T] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p
$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Constant $p$**

$$
E[T] = p \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Standard Power Series Identity**

Recall the identity for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot x^{k-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2}
$$

Setting $x = 1 - p$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} = \frac{1}{[1 - (1-p)]^2} = \frac{1}{p^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine the Results**

$$
E[T] = p \cdot \frac{1}{p^2} = \frac{1}{p}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by Cost per Iteration**

If each iteration costs $c$ time units, the total expected running time is:

$$
E[T_{\text{total}}] = c \cdot E[T] = \frac{c}{p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The expected running time of a Las Vegas algorithm whose per-iteration success probability is $p$ is $\boxed{E[T_{\text{total}}] = c/p}$. This is the central formula in KTU Module 4 Las Vegas analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Error Amplification in Monte Carlo Algorithms

**Problem Setup:**
A Monte Carlo algorithm returns the correct answer with probability $p \geq 1 - \epsilon$ on any single run (so the single-run error is $\epsilon$). We run the algorithm independently $k$ times and use a **majority vote**. Derive the resulting error probability.

**Step 1 — Define the Error per Run**

The probability that any single run is *incorrect* is:

$$
P(\text{single run wrong}) = \epsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Majority-Wrong Event**

A majority of $k$ runs is wrong only if the number of wrong runs $W > k/2$. Since the runs are independent, $W \sim \text{Binomial}(k, \epsilon)$:

$$
P(W = j) = \binom{k}{j} \epsilon^{j} (1-\epsilon)^{k-j}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Union Bound (Simplified KTU Approach)**

The probability that the majority is wrong is:

$$
P(\text{majority wrong}) = P\left(W > \frac{k}{2}\right) = \sum_{j = \lceil k/2 \rceil}^{k} \binom{k}{j} \epsilon^{j} (1-\epsilon)^{k-j}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Chernoff / Hoeffding Bound**

For small $\epsilon$ and odd $k$, this sum is bounded by:

$$
P(\text{majority wrong}) \leq e^{-2k(0.5 - \epsilon)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Concrete Numerical Result**

If $\epsilon = 0.49$ (just below 0.5) and $k = 3$:

$$
P(\text{majority wrong}) = P(W \geq 2) = \binom{3}{2}(0.49)^2(0.51) + \binom{3}{3}(0.49)^3
$$

Evaluating each term:

$$
P(W=2) = 3 \times 0.2401 \times 0.51 = 0.3674
$$

$$
P(W=3) = 1 \times 0.1176 = 0.1176
$$

$$
P(\text{majority wrong}) = 0.3674 + 0.1176 = 0.4850
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Notice that for a one-sided error Monte Carlo, simply repeating $k$ times reduces error from $\epsilon$ to $\epsilon^k$ (much faster). For two-sided errors (as above), majority voting only halves the error per bit of confidence — the Chernoff bound is the correct tool. **This distinction is a frequent KTU exam trap.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Mandated Comparison

| Dimension | **Las Vegas** | **Monte Carlo** |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Output Correctness** | Always correct | Correct with prob. $\geq 1-\epsilon$ |
| **Running Time** | Random variable $T(n)$ | Deterministic / strictly bounded |
| **Expected Time** | $E[T(n)]$ polynomial | Worst-case $T(n)$ polynomial |
| **Error Type** | None (output is exact) | One-sided or two-sided |
| **Verification** | Implicit (output is the proof) | Requires certificate for amplification |
| **Worst-Case Guarantee** | $T(n)$ may be unbounded (rare) | Strictly bounded |
| **Typical Use Case** | Sorting, hashing, routing | Primality testing, polynomial identity, min-cut |
| **Exemplar Algorithm** | Randomized Quicksort | Freivalds' Matrix Multiplication |
| **Conversion Direction** | Truncate to get Monte Carlo | Add verification to get Las Vegas |
| **Probability Tool** | Indicator variables, $E[T] = c/p$ | Chernoff, Chebyshev, Markov bounds |
| **Confidence Boosting** | Re-run if unbounded time | Independent re-runs + majority vote |
